topics.title: Introduction, Evolution, Relay vs PLC vs Computer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction, Evolution, Relay vs PLC vs Computer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Industrial Automation?

**Industrial Automation** is the use of control systems (such as computers, controllers, or robots) and information technologies to handle different industrial processes and machinery, replacing human intervention with automated decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEICT635 - Module 1):**
> Industrial Automation is the engineering discipline that deals with the design, implementation, and monitoring of *closed-loop control systems* using electromechanical, electronic, and computational devices to perform tasks autonomously with minimal or no human assistance.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Home" Analogy

Imagine your morning routine:

- **Old Mechanical Era**: You manually press switches, open valves, and turn knobs for every appliance (just like a hard-wired relay panel).
- **Relay Era**: You install timers that turn the geyser ON at 6 AM and OFF at 6:30 AM (fixed-sequence electromechanical control).
- **PLC Era**: A single "smart brain" (the PLC) reads sensors like a thermostat or motion detector, decides the action, and triggers outputs — but only for one home or one factory line.
- **Computer / DCS Era**: A central "smart supervisor" (computer or DCS) coordinates *many* smart brains (PLCs) across the entire apartment complex, sharing data, optimizing energy, and alerting you on your phone.

This is exactly how industrial automation has evolved — from **local hard-wired logic** to **distributed intelligent networks**.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of PLC

> [!NOTE]
> **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** — As per **NEMA ICS3-240** and adopted by KTU syllabus:
> A solid-state, microprocessor-based control device that uses programmable memory to store instructions and to implement specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations in order to control machines and industrial processes.

### Key Properties of a PLC (KTU Board-Standard Terminology)

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Ruggedized** | Built to withstand **vibration (up to 2 g)**, dust, moisture, electrical noise, and temperature extremes (**0 °C to 60 °C** typical industrial range). |
| **Ladder Logic Native** | Originally programmed in **Ladder Diagram (LD)** language to be readable by electricians familiar with relay schematics. |
| **Deterministic Scan** | Guaranteed I/O update cycle time, typically **1–20 ms** for mid-range PLCs. |
| **Fail-Safe I/O** | Galvanic isolation between field devices and internal logic; redundant power supplies in critical applications. |

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition of DCS

> [!NOTE]
> **DCS (Distributed Control System)** — KTU/ISA Standard Definition:
> A control architecture in which multiple controllers (PLCs, RTUs, or dedicated controllers) are geographically distributed across a plant, each handling a section of the process, all networked to a central supervisory station for unified monitoring, data historian, and advanced control.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra"

A **PLC** is like a **single musician** who plays one instrument perfectly.
A **DCS** is like a **conductor** coordinating dozens of musicians, each playing different sections, all synchronized through a common sheet of music (the supervisory network).

---

## 1.4 Relay-Based Control — The Predecessor

A **Relay** is an *electromechanical switch* operated by an electromagnet coil. When the coil is energized, contacts move to open/close circuits. Relay logic implements Boolean control using **Normally Open (NO)** and **Normally Closed (NC)** contacts wired in series/parallel — this is called **hard-wired logic**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Board Exams:**
> The moment a relay's wiring is changed, *the entire control logic physically changes*. With a PLC, the wiring stays the same and only the *program* changes. This is the **fundamental paradigm shift** of the modern controller.

---

## 1.5 Computer-Based Control — The Modern Era

A general-purpose **Industrial PC (IPC)** or **Embedded Controller** running SCADA/HMI software can also execute control algorithms. However, it differs fundamentally from a PLC in **determinism, ruggedness, and reliability**. We will formalize this in SECTION 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Industrial Control Systems

The evolution can be tracked across **five distinct generations**, each solving a fundamental limitation of its predecessor:

### Generation 1 — Mechanical / Cam Timers (Pre-1960s)
- Pure mechanical linkages, rotating cams, and clockwork mechanisms.
- **Limitation**: Zero flexibility; each new product required new mechanical hardware.

### Generation 2 — Electromagnetic Relays (1960s)
- Hard-wired relay panels implementing Boolean logic.
- **Limitation**: Thousands of wires, high failure rate, troubleshooting nightmare, no diagnostics.

### Generation 3 — Solid-State Electronics & Logic Gates (Late 1960s)
- Discrete transistors, TTL/CMOS gates replaced relays for logic functions.
- **Limitation**: Still hard-wired PCB; changes required board rework.

### Generation 4 — PLC — Birth in 1968
- **Dick Morley** at Bedford Associates (Modicon) invented the **Modicon 084**, the first commercial PLC, for **General Motors Hydra-Matic Division**.
- Purpose: Replace unreliable relay panels in automotive assembly lines.
- **Revolution**: Software replaced wires; ladder logic matched electrician's mental model.

### Generation 5 — DCS / Computer-Based Networks (1975 onwards)
- **Honeywell TDC 2000** (1975) — first true DCS.
- **Microprocessor revolution** enabled distributed intelligence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact (Repeated in Examinations):**
> The PLC was invented in **1968** by **Dick Morley**. The first DCS was the **Honeywell TDC 2000** introduced in **1975**. These two years are frequently asked in **Part A (3-mark)** questions.

---

## 2.2 Operational Architecture of a Relay System

A relay control panel implements combinational logic using the **electromagnetic coil–contact mechanism**:

$$ V_{coil} = I_{coil} \cdot R_{coil} $$

Where:
- $V_{coil}$ = Coil energizing voltage (typically **24 V DC** or **230 V AC**).
- $I_{coil}$ = Coil current draw.
- $R_{coil}$ = Coil DC resistance.

The Boolean logic is **physically encoded** in copper wiring. For example, the classic **AND logic** in relay form requires two NO contacts in *series*:

$$ Y = A \cdot B $$

Implemented as: **A (NO contact)** → **B (NO contact)** → **Coil Y**. Both A and B must close to energize Y.

---

## 2.3 Operational Architecture of a PLC

A PLC operates on the **Cyclic Scan Principle**:

$$ T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping} $$

Where:
- $T_{scan}$ = Total scan cycle time (typically **5–20 ms**).
- $T_{input}$ = Time to read all physical inputs into the **Process Image Input (PII)** table.
- $T_{program}$ = Time to execute user logic sequentially.
- $T_{output}$ = Time to write the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table to physical outputs.
- $T_{housekeeping}$ = Time for diagnostics, communication, and OS overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Determinism Guarantee**: Unlike a Windows/Linux PC (which is *interrupt-driven and non-deterministic*), a PLC scan is **deterministic** — the maximum scan time is bounded and predictable. This is the single most important reason PLCs dominate safety-critical and real-time applications.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the highest-yield content for KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 questions. Memorize the symbols and typical numerical ranges.**

### Comparison: Relay vs PLC vs Computer

| Parameter | Relay Panel | PLC | Industrial Computer (PC/IPC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Logic Storage** | Physical wiring | Program in non-volatile memory | Program on HDD/SSD (volatile RAM at runtime) |
| **Logic Modification** | Re-wire (hours, downtime) | Edit program (minutes, no downtime) | Edit program (minutes) |
| **Operating Environment** | Moderate | **Industrial: 0–60 °C, dust, vibration, EMI** | Climate-controlled cabinet |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | ~10,000 hrs | **100,000–500,000 hrs** | 30,000–60,000 hrs |
| **Determinism** | Fully deterministic (electromechanical) | **Fully deterministic (cyclic scan)** | Non-deterministic (OS interrupts) |
| **I/O Density per Rack** | 8–16 contacts/relay | **32–256 I/O per rack** | Limited (via I/O cards) |
| **Cost per I/O Point** | High (₹800–1500/point) | **Low (₹200–500/point) at scale** | Medium |
| **Troubleshooting** | Manual continuity check | **Built-in diagnostics, online monitoring** | Software-only |
| **Real-Time Response** | ~10–20 ms (mechanical) | **1–10 ms (scan-time bound)** | 1–50 ms (OS jitter) |
| **Expandability** | Add more relays (wiring grows) | Add I/O modules & racks | Limited by slots |
| **Communication** | None | **EtherNet/IP, Profinet, Modbus, OPC UA** | TCP/IP, OPC UA |
| **Failure Mode** | Coil burnout, contact welding | Watchdog timeout, fail-safe I/O | OS crash, hard-drive failure |
| **Programming Language** | Wiring diagram | **Ladder (LD), FBD, ST, SFC, IL (IEC 61131-3)** | C/C++, Python, SCADA scripts |
| **Skill Required** | Electrician | Automation engineer | Software/IT engineer |

### Key Engineering Constants & Units (Bolded for KTU Board Exams)

- **PLC Scan Time:** **1 ms to 50 ms** typical (high-end: <1 ms).
- **MTBF of industrial PLC:** **≥ 100,000 hours** (~11 years continuous).
- **Operating Temperature:** **0 °C to 60 °C** (extended: −40 °C to +75 °C).
- **Coil Voltage (standard relay):** **24 V DC** or **230 V AC** at **50 Hz**.
- **First PLC invented in:** **1968** by **Dick Morley** (Modicon 084).
- **First DCS introduced in:** **1975** (Honeywell TDC 2000).
- **IEC 61131-3 Programming Languages:** **LD, FBD, ST, SFC, IL** (5 languages).

### Real-World Utility

This comparison is foundational in **any interview** and **any KTU ESE exam**. Choosing between a relay, PLC, and computer depends on:

- **Process complexity** (logic gates vs control loops).
- **Need for data historian & SCADA** (favours DCS).
- **Safety integrity level (SIL)** (favours PLC + SIL-rated I/O).
- **Capital vs operational cost** over 10-year lifecycle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Motor Start-Stop Control

This is the **canonical KTU problem** to convert hard-wired relay logic into PLC ladder logic. Solve it carefully.

### Problem Statement

Design both a **relay logic circuit** and an **equivalent PLC ladder program** for a 3-phase induction motor with the following requirements:

- **PB1** (Normally Open push button) — START
- **PB2** (Normally Closed push button) — STOP
- **OL** (Normally Closed overload contact) — Protection
- The motor must **latch** (self-hold) after START is released.
- The motor must **stop** if STOP or Overload is activated.
- Indicator lamp **L** glows whenever the motor runs.

---

### Step 1 — Translate to Boolean Expression

Define Boolean variables:

- $S$ = START push button (PB1, NO)
- $T$ = STOP push button (PB2, NC) → in logic, $T = 1$ when *not pressed*
- $O$ = Overload contact (NC) → $O = 1$ when *healthy*
- $M$ = Motor contactor coil
- $L$ = Indicator lamp

**Latching condition:**

$$ M_{next} = (S + M_{current}) \cdot T \cdot O $$

Where:
- "$+$" denotes **OR** (parallel paths for START or self-hold).
- "$\cdot$" denotes **AND** (series — all conditions must hold).

**Lamp condition:**

$$ L = M_{current} $$

---

### Step 2 — Derive the Relay Wiring Logic

In relay hardware, "OR" is implemented by **parallel contacts** and "AND" by **series contacts**.

- **OR** $(S + M)$ → PB1 (NO) **in parallel with** auxiliary contact of M (NO).
- **AND** with $T \cdot O$ → STOP (NC) **in series with** Overload (NC).

$$\boxed{\text{Rung} = \text{PB1 (NO)} \parallel M_{aux} \text{(NO)} \text{ --- STOP (NC)} \text{ --- OL (NC)} \text{ --- } M_{coil}}$$

For the lamp:

$$\boxed{\text{Lamp L} = M_{aux} \text{(NO contact)} \text{ --- } L_{coil}}$$

---

### Step 3 — Convert to PLC Ladder Diagram (LD)

The PLC ladder has **two rungs**:

```
Rung 1 (Motor Control):
|  PB1     M_aux     STOP     OL          |
|--| |-----| |------| |-----|/|----------( M )--|
|    Start   Hold      Stop   Overload    Motor |

Rung 2 (Lamp):
|  M_aux                |
|--| |-----------------( L )--|
```

**Symbols:**

- `| |` = Normally Open contact
- `|/|` = Normally Closed contact
- `( )` = Output coil

---

### Step 4 — Mathematical Verification of Latching

**Case 1: Initial Press of START**
- $S = 1$, $T = 1$, $O = 1$, $M_{current} = 0$
- $M_{next} = (1 + 0) \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 1$ → Motor starts.

**Case 2: START released (latch check)**
- $S = 0$, $T = 1$, $O = 1$, $M_{current} = 1$ (just set)
- $M_{next} = (0 + 1) \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 1$ → Motor **continues** (latched via $M_{aux}$).

**Case 3: STOP pressed**
- $S = 0$, $T = 0$, $O = 1$, $M_{current} = 1$
- $M_{next} = (0 + 1) \cdot 0 \cdot 1 = 0$ → Motor **stops**.

**Case 4: Overload trips**
- $S = 0$, $T = 1$, $O = 0$, $M_{current} = 1$
- $M_{next} = (0 + 1) \cdot 1 \cdot 0 = 0$ → Motor **stops**.

$$\therefore \text{ Boolean equation is verified across all four states.}$$

---

### Step 3.5 — Scan-Time Impact Analysis

If the scan time is $T_{scan} = 10 \text{ ms}$, the maximum motor stop latency is:

$$ t_{stop,max} = T_{scan} + t_{contact\_release} $$

$$ t_{stop,max} = 10 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ ms} = 18 \text{ ms} $$

Where $t_{contact\_release} \approx 8 \text{ ms}$ is the mechanical dropout time of a standard contactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **For safety-critical stops (E-stop), KTU expects students to mention that PLC scan time must be < 50% of the safety response budget.** If the safety requirement is $t_{stop,max} < 100 \text{ ms}$, a 10 ms PLC scan comfortably meets it.

---

### Step 3.6 — Transition Matrix: Hard-Wired Logic → PLC Program

| Hard-Wired Relay Element | PLC Equivalent |
|---|---|
| NO contact of PB1 | `PB1` (Boolean variable, `| |` contact) |
| NC contact of PB2 | `STOP` (Boolean variable, `|/|` contact) |
| Motor contactor coil | Output coil `M` (address e.g., Q0.0) |
| Auxiliary NO contact of M | Internal relay bit `M_aux` (address e.g., M0.0) |
| Latching action | Sealed/holding contact via `M_aux` |
| Indicator lamp | Output coil `L` (address e.g., Q0.1) |

This transition is the **most frequently asked 7-mark sub-question** in Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evolution Flowchart — Generations of Industrial Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gen 1: Mechanical Cam Timers\nPre-1960s\nNo flexibility] --> B[Gen 2: Electromagnetic Relays\n1960s\nHard-wired logic]
    B --> C[Gen 3: Solid-State Electronics\nLate 1960s\nTTL / CMOS gates]
    C --> D[Gen 4: PLC\n1968 by Dick Morley\nModicon 084]
    D --> E[Gen 5: DCS / Computer Networks\n1975 Honeywell TDC 2000\nDistributed Intelligence]
    E --> F[Modern Era: Industry 4.0\nIIoT, Edge, Cloud]

    style A fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#FFE680,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#B3E5B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#A0D8F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#C7B3F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Relay Panel vs PLC vs Computer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RELAY[Relay Panel System]
        R1[Sensors / Push Buttons] --> R2[Hard-Wired\nRelay Logic]
        R2 --> R3[Contactors\n& Actuators]
    end

    subgraph PLC[PLC System]
        P1[Field Sensors\nInputs] --> P2[CPU\nCyclic Scan\nLadder Logic]
        P2 --> P3[Output Modules\nIsolation Relays]
    end

    subgraph PC[Industrial Computer PC System]
        C1[Field I/O Cards] --> C2[CPU\nNon-Deterministic OS]
        C2 --> C3[SCADA / HMI\nSoftware]
    end

    R3 -. replaced by .-> P3
    P3 -. supplemented by .-> C3

    style RELAY fill:#FFE5CC,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px
    style PLC fill:#D4F4D4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style PC fill:#D4E6F4,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 PLC Internal Architecture (CPU Scan Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power ON]) --> Init[Initialization\nSelf-Diagnostics\nLoad OS]
    Init --> ReadInput[Read All Physical Inputs\ninto Process Image Input Table]
    ReadInput --> ExecLogic[Execute User Program\nLadder Logic Top-to-Bottom]
    ExecLogic --> WriteOutput[Write Process Image Output Table\nto Physical Output Modules]
    WriteOutput --> Housekeep[Housekeeping\nCommunication, Diagnostics]
    Housekeep --> Watchdog{Watchdog\nTimeout OK?}
    Watchdog -- Yes --> ReadInput
    Watchdog -- No --> Fault[Fault State\nOutputs OFF\nAlarm Triggered]

    style Start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style Fault fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    style Watchdog fill:#FFE680,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Positioning — Where Each Technology Fits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Low[Low Complexity]
        L1[Simple On/Off\nHome Appliances]
    end
    subgraph Mid[Mid Complexity]
        M1[Relay Panel\nManufacturing Lines]
        M2[PLC\nDiscrete & Process Control]
    end
    subgraph High[High Complexity]
        H1[DCS\nRefineries, Power Plants]
        H2[Industrial PC SCADA\nLarge Plants + IT Integration]
    end

    L1 --> M1 --> M2 --> H1 --> H2
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Q1: Process\nComplexity?} -->|Simple| QR1[Relay or Smart Relay]
    Q1 -->|Moderate| Q2{Q2: Need for\nSafety SIL?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| QP1[PLC with Safety I/O\nSIL 2/3 certified]
    Q2 -->|No| QP2[Standard PLC]
    Q1 -->|High Distributed| QD1[DCS or PLC + SCADA]

    style Q1 fill:#FFE680,stroke:#996600
    style Q2 fill:#FFE680,stroke:#996600
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. Define a PLC. List any FOUR characteristics that distinguish it from a conventional relay panel. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, microprocessor-based industrial control device that uses programmable, non-volatile memory to store user-defined instructions for implementing logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations to control industrial machines and processes.

> **Four distinguishing characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Programmability**: Logic is stored in software (ladder program), not in physical wiring. Modifications are made by editing the program, not re-wiring.
> 2. **Rugged Industrial Design**: Operates reliably in **0–60 °C**, with high immunity to vibration, dust, moisture, and electromagnetic noise (EMI/RFI).
> 3. **Deterministic Cyclic Scan**: Guaranteed bounded scan time (typically 1–20 ms), unlike a PC's OS-driven non-deterministic execution.
> 4. **Modular & Expandable I/O**: I/O modules (DI, DO, AI, AO) can be added without redesigning the controller; communication via **EtherNet/IP, Profinet, Modbus, OPC UA**.

---

### Q2. State the year of invention of the PLC and name the inventor. Mention the first commercial model. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Year of invention:** **1968** (1 mark)
- **Inventor:** **Dick Morley** at Bedford Associates (1 mark)
- **First commercial model:** **Modicon 084**, built for the **General Motors Hydra-Matic Division** to replace hard-wired relay panels on automotive transmission assembly lines (1 mark).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Relay vs PLC Comparative Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Compare relay-based control, PLC-based control, and computer-based control systems based on any **SEVEN** key parameters. Present your answer in a **tabular format**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Relay Panel | PLC | Computer (PC/IPC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Logic Implementation** | Hard-wired contacts & coils | Software program (Ladder, FBD) | Program on OS (C, Python, SCADA) |
| **2. Modification Time** | Hours to days (downtime needed) | Minutes (online editing possible) | Minutes |
| **3. Reliability (MTBF)** | ~10,000 hrs (contact wear) | **100,000–500,000 hrs** | 30,000–60,000 hrs |
| **4. Determinism** | Deterministic | **Cyclic scan, deterministic** | Non-deterministic (OS jitter) |
| **5. Operating Environment** | Moderate | **Industrial: 0–60 °C, EMI-tolerant** | Climate-controlled room |
| **6. Diagnostics** | Manual multimeter check | **Built-in online monitoring, status LEDs** | Software logs only |
| **7. Cost per I/O Point** | High (₹800–1500) | **Low (₹200–500) at scale** | Medium |

*(1 mark per parameter × 7 = 7 marks)*

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **cyclic scan operation** of a PLC. Why is determinism important in industrial control? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Cyclic Scan Stages (4 marks):**

The PLC executes a continuous loop called the **scan cycle**, comprising four stages:

1. **Input Scan** — The CPU reads the status of all physical input modules (limit switches, sensors, push buttons) and stores the result in the **Process Image Input (PII)** table in RAM. *(1 mark)*

2. **Program Scan** — The CPU executes the user program (ladder logic) sequentially from top to bottom, left to right. It uses the PII values (not the live inputs) for evaluation, ensuring consistent logic. *(1 mark)*

3. **Output Scan** — After program execution, the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table is transferred to the physical output modules (contactors, solenoids, lamps). *(1 mark)*

4. **Housekeeping** — Communication with HMI/SCADA, diagnostics, watchdog reset, and housekeeping tasks are performed before the next scan begins. *(1 mark)*

**Cycle Time Equation:**

$$ T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping} $$

Typical $T_{scan}$ ranges from **1 ms to 20 ms**.

**Why Determinism is Important (3 marks):**

- **Predictable response time:** A safety E-stop must guarantee that the motor stops within a defined time budget (e.g., <100 ms). A PLC guarantees this because the worst-case scan time is bounded.
- **Reproducibility:** Every scan produces the same result for the same input — essential for **SIL-rated safety loops** and **batch process validation**.
- **Contrast with PC OS:** Windows/Linux uses preemptive multitasking, interrupts, and page faults that cause *jitter* (variable delay). A real-time PLC OS avoids this by using a single deterministic scan loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):**
> 1. Students often *omit* the housekeeping stage — KTU board deducts **1 mark**.
> 2. Many confuse "scan time" with "program execution time" — only the latter is the logic run; scan time includes all four stages.
> 3. Failing to mention the **watchdog timer** loses 0.5 mark. The watchdog resets the PLC if the scan exceeds a maximum threshold, forcing outputs to a safe state.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Evolution & Worked Relay-to-PLC Conversion `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Briefly describe the **evolution of industrial control systems** from mechanical relays to modern DCS, highlighting at least **FIVE generations** with one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Generation | Technology | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **1 (Pre-1960)** | **Mechanical cam timers, linkages** (1 mark) | Zero flexibility; any change required new hardware (1 mark) |
| **2 (1960s)** | **Electromagnetic Relays** (1 mark) | Thousands of wires; contact wear; difficult troubleshooting (1 mark) |
| **3 (Late 1960s)** | **Solid-State Logic (TTL/CMOS)** (1 mark) | Still hard-wired on PCB; changes required board rework (1 mark) |
| **4 (1968)** | **PLC** — Dick Morley, Modicon 084 (1 mark) | Higher initial cost; requires trained programmer (0.5 mark) |
| **5 (1975 onwards)** | **DCS** — Honeywell TDC 2000 (1 mark) | Complex network configuration; vendor lock-in (0.5 mark) |

*(7 marks distributed across all rows)*

---

**(b)** Design the **hard-wired relay logic** and the **equivalent PLC ladder program** for a motor START–STOP control with overload protection and self-holding (latching). Clearly state the Boolean equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Boolean Equation (2 marks):**

$$ M_{next} = (S + M_{current}) \cdot T \cdot O $$

Where $S$ = START (NO), $T$ = STOP (NC), $O$ = Overload (NC), $M$ = Motor coil.

**Relay Wiring Diagram (2 marks):**

Two rungs as shown in Section 3.3 above:
- Rung 1: `PB1(NO) ∥ M_aux(NO) — STOP(NC) — OL(NC) — M_coil`
- Rung 2: `M_aux(NO) — L_coil`

**PLC Ladder Equivalent (3 marks):**

- Address map: `I0.0 = PB1`, `I0.1 = STOP`, `I0.2 = OL`, `Q0.0 = Motor`, `Q0.1 = Lamp`, `M0.0 = M_aux`.
- Rung 1: `| I0.0 | — | M0.0 | — | I0.1 (NC) | — | I0.2 (NC) | — ( Q0.0 )` with `| M0.0 |` sealed in parallel with `I0.0`.
- Rung 2: `| Q0.0 | — ( Q0.1 )` (Lamp follows motor).

**Verification of latching behaviour** — walk through four cases (as in Section 3.3) to confirm motor holds after START release and drops on STOP/OL activation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> 1. **Using `M0.0` instead of `Q0.0` for the auxiliary contact** — common mistake. The seal-in contact must reference the *internal holding bit* OR the *output bit* depending on PLC convention; Siemens uses `Q0.0` while Allen-Bradley uses `B3:0/0`. For KTU, explicitly mention both.
> 2. **Forgetting to negate STOP and OL** — they are NC, so they appear as `|/|` (NC contact) in ladder. Writing them as NO loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not showing two rungs** — students often combine motor and lamp into one rung; KTU expects separate rungs.
> 4. **Skipping the Boolean equation** — losing 2 marks free of cost.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Introduction & Evolution)**

- ☐ **PLC Definition**: Solid-state, microprocessor-based, programmable memory, implements logic/sequencing/timing/counting/arithmetics.
- ☐ **PLC Invented**: **1968**, **Dick Morley**, **Modicon 084**, for **GM Hydra-Matic**.
- ☐ **First DCS**: **Honeywell TDC 2000, 1975**.
- ☐ **Five Generations**: Mechanical → Relays → Solid-State → PLC → DCS.
- ☐ **Cyclic Scan Equation**: $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$.
- ☐ **Typical Scan Time**: **1–20 ms** (high-end <1 ms).
- ☐ **PLC MTBF**: **≥ 100,000 hours**.
- ☐ **Operating Temp**: **0–60 °C** (industrial).
- ☐ **Standard Coil Voltages**: **24 V DC** or **230 V AC, 50 Hz**.
- ☐ **IEC 61131-3 Languages**: LD, FBD, ST, SFC, IL.
- ☐ **Latching Equation**: $M_{next} = (S + M_{current}) \cdot T \cdot O$.
- ☐ **Determinism**: PLC scan is *bounded* and *predictable*; PC OS is *interrupt-driven* and *jittery*.
- ☐ **Hard-wired logic → PLC**: Same physical wiring; only the *program* changes.
- ☐ **Always mention** watchdog timer, galvanic isolation, and Process Image tables in scan-cycle answers.
- ☐ **Common KTU keywords**: *deterministic, ruggedized, modular, IEC 61131-3, ladder diagram, process image, cyclic scan, watchdog.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Module 1:**
> 1. Confusing **PLC scan time** with **program execution time**.
> 2. Writing STOP and Overload as NO instead of NC contacts in ladder diagrams.
> 3. Forgetting the **housekeeping stage** of the scan cycle.
> 4. Not providing the **Boolean equation** before drawing the ladder.
> 5. Citing wrong inventor/year — always write **Dick Morley, 1968, Modicon 084**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Hardware and Internal Architecture, Input-Output Devices, Basics of Ladder Programming
Internal Relay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internal Relay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Internal Relay** (also known as an **Auxiliary Relay**, **Marker Relay**, **Internal Coil**, or **Memory Bit**) in a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) is a *software-implemented, virtual electromagnetic relay* that resides entirely within the PLC's central processing unit (CPU) memory map. Unlike physical output relays that drive field actuators, an internal relay has **no physical Input/Output (I/O) terminals**, no mechanical contacts, and no external wiring points. It exists solely as a single **binary memory bit** (logical 0 or logical 1) inside the PLC's Random Access Memory (RAM) that mimics the energised/de-energised behaviour of a hardware relay.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for PLC and DCS, the internal relay is formally defined as:

> *"A discrete memory element within the PLC used to store intermediate logical states of a control program, allowing complex Boolean operations, sequencing, latching, and interlocking functions without consuming any physical I/O points."*

The address of an internal relay varies across manufacturers, but universally follows the pattern:

| Manufacturer | Internal Relay Address Notation |
| :--- | :--- |
| Allen-Bradley (Rockwell) | `B3:0/0` (Binary file, word 0, bit 0) |
| Siemens (S7-1200/1500) | `M0.0` (Merker/Memory bit) |
| Mitsubishi (MELSEC) | `M0` |
| Schneider (Modicon) | `%M0` |
| ABB (AC500) | `%MX0.0` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Internal relays are explicitly categorised under *soft elements* of the PLC. They are critical for implementing complex ladder logic without expanding the physical I/O hardware module.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are conducting a large orchestra. The musicians (physical outputs like motors, valves, lamps) are scattered across the stage, each holding an instrument. The musical score on your desk (the ladder logic program) is the master plan. Now, imagine you need to **remember small intermediate notes to yourself** while reading the score — for example, "the flute section has already played its intro" or "we are now in the second movement." These are not sounds played aloud; they are **private mental bookmarks**.

**An Internal Relay is exactly this mental bookmark.**

- It is **not** an instrument (not a physical output).
- It is **not** a microphone (not a physical input).
- It is a **secret note you write to yourself inside your own brain** to remember a state that will be used later in the score.

In electrical terms, it is a **"relay without terminals"** — a coil that you can energise in software, and contacts (normally open, normally closed) that you can read back from software. The current flows only inside the PLC's silicon brain, never reaching the outside world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** If you can write the coil symbol and read the contact symbol of a relay in your ladder program, but you cannot trace a wire from it to any physical field device — it is almost certainly an internal relay.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Bit Size:** Each internal relay occupies exactly **1 bit** of PLC memory.
- **Typical Memory Capacity:** Modern PLCs allocate between **2048 to 32768 internal relays** depending on CPU model (e.g., Siemens S7-1200 offers 8192 bytes of Merker memory = 65,536 bits).
- **Scan Time Impact:** Each internal relay contact used in a rung adds approximately **0.1 µs to 2 µs** to the program scan time, depending on PLC processor speed.
- **Retention Property:** Internal relays may be **Volatile** (state lost on power failure) or **Retentive** (state preserved through power cycles via backup battery/capacitor or non-volatile memory). Standard KTU laboratory PLCs (e.g., Siemens LOGO!, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1400) typically allocate the lower 50% of markers as volatile and the upper 50% as retentive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture: The 'Why' and 'How'

The internal relay operates on the principle of **memory-mapped I/O**. The PLC's CPU reserves a contiguous block of its RAM exclusively for these virtual relays. The operational flow can be broken down into five logical stages:

**Stage 1 — Address Resolution**
During program compilation, the programming software (e.g., TIA Portal, RSLogix 500) translates the symbolic address `M0.0` or `B3:0/0` into a **physical memory address** (e.g., RAM byte 100, bit 0). This translation is stored in the system memory's address cross-reference table.

**Stage 2 — Input Scan (Read Phase)**
At the beginning of every PLC scan cycle, the CPU reads all physical input states into the **Process Input Image (PII)** table. Internal relays are *not* read here — they are read from their dedicated memory block within the **Bit Memory (Merkers)** region.

**Stage 3 — Program Execution (Logic Solve Phase)**
The CPU executes the ladder logic rung-by-rung from top to bottom, left to right. When it encounters an internal relay coil instruction (e.g., `-( )-` or `-(M)-`), it writes the result of the preceding Boolean logic to the corresponding memory bit. When it encounters a contact instruction referencing that relay (`-|||-` or `-|\|-`), it reads the current bit value without modification.

**Stage 4 — Output Write Phase**
At the end of the scan cycle, the CPU transfers the **Process Output Image (POI)** to the physical output modules. **Internal relays produce no output write** — they only update internal memory.

**Stage 5 — Housekeeping and Diagnostics**
The CPU performs self-diagnostics, communication servicing, and updates the internal relay memory for any retentive regions to backup storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Distinction:** An internal relay coil instruction in ladder logic appears identical to a physical output coil. The *only* difference is the **address prefix** (`M` for internal vs. `Q` for physical output in Siemens, or `B3` for internal vs. `O:0` for physical output in Allen-Bradley).

### 2.2 Key Functional Categories of Internal Relays

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly identifies four functional categories:

1.  **General-Purpose Internal Relays:** Used for general Boolean logic, intermediate flag storage, and rung-to-rung communication.
2.  **Latching (Retentive) Internal Relays:** Maintain their state through power cycles. Used for machine mode memory (e.g., "auto mode selected before power failure").
3.  **One-Shot (Pulse) Internal Relays:** Generate a single-scan pulse (one PLC cycle wide) on the rising or falling edge of a signal. Used for event capture and counters.
4.  **Special Function Internal Relays (System Relays):** Predefined by the manufacturer for diagnostic purposes (e.g., First Scan bit `S:1/15` in Allen-Bradley, `FirstScan` system bit in Siemens).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarises the key mathematical and logical formulations governing internal relay behaviour:

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Memory Footprint | $\text{Memory} = N_{\text{relays}} \times 1 \text{ bit}$ | $N$ = number of relays in use |
| Boolean Output (OR logic) | $M_{\text{out}} = I_1 \vee I_2 \vee \ldots \vee I_n$ | Logical OR of $n$ inputs |
| Boolean Output (AND logic) | $M_{\text{out}} = I_1 \wedge I_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge I_n$ | Logical AND of $n$ inputs |
| Latch Equation (Set-Dominant) | $M_{t+1} = (M_t \wedge \overline{R}) \vee S$ | $S$ = Set input, $R$ = Reset input |
| Toggle Equation (Flip-Flop) | $M_{t+1} = \overline{M_t}$ | State alternates every scan |
| Scan Time Penalty | $T_{\text{scan}}^{\text{new}} = T_{\text{scan}}^{\text{old}} + N_{\text{contacts}} \times \tau_{\text{inst}}$ | $\tau_{\text{inst}}$ ≈ 0.1–2 µs per contact |
| Retentive Memory Fraction | $R_{\text{fraction}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{ret}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Typically $R_{\text{fraction}} \in [0, 1]$ |
| Edge Detection (Rising) | $M_{\text{pulse}} = \overline{M_{\text{prev}}} \wedge M_{\text{now}}$ | True for exactly one scan |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** When using the **Latch Equation**, students frequently confuse set-dominant vs. reset-dominant latches. In a set-dominant latch, if both $S$ and $R$ are true simultaneously, the output remains SET. The Boolean expression is $M_{t+1} = S \vee (M_t \wedge \overline{R})$, *not* $M_{t+1} = (S \wedge \overline{R}) \vee M_t$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Internal relays are the **silent workhorses** of industrial automation. Their engineering utility spans:

- **Sequential Control:** Tracking which step of a multi-step process is currently active (e.g., washing machine cycles: fill → wash → drain → spin).
- **Safety Interlocking:** Preventing two contradictory actions from occurring simultaneously (e.g., a motor cannot run forward and reverse at the same time).
- **Mode Selection Memory:** Remembering whether the operator selected "Manual" or "Automatic" mode after a brief power interruption.
- **Event Counting Flags:** Holding intermediate overflow states for cascaded counters.
- **Diagnostic Annunciation:** Triggering HMI alarms when fault conditions are detected, without consuming physical outputs.
- **Scan-Cycle Synchronisation:** Using one-shot relays to ensure an action executes only once per event, regardless of how long a button is held.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Latch (Seal-In) Circuit Using Internal Relays

The most common application of internal relays is implementing a **latching circuit**. We will derive the Boolean equation step-by-step from first principles.

**Problem Statement:**
Design a ladder logic rung using an internal relay `M0.0` that latches ON when a momentary pushbutton `I0.0` (Start) is pressed, and resets only when `I0.1` (Stop) is pressed. Both buttons are normally open (NO).

**Step 1 — Identify Inputs and the Memory Element**
- Input 1: `I0.0` — Start pushbutton (NO contact)
- Input 2: `I0.1` — Stop pushbutton (NO contact, but we will use NC contact in series for fail-safe logic)
- Output (Memory): `M0.0` — Latching internal relay

**Step 2 — Construct the Ladder Rung Logic**
The rung has two parallel branches feeding the internal relay coil `M0.0`:
- Branch A: `I0.0` (Start button, NO)
- Branch B: `M0.0` (Seal-in contact, NO — the "memory" branch)

In series, before both branches, we place `I0.1` as a **normally closed (NC) contact** (fail-safe — if the wire breaks, the system stops).

**Step 3 — Translate Rung to Boolean Equation**

$$\text{Rung Condition} = \overline{I_{0.1}} \wedge (I_{0.0} \vee M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}})$$

where $M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}}$ denotes the previous scan cycle's state of the internal relay.

**Step 4 — Apply the Memory Update Rule**
At the end of each scan, the coil writes the new state back to memory:

$$M_{0.0}^{\text{next}} = \overline{I_{0.1}} \wedge (I_{0.0} \vee M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}})$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Steady-State Behaviour**

*Case A — System is OFF, Start is pressed:*
Assume $M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}} = 0$ and $I_{0.0} = 1$, $I_{0.1} = 0$.

$$M_{0.0}^{\text{next}} = \overline{0} \wedge (1 \vee 0) = 1 \wedge 1 = 1$$

The relay energises. ✓

*Case B — System is ON, Start is released:*
Now $M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}} = 1$ (latched), $I_{0.0} = 0$, $I_{0.1} = 0$.

$$M_{0.0}^{\text{next}} = \overline{0} \wedge (0 \vee 1) = 1 \wedge 1 = 1$$

The relay *remains* energised because of the seal-in contact. ✓

*Case C — System is ON, Stop is pressed:*
$M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}} = 1$, $I_{0.0} = 0$, $I_{0.1} = 1$.

$$M_{0.0}^{\text{next}} = \overline{1} \wedge (0 \vee 1) = 0 \wedge 1 = 0$$

The relay de-energises. ✓

**Step 6 — Verify Reset Dominance**
After reset, even if Start is still held: $I_{0.0} = 1$, $I_{0.1} = 1$.

$$M_{0.0}^{\text{next}} = \overline{1} \wedge (1 \vee 0) = 0 \wedge 1 = 0$$

The Stop (NC) contact **dominates** the logic. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Edge Detection (One-Shot) Using Internal Relays

**Problem Statement:**
Generate a one-scan-wide pulse when input `I0.2` transitions from OFF to ON (rising edge).

**Step 1 — Rung Construction**
We require two internal relays: `M0.1` (the edge detector flag) and `M0.2` (the output pulse).

- **Rung 1:** `I0.2` → `M0.1` (latched — tracks the current input state)
- **Rung 2:** `M0.1` AND `NOT M0.2_prev` → `M0.2` (pulse output)
- **Rung 3:** `M0.1` → `M0.2` (latch backup for next scan)

**Step 2 — Boolean Equation**

$$M_{0.2}^{\text{next}} = M_{0.1}^{\text{next}} \wedge \overline{M_{0.2}^{\text{prev}}}$$

**Step 3 — Truth Table Verification**

| Scan # | $I_{0.2}$ | $M_{0.1}$ (previous) | $M_{0.2}$ (previous) | $M_{0.2}$ (next) | Interpretation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $0 \wedge \overline{0} = 0$ | Idle |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $1 \wedge \overline{0} = \mathbf{1}$ | **Rising Edge Detected** |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | $1 \wedge \overline{1} = 0$ | Pulse Ends |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $1 \wedge \overline{0} = 1$ | (Self-latch holds) |
| 5 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $0 \wedge \overline{1} = 0$ | Falling — no pulse |

**Step 4 — Refined One-Shot (Standard Industry Form)**
To avoid multi-cycle behaviour, the cleaner industry-standard equation uses an XOR gate:

$$M_{0.2} = I_{0.2} \oplus M_{0.1}^{\text{prev}}$$

where $M_{0.1}^{\text{prev}}$ is the previous scan's $I_{0.2}$ state.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Internal Relay vs. Physical Output Relay

| Parameter | Internal Relay | Physical Output Relay |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Physical Existence | None (software bit) | Electromechanical or solid-state |
| Wiring Required | No | Yes (terminal block, field device) |
| Response Time | ~1 scan cycle (µs) | 5–15 ms (mechanical) or 1 ms (SSR) |
| Lifetime | Unlimited (no wear) | 100,000–10,000,000 operations |
| Number Available | Hundreds to thousands | Limited by I/O module slots |
| Power Consumption | Negligible (memory cell) | 0.5–5 W per coil |
| Failure Mode | Bit corruption (rare) | Contact welding, coil burn-out |
| Diagnostic Visibility | Memory monitor only | LED indication + field feedback |
| Cost | Zero (included in CPU) | ₹200–₹2000 per point |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Internal Relay Memory Integration in PLC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputModule[Physical Input Module I0.0 to I0.7]
    inputImg[Process Input Image PII Memory]
    outputModule[Physical Output Module Q0.0 to Q0.7]
    outputImg[Process Output Image POI Memory]
    cpuBlock[CPU Execution Engine]
    irMem[Internal Relay Memory Block M0.0 to M255.7]
    irGP[General Purpose Markers M0.0 to M127.7]
    irRet[Retentive Markers M128.0 to M255.7]
    irPulse[Pulse and Edge Detectors]
    irSys[System Flags FirstScan AlwaysOn]
    programBlock[Ladder Logic Program Memory]
    scanCycle[Scan Cycle Controller]
    battery[Backup Battery Capacitor]

    scanCycle --> inputModule
    inputModule --> inputImg
    inputImg --> cpuBlock
    programBlock --> cpuBlock
    cpuBlock --> irMem
    irMem --> irGP
    irMem --> irRet
    irMem --> irPulse
    irMem --> irSys
    battery -.-> irRet
    cpuBlock --> outputImg
    outputImg --> outputModule
    outputModule -.No connection.-> irMem

    classDef memory fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef physical fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class inputModule,outputModule physical
    class irMem,irGP,irRet,irPulse,irSys,inputImg,outputImg memory
    class cpuBlock,programBlock,scanCycle control
```

### 4.2 Ladder Logic Schematic: Latching Internal Relay Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[DC Power Rail L1] --> N1[Stop NC I0.1]
    N1 --> P1{Parallel Branch Node}
    P1 --> B1[Start NO I0.0]
    P1 --> B2[Seal-in Contact M0.0 NO]
    B1 --> P2
    B2 --> P2
    P2 --> Coil1[M0.0 Internal Relay Coil]
    Coil1 --> N2[Neutral Rail]

    L2[DC Power Rail L2] --> N3[Condition I0.3]
    N3 --> Coil2[Q0.0 Physical Motor Output]
    Coil2 --> N4[Neutral Rail]

    classDef rail fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef contact fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef coil fill:#4caf50,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef parallel fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class L1,L2,N2,N4 rail
    class N1,B1,B2,N3 contact
    class Coil1,Coil2 coil
    class P1,P2 parallel
```

### 4.3 State Machine: Sequential Operation Using Internal Relays

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle: Step 0 Idle Marker M0.0
    Filling: Step 1 Filling Tank Marker M0.1
    Heating: Step 2 Heating Water Marker M0.2
    Washing: Step 3 Washing Cycle Marker M0.3
    Draining: Step 4 Drain Water Marker M0.4
    Spinning: Step 5 Spin Dry Marker M0.5
    Complete: Step 6 Cycle Done Marker M0.6

    Idle --> Filling : Start Button AND Level Low
    Filling --> Heating : Level High
    Heating --> Washing : Temperature Reached
    Washing --> Draining : Timer Done
    Draining --> Spinning : Level Low
    Spinning --> Complete : Timer Done
    Complete --> Idle : Reset Button

    note right of Filling : Only one M bit is true at any time\nMutual exclusivity is enforced in logic
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagrams:** The block diagram clearly shows that the **Internal Relay Memory Block** sits between the CPU and the physical I/O. The dashed line from `outputModule` to `irMem` carries the explicit label **"No connection"**, visually reinforcing that internal relays never physically drive field devices. The state diagram demonstrates the classic use of internal relays as **sequence step markers**, where exactly one marker is true at any moment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an internal relay in a PLC. How does it differ from a physical output relay?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **internal relay** is a software-implemented virtual relay that resides entirely within the PLC's CPU memory as a single binary bit, used for storing intermediate logical states within the control program.

**Differences from a physical output relay:**

| Aspect | Internal Relay | Physical Output Relay |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Existence | Memory bit only | Hardware electromagnetic/SSR |
| External wiring | Not required | Required to field device |
| Physical terminals | None | Present (common, NO, NC) |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differentiation with correct technical comparison)*

---

**Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four applications of internal relays in PLC programming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1.  **Latching circuits** — Maintaining the ON state of an output after a momentary start signal is removed.
2.  **Sequential control** — Tracking the active step in a multi-step process (e.g., batch mixing sequence).
3.  **Interlocking** — Preventing simultaneous activation of conflicting outputs (e.g., motor forward/reverse interlock).
4.  **One-shot pulse generation** — Producing a single-scan-wide pulse on the rising edge of an input signal.

*(Acceptable alternatives: event counting flags, mode memory, fault annunciation, scan synchronisation)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of internal relays in a PLC with a neat diagram. Describe their role in implementing a latching (seal-in) circuit with a suitable Boolean equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLC uses 512 internal relays in a sequential control program. If each contact adds 1.2 µs to the scan time, and the ladder program uses an average of 3 internal relay contacts per rung across 80 rungs, calculate the total scan time overhead due to internal relay contacts. If the total acceptable scan time is 20 ms, comment on whether the design is acceptable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part Solution (7 marks):**

Internal relays are memory-mapped virtual relays within the PLC's CPU. They are addressed using manufacturer-specific notation (e.g., `M0.0` in Siemens, `B3:0/0` in Allen-Bradley).

**Role in Latching Circuit:**
A latching circuit uses an internal relay's NO contact in parallel with the start input to maintain the energised state after the start button is released. The circuit comprises:
- A **Stop button** (NC contact) in series with the rung.
- A **Start button** (NO contact) in parallel with the internal relay's own NO seal-in contact.
- The internal relay **coil** at the output end.

The Boolean equation derived in Section 3.1 is:

$$M_{0.0}^{\text{next}} = \overline{I_{0.1}} \wedge (I_{0.0} \vee M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}})$$

This equation demonstrates that the relay energises when Start is pressed OR it was already on from the previous scan, AND the Stop button is not pressed. *(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for explanation, 2 marks for Boolean equation with correct variables)*

**(b) Part Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Number of rungs = 80
- Average internal relay contacts per rung = 3
- Time per contact $\tau_{\text{inst}}$ = 1.2 µs

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Internal Relay Contacts**

$$N_{\text{contacts}} = N_{\text{rungs}} \times C_{\text{avg}}$$

$$N_{\text{contacts}} = 80 \times 3 = 240 \text{ contacts}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Scan Time Overhead**

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = N_{\text{contacts}} \times \tau_{\text{inst}}$$

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = 240 \times 1.2 \text{ µs}$$

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = 288 \text{ µs}$$

Converting to milliseconds:

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = 0.288 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Acceptable Scan Time**

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = 0.288 \text{ ms} \ll T_{\text{acceptable}} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

$$\text{Percentage Overhead} = \frac{0.288}{20} \times 100\% = 1.44\%$$

**Conclusion:** The scan time overhead due to internal relay contacts is only **1.44%** of the acceptable scan time. The design is **fully acceptable** with substantial margin. The internal relays contribute negligibly to scan time compared to physical I/O processing and communication overhead.

*(2 marks for step 1, 2 marks for step 2 with units, 2 marks for comparison and conclusion, 1 mark for percentage calculation)*

---

**Question B:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of a ladder logic diagram, explain how internal relays are used to implement (i) a latching circuit and (ii) a one-shot rising-edge detector. Provide the relevant Boolean equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLC program uses an internal relay `M0.0` with the following Boolean logic: $M_{0.0} = (I_1 \wedge \overline{I_2}) \vee (M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}} \wedge \overline{I_2})$. Construct the truth table for all four input combinations and determine the memory behaviour of `M0.0`. Identify the dominant input. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Latching Circuit:** As derived in Section 3.1, the latch uses a Start button (NO) in parallel with a seal-in contact of `M0.0`, in series with a Stop button (NC):

$$M_{0.0}^{\text{next}} = \overline{I_{\text{Stop}}} \wedge (I_{\text{Start}} \vee M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}})$$

**(ii) One-Shot Rising Edge Detector:** As derived in Section 3.2, using two internal relays `M0.1` (input tracker) and `M0.2` (pulse output):

$$M_{0.2} = I_{0.2} \oplus M_{0.1}^{\text{prev}}$$

The XOR operation yields 1 only when the input changes state, producing a one-scan pulse.

*(3 marks for latch explanation with diagram and equation, 3 marks for one-shot explanation with diagram and equation, 1 mark for correct identification of XOR property)*

**(b) Part Solution (7 marks):**

Given Boolean equation:

$$M_{0.0}^{\text{next}} = (I_1 \wedge \overline{I_2}) \vee (M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}} \wedge \overline{I_2})$$

**Step 1 — Truth Table Construction**

| Row | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_1 \wedge \overline{I_2}$ | $M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}} \wedge \overline{I_2}$ | $M_{0.0}^{\text{next}}$ | Memory Behaviour |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | $0 \wedge 1 = 0$ | $0 \wedge 1 = 0$ | **0** | Stays OFF (initial state) |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | $1 \wedge 1 = 1$ | $0 \wedge 1 = 0$ | **1** | SET — first trigger |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | $1 \wedge 1 = 1$ | $1 \wedge 1 = 1$ | **1** | Stays SET (latched) |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | $0 \wedge 1 = 0$ | $1 \wedge 1 = 1$ | **1** | Stays SET (memory retained) |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | $1 \wedge 0 = 0$ | $1 \wedge 0 = 0$ | **0** | RESET — $I_2$ dominates |
| 6 | 0 | 1 | $0 \wedge 0 = 0$ | $0 \wedge 0 = 0$ | **0** | Stays OFF |

**Step 2 — Dominant Input Identification**

Examining the equation: the term $\overline{I_2}$ appears in **both** conjuncts. When $I_2 = 1$, both terms evaluate to 0 regardless of $I_1$ or $M_{0.0}^{\text{prev}}$.

**Therefore, $I_2$ is the RESET-DOMINANT input.** It overrides all other conditions and forces `M0.0` to OFF. This is analogous to a **Stop/Emergency input** in industrial control.

**Step 3 — Functional Interpretation**
- $I_1$ acts as the **Set/Start** input.
- $I_2$ acts as the **Reset/Stop** input.
- The circuit functions as a **reset-dominant SR latch** implemented using an internal relay.

*(3 marks for complete truth table with all six valid rows, 2 marks for dominant input identification with justification, 2 marks for functional interpretation as SR latch)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing coil addresses:** Students frequently write ladder logic using `Q0.0` (physical output) instead of `M0.0` (internal relay) when the question explicitly asks for internal relay implementation. This loses **2–3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the overline on Stop NC:** When drawing a fail-safe Stop button, the NC contact symbol must be explicitly drawn as `-\|\|-` (with the slash), not `-\|\|-` in the wrong orientation. Examiners check this carefully.
> 3. **Boolean equation variable confusion:** Writing $M^{\text{next}}$ when the question asks for $M^{\text{prev}}$ (or vice versa) in the seal-in equation loses **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 4. **Unit conversion errors:** Scan time problems frequently require conversion between µs and ms. Forgetting to divide by 1000 is a recurring **1-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Ignoring retentive vs. volatile distinction:** When answering questions about power failure behaviour, students must explicitly state whether the internal relay is in a retentive or volatile memory region.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** An internal relay is a *software-only, memory-resident virtual relay* with no physical terminals.
- **Address:** Manufacturer-specific (`M`, `B`, `%M`); occupies exactly **1 bit** of RAM.
- **Primary Functions:** Latching, sequencing, interlocking, edge detection, mode memory.
- **No I/O:** Internal relays do **not** consume physical I/O points and do **not** drive field devices.
- **Scan Behaviour:** Written once per scan (at the coil), read unlimited times via contacts; no contact bounce.
- **Retention:** Lower markers are typically **volatile**; upper markers are **retentive** (battery-backed).
- **One-Shot Pulse:** Produces a single-scan-wide pulse via XOR edge detection or differential contact logic.
- **Latch Equation (Set-Dominant):** $M_{t+1} = S \vee (M_t \wedge \overline{R})$ — Stop ($R$) is dominant.
- **Latch Equation (Reset-Dominant):** $M_{t+1} = \overline{R} \wedge (S \vee M_t)$ — Stop overrides Start.
- **Memory Footprint:** 1 bit per relay; negligible scan time contribution (~1.44% in typical designs).
- **Difference from Physical Relay:** No wiring, no terminals, no failure mode, unlimited lifetime, zero incremental cost.
- **Edge Detection:** $M_{\text{pulse}} = I_{\text{now}} \oplus I_{\text{prev}}$ for rising-edge single-scan pulse generation.
- **Interlocking Use:** Two outputs $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ can be mutually exclusive via $M = Q_1 \wedge \overline{Q_2}$.
- **Mode Memory:** Use **retentive** markers for mode selection persistence across power cycles.
- **Diagnostic Advantage:** All internal relay states are visible in online monitoring without affecting field wiring.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jump Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Jump Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)**, **Jump Instructions** are a class of program flow control instructions that override the normal sequential (top-to-bottom, left-to-right) execution order of the PLC scan cycle. They conditionally or unconditionally transfer program execution from the current rung to a predefined **Label (LBL)** elsewhere in the program, thereby skipping intermediate rungs.

According to the **IEC 61131-3** standard adopted by KTU (Allen-Bradley / Siemens S7 convention), the standard jump instruction set includes:

- **JMP (Jump)** – Transfers execution to a label.
- **LBL (Label)** – Target destination marker.
- **JMPE / JEND (Jump End)** – Marks the end of a jumped segment (used in some platforms like Siemens).
- **MC / MCR (Master Control / Master Control Reset)** – A related *zone-control* instruction pair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** Jump instructions are *not* the same as Subroutine Calls. A **jump** permanently bypasses logic; a **subroutine** executes a block and *returns* to the calling point. This distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark question in KTU ESE.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your PLC program as a **recipe book** with numbered steps (rungs):

| Cooking Analogy | PLC Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Reading the recipe from top to bottom | Normal scan cycle execution |
| **"Skip to Step 7"** written beside Step 3 | **Jump Instruction (JMP)** |
| The little circle labeled "Step 7" | **Label (LBL)** |
| Crossing out Steps 4–6 with a pen | **JMPE / JEND (Jump End)** marker |
| A *side recipe* that you cook, then return to the main recipe | **Subroutine Call (JSR/SBR)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Analogy:** In a bottling plant, if the **Emergency Stop** is *not* pressed, the PLC executes filling, capping, and labelling in sequence. If a sensor detects "Bottle Not Present" (`X1` = OFF), a **conditional JMP** can skip the *filling* rung and jump directly to the *capping* rung. This avoids spurious outputs on dry cycles.

---

### 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Typical Jump Instruction Execution Time:** 0.5 µs to 2.5 µs (controller-dependent, e.g., Allen-Bradley CompactLogix ≈ 0.06 µs).
- **Maximum Number of Nested Jumps Allowed:** **8 levels** (IEC 61131-3 baseline).
- **Scan Cycle:** Repeated every **T_scan ≈ 10 ms – 50 ms** depending on program length and I/O count.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbolic Notation Standard:** When referring to jump targets, KTU notation uses **`LBL 10`**, **`JMP 10`**, where **10** is an integer label identifier (range typically 000–999).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Jump Instructions

Jump instructions are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis A — Trigger Condition:**

1. **Unconditional Jump (JMP / JU)**
   - Always executes the jump whenever the rung is reached.
   - No input condition is required (rung is always TRUE during scan).
   - Used for static program partitioning (e.g., modes of operation).

2. **Conditional Jump (JMP / JC)**
   - Jump occurs *only* if the preceding input logic is TRUE.
   - If the input is FALSE, the scan falls through to the next rung normally.

**Axis B — Scope / Pairing:**

1. **Standalone Jump (JMP → LBL)** — Allen-Bradley convention; just a target label, no explicit end marker.
2. **Paired Jump (JMP … JEND)** — Siemens S7 convention; requires explicit end marker.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown)

**Step 1 — Scan Cycle Initiation**
The CPU begins a new scan: reads inputs into the **Process Image Input (PII)**.

**Step 2 — Sequential Execution Starts**
The PLC executes rungs from the **first** rung downward.

**Step 3 — Encountering a JMP Rung**
- The PLC evaluates the rung condition.
- If `TRUE` and the instruction is conditional → the program counter is **immediately reloaded** with the address of the target **LBL**.
- If `FALSE` (conditional) → the next sequential rung is executed.

**Step 4 — Target LBL Encountered**
Execution resumes at the rung containing the matching label.

**Step 5 — Skipped Rungs**
**Crucially**, any rungs between the JMP and LBL are **not executed during that scan**. Their outputs:
- Retain their **last computed value** (latched), OR
- Are forced OFF (controller and retentive-flag dependent).

**Step 6 — Scan Cycle Completion**
After reaching the END rung, the CPU updates the **Process Image Output (PIO)** and the cycle repeats.

---

### 2.3 Master Control Instructions (MC / MCR) — Companion Concept

Master Control is a *zone-disable* mechanism often taught alongside JMP:

- **MC (Master Control):** Disables all rungs within its zone when its input condition is FALSE.
- **MCR (Master Control Reset):** Marks the end of the MC zone.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** With **JMP**, outputs in the skipped zone *retain their last state*. With **MC/MCR**, outputs in the zone are *forced OFF* (non-retentive outputs only).

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The "formula" for jump instructions is essentially a **scan time optimization equation** and a **conditional truth table**.

**Table 2.A — Scan Time Equation**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output}
$$

$$
T_{program} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{exec}} t_i
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{scan}$ | Total scan cycle time | **10–50 ms** |
| $T_{input}$ | Input image update time | 1–3 ms |
| $T_{program}$ | Program execution time | 1–20 ms |
| $T_{output}$ | Output image update time | 1–3 ms |
| $N_{exec}$ | Number of instructions *actually executed* per scan | Variable |
| $t_i$ | Execution time of instruction $i$ | **0.06 µs – 5 µs** |

> ⚠️ Use `\vert` (vertical bar with different math spacing) when writing absolute value in plain text.

**Table 2.B — Jump Instruction Truth Table (Conditional JMP)**

| Input Condition $I$ | JMP Type | Action | Output State in Skipped Zone |
|---|---|---|---|
| FALSE | Conditional | Fall-through (no jump) | Normal execution |
| TRUE | Conditional | Jump to LBL | Last state latched |
| X (don't care) | Unconditional | Always jump | Last state latched |
| FALSE | MC | Zone disabled | Outputs OFF (non-retentive) |
| TRUE | MC | Zone enabled | Normal execution |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Mode Switching:** Jumping between *Manual* and *Automatic* modes without executing both simultaneously.
- **Safety Zones:** Bypassing non-essential rungs during fault conditions to reduce scan time.
- **Recipe Selection:** Different products on a packaging line can use different program segments selected by JMP.
- **Scan Time Optimization:** Industrial PLCs with 10,000+ rungs use JMP to skip inactive zones, reducing $T_{program}$ by up to **40%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Scan Time Reduction Using JMP

**Problem Statement:** A PLC program has **N = 1000** rungs. Each rung requires an average execution time of **$t_{avg}$ = 2 µs**. A conditional JMP skips **N_skip = 300** rungs when condition C is TRUE (60% of the time). Calculate the **percentage reduction in $T_{program}$**.

---

**Given:**
$$
N = 1000, \quad N_{skip} = 300, \quad t_{avg} = 2\ \mu s, \quad P(C = TRUE) = 0.6
$$

**Step 1 — Compute baseline program execution time (no JMP)**

$$
T_{prog,\ base} = N \cdot t_{avg}
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ base} = 1000 \cdot 2\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ base} = 2000\ \mu s = 2\ ms
$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective execution time when JMP is TRUE**

$$
T_{prog,\ TRUE} = (N - N_{skip}) \cdot t_{avg}
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ TRUE} = (1000 - 300) \cdot 2\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ TRUE} = 700 \cdot 2\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ TRUE} = 1400\ \mu s = 1.4\ ms
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective execution time when JMP is FALSE**

$$
T_{prog,\ FALSE} = N \cdot t_{avg} = 2000\ \mu s = 2\ ms
$$

**Step 4 — Compute average program execution time**

$$
T_{prog,\ avg} = P(C=TRUE) \cdot T_{prog,\ TRUE} + P(C=FALSE) \cdot T_{prog,\ FALSE}
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ avg} = (0.6 \cdot 1.4\ ms) + (0.4 \cdot 2\ ms)
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ avg} = 0.84\ ms + 0.80\ ms
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ avg} = 1.64\ ms
$$

**Step 5 — Compute percentage reduction**

$$
\%\ Reduction = \frac{T_{prog,\ base} - T_{prog,\ avg}}{T_{prog,\ base}} \cdot 100
$$

$$
\%\ Reduction = \frac{2.00\ ms - 1.64\ ms}{2.00\ ms} \cdot 100
$$

$$
\%\ Reduction = \frac{0.36}{2.00} \cdot 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%\ Reduction = 18\%}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: JMP vs Subroutine vs MC/MCR

The following table provides the definitive **comparison matrix** that KTU examiners expect:

| Feature | Jump (JMP/LBL) | Subroutine (JSR/SBR/RET) | Master Control (MC/MCR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Execution Pattern** | Forward skip | Branch + Return | Zone enable/disable |
| **Returns to Caller?** | No | Yes | No (linear flow) |
| **Skipped Rung Outputs** | Last state retained | N/A | Forced OFF (non-retentive) |
| **Nesting Limit** | 8 levels | 8 levels | 1 level typically |
| **Typical Use** | Mode selection | Reusable logic | Safety overrides |
| **Scan Time Impact** | Reduces $T_{prog}$ | Slight overhead | Reduces $T_{prog}$ |
| **Ladder Symbol** | JMP, LBL | JSR, SBR, RET | MC, MCR |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Ladder Logic with Conditional JMP

**Scenario:** A conveyor has two modes:
- **Mode 1 (Selector M1 = ON):** Execute Rungs 1–5 (Loading).
- **Mode 2 (Selector M2 = ON):** Execute Rungs 6–10 (Unloading).

**Ladder Representation:**

$$
\text{Rung 1: } \boxed{\ \vert\,M1\,\vert\ \ } \longrightarrow \text{JMP 5 (skip to LBL 5)}
$$

$$
\text{Rung 2: } \boxed{\ \vert\,Start\,\vert\ \ } \longrightarrow \text{(Loading Motor ON)}
$$

$$
\text{Rung 3: } \boxed{\ \vert\,Sensor1\,\vert\ \ } \longrightarrow \text{(Actuator A1)}
$$

$$
\text{Rung 4: } \text{...(Loading logic)...}
$$

$$
\text{Rung 5 (LBL): } \text{LBL 5}
$$

$$
\text{Rung 6: } \boxed{\ \vert\,M2\,\vert\ \ } \longrightarrow \text{JMP END (skip to END)}
$$

$$
\text{Rung 7: } \boxed{\ \vert\,Start\,\vert\ \ } \longrightarrow \text{(Unloading Motor ON)}
$$

**Execution Trace:**

If $M1 = ON$, $M2 = OFF$ → Rung 1 jumps to LBL 5 → Rungs 2–4 are **skipped** → Unloading logic is also **skipped** because $M2 = OFF$ does not trigger its JMP.

If $M1 = OFF$, $M2 = ON$ → Rung 1 is FALSE → falls through → Rungs 2–4 execute → Rung 6 triggers JMP END → Skips unloading.

This is a classic **mutually exclusive mode selection** pattern using JMP.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Conditional Jump Execution Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start Scan]) --> B[Read Inputs to PII]
    B --> C{Rung Contains JMP}
    C -->|No| D[Execute Rung Normally]
    C -->|Yes| E{Input Condition TRUE}
    E -->|Yes| F[Load LBL Address into PC]
    E -->|No| D
    F --> G[Skip Intermediate Rungs]
    G --> H[LBL Target Reached]
    H --> I[Resume Sequential Execution]
    D --> J{More Rungs}
    I --> J
    J -->|Yes| C
    J -->|No| K[Update PIO Outputs]
    K --> L([End Scan - Repeat])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — PLC Scan Cycle with Jump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCAN_CYCLE["PLC SCAN CYCLE"]
        A1[Input Scan Module] --> A2[Program Execution Engine]
        A2 --> A3{JMP Encountered}
        A3 -->|Jump Active| A4[Skip Zone]
        A3 -->|No Jump| A5[Normal Execution]
        A4 --> A6[Output Scan Module]
        A5 --> A6
    end
    A1 -.PII.-> A2
    A2 -.PC Update.-> A3
    A6 -.PIO.-> A1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Block — JMP vs Subroutine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JMP_BLOCK["JUMP INSTRUCTION FLOW"]
        J1[Rung N: JMP 50] --> J2[Rung N+1 SKIPPED]
        J2 --> J3[Rung N+2 SKIPPED]
        J3 --> J4[LBL 50 Resumes]
    end
    subgraph SBR_BLOCK["SUBROUTINE FLOW"]
        S1[Main: JSR SubA] --> S2[SubA SBR]
        S2 --> S3[SubA Body]
        S3 --> S4[RET Return]
        S4 --> S5[Main Resumes]
    end
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Machine — Mode Selection via JMP

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Mode1: M1 TRUE
    Idle --> Mode2: M2 TRUE
    Mode1 --> Loading: Execute Rungs 2-4
    Mode1 --> Idle: M1 FALSE
    Mode2 --> Unloading: Execute Rungs 7-9
    Mode2 --> Idle: M2 FALSE
    Loading --> Mode1: JMP LBL5
    Unloading --> Mode2: JMP END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Notice in the JMP block that the flow **does not return** to the original rung after LBL execution — it simply resumes sequential flow from the LBL onward. This is the key visual difference from subroutine flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define a Jump Instruction in a PLC. How does it differ from a Subroutine Call?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Jump Instruction (JMP)** is a program flow control instruction that transfers execution from the current rung to a predefined **Label (LBL)** elsewhere in the PLC program when a specified condition is met. It causes the PLC to *skip* intermediate rungs during the current scan.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Jump (JMP) | Subroutine (JSR) |
|---|---|---|
| Return to caller | **No** — flow continues from LBL onwards | **Yes** — RET returns control |
| Purpose | Skip unwanted logic temporarily | Execute reusable code block |
| Scan time | Reduces program execution | Adds slight overhead |

Jump is used for **mode selection** and **scan optimization**, while Subroutine is used for **modular, reusable logic**. *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Q2. What is the function of a Master Control Reset (MCR) instruction? State one industrial application.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Master Control Reset (MCR)** instruction marks the end of a *Master Control zone*. Within an MC-MCR pair, when the MC input condition is **FALSE**, all non-retentive outputs in the zone are **forced OFF**, regardless of their logic state.

**Industrial Application:** In an automated assembly line, the **MC/MCR** zone can disable all conveyor motors when the **Emergency Stop** button is pressed, while still allowing the scan to continue and monitoring safety logic. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of PLC program execution with reference to the **scan cycle**. Derive the expression for total scan time and show how a Jump Instruction can reduce the effective $T_{program}$. Use a numerical example to demonstrate a **15% reduction**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Scan Cycle Architecture:**

The PLC executes a repetitive **three-phase cycle**:

$$
\text{Cycle} = \text{Input Scan} \rightarrow \text{Program Execution} \rightarrow \text{Output Scan}
$$

- **Input Scan:** Reads the physical input states into the **Process Image Input (PII)** memory.
- **Program Execution:** The CPU solves the ladder logic top-to-bottom, rung-by-rung.
- **Output Scan:** Writes the solved PIO states to the physical output modules.

**2. Total Scan Time Equation:**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output}
$$

$$
T_{program} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{exec}} t_i
$$

**3. Jump-Induced Reduction:**

When a conditional JMP is TRUE, the program counter jumps to LBL, bypassing $N_{skip}$ rungs. The effective execution becomes:

$$
T_{prog,\ TRUE} = (N - N_{skip}) \cdot t_{avg}
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ avg} = P(C=TRUE) \cdot T_{prog,\ TRUE} + P(C=FALSE) \cdot T_{prog,\ FALSE}
$$

**4. Numerical Example (15% reduction target):**

Let $N = 800$, $t_{avg} = 1.5\ \mu s$, $N_{skip} = ?$, $P(C=TRUE) = 0.5$.

$$
T_{prog,\ base} = 800 \cdot 1.5\ \mu s = 1200\ \mu s
$$

We want:

$$
\frac{T_{prog,\ base} - T_{prog,\ avg}}{T_{prog,\ base}} = 0.15
$$

$$
T_{prog,\ avg} = 0.85 \cdot 1200\ \mu s = 1020\ \mu s
$$

Setting up the average:

$$
0.5 \cdot (800 - N_{skip}) \cdot 1.5 + 0.5 \cdot 1200 = 1020
$$

$$
0.75 \cdot (800 - N_{skip}) + 600 = 1020
$$

$$
0.75 \cdot (800 - N_{skip}) = 420
$$

$$
800 - N_{skip} = 560
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{skip} = 240\ \text{rungs}}
$$

So, jumping over **240 rungs** when condition is TRUE (with 50% probability) yields a **15% reduction** in $T_{program}$. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b)** With the help of a ladder logic diagram, explain how **JMP and LBL instructions** are used to implement a **two-mode (Manual / Automatic) operation** of an industrial process. Discuss the role of Master Control (MC/MCR) as an alternative. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Two-Mode Operation Using JMP/LBL:**

The selector switch `MODE_SEL` chooses between Manual (Rungs 1–5) and Automatic (Rungs 6–10). Using JMP:

- **Rung 1:** `MODE_SEL_AUTO` → **JMP 6** (skip Manual logic, go to Auto).
- **Rungs 2–5:** Manual control rungs (Push-button Start/Stop).
- **Rung 6 (LBL 6):** Label for Auto mode start.
- **Rungs 7–10:** Automatic sequencing (timers, sensors).
- **Rung 11:** `MODE_SEL_MAN` → **JMP END** (skip Auto logic).

**2. Ladder Logic Sketch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Rung 1: } \vert\,MODE\_SEL\_AUTO\,\vert \longrightarrow JMP\ 6 \\
&\text{Rung 2-5: Manual push-button logic (executed only if MODE not Auto)} \\
&\text{Rung 6: } LBL\ 6 \\
&\text{Rung 7-10: Automatic sensor/timer logic} \\
&\text{Rung 11: } \vert\,MODE\_SEL\_MAN\,\vert \longrightarrow JMP\ END \\
&\text{Rung END: } END
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. MC/MCR Alternative:**

The same functionality can be implemented using:

- **Rung 1:** `MODE_SEL_AUTO` → **MC** (begin zone).
- **Rungs 2–5:** Inside MC zone — Auto logic.
- **Rung 6:** **MCR** (end zone).
- **Rungs 7–10:** Manual logic.

**4. Key Differences:**

- **JMP** retains output states of skipped rungs.
- **MC/MCR** forces outputs OFF in the disabled zone (useful for safety).
- **JMP** can nest up to 8 levels; **MC/MCR** is typically flat (1 level).

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Jump Instructions, Subroutine Calls, and Master Control Instructions** in PLC programming. Use a comparison table and justify which is most suitable for each of the following scenarios: **(7 marks)**

1. Reusable calculation block called from multiple rungs.
2. Bypassing heater control when product is absent.
3. Emergency stop disabling all actuators except alarms.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Jump (JMP) | Subroutine (JSR/SBR) | Master Control (MC/MCR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Returns? | No | Yes | No |
| Effect on outputs | Retain state | N/A | Force OFF |
| Nesting | 8 levels | 8 levels | 1 level |
| Purpose | Mode skip | Code reuse | Zone disable |

**Scenario Justifications:**

1. **Reusable calculation block:** Use **Subroutine (JSR/SBR)** because it can be called from multiple locations and *returns* to the caller — perfect for repeated computations like PID or scaling. *(2 marks)*

2. **Bypassing heater control when product absent:** Use **Conditional Jump (JMP)**. When `Product_Present = FALSE`, jump over the heater rung. Outputs (e.g., heater contactor) retain their last state — useful for maintaining temperature with hysteresis. *(2 marks)*

3. **Emergency stop disabling all actuators except alarms:** Use **MC/MCR** zone. When `E_STOP = TRUE`, MC disables all actuator outputs (forced OFF), while alarms placed *outside* the MC zone continue to operate. This ensures fail-safe behavior. *(3 marks)*

---

**(b)** A PLC controls a packaging machine with **5 operational modes** selected by a 3-bit selector input (`S2 S1 S0`). Each mode corresponds to a different packaging size (100g, 200g, 500g, 1kg, 5kg) and requires a distinct set of rungs. Design a **JMP-based ladder program** with appropriate Labels and explain the logic flow. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Mode Selection Logic:**

A 3-to-5 decoder maps binary input to one of 5 JMP targets.

**2. Ladder Logic Design:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Rung 1: } \vert\,S2\,\vert\ \vert\,S1\,\vert\ \vert\,S0\,\vert = 000 \rightarrow JMP\ LBL100 \\
&\text{Rung 2: } \vert\,S2\,\vert\ \vert\,S1\,\vert\ \vert\,\overline{S0}\,\vert = 001 \rightarrow JMP\ LBL200 \\
&\text{Rung 3: } \vert\,S2\,\vert\ \vert\,\overline{S1}\,\vert\ \vert\,S0\,\vert = 010 \rightarrow JMP\ LBL500 \\
&\text{Rung 4: } \vert\,S2\,\vert\ \vert\,\overline{S1}\,\vert\ \vert\,\overline{S0}\,\vert = 011 \rightarrow JMP\ LBL1K \\
&\text{Rung 5: } \vert\,\overline{S2}\,\vert \rightarrow JMP\ LBL5K
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Label Targets:**

- **LBL 100:** Rungs for 100g packaging (small filler, 2-second timer).
- **LBL 200:** Rungs for 200g packaging.
- **LBL 500:** Rungs for 500g packaging.
- **LBL 1K:** Rungs for 1kg packaging (medium filler, 10-second timer).
- **LBL 5K:** Rungs for 5kg packaging (large auger, 30-second timer).

**4. Logic Flow Explanation:**

When the operator selects mode `000` (100g), Rung 1 evaluates TRUE and the program jumps to `LBL100`, skipping Rungs 6–50 (used for other sizes). Only the 100g-specific rungs execute, dramatically reducing scan time.

Each mode is **mutually exclusive** — exactly one JMP fires per scan — making the design deterministic and safe.

The END of each segment can use **JMP END** (Siemens) or rely on sequential flow into the next LBL/END (Allen-Bradley).

*(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing JMP with Subroutine:** Writing "JMP returns to the calling point" — this is FALSE and will cost **2 marks** outright. Jumps NEVER return.
> 2. **Forgetting that MC forces outputs OFF:** Students often write "MC skips the logic like JMP." Wrong — MC *disables* outputs by forcing them OFF. JMP *retains* outputs.
> 3. **Not specifying units in numerical derivations:** Always include µs / ms. A bare "1200" without units loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Wrong label syntax:** Writing `LBL: 10` instead of `LBL 10` (colon not allowed in IEC 61131-3).
