> 5. **Not drawing ladder diagrams:** For 7-mark questions on JMP/LBL, **ladder diagrams are mandatory**. A purely textual answer loses at least **2–3 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Jump Instructions**

- **JMP (Jump):** Transfers program flow to LBL when condition TRUE.
- **LBL (Label):** Target destination; numeric identifier (000–999).
- **JEND (Jump End):** Optional explicit end marker (Siemens convention).
- **Conditional vs Unconditional:** Conditional depends on input rung; unconditional always fires.
- **Skipped Output Behavior:** Retain last state (NOT forced OFF).
- **MC / MCR:** Disables zone; forces non-retentive outputs OFF; for safety overrides.
- **Subroutine (JSR/SBR/RET):** Executes reusable block and *returns*; opposite of JMP.
- **Nesting Limit:** **8 levels** (IEC 61131-3).
- **Scan Time Formula:** $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output}$.
- **Scan Time Reduction Equation:** $T_{prog, avg} = P \cdot T_{TRUE} + (1-P) \cdot T_{FALSE}$.
- **Application #1:** Mode selection (Manual/Auto).
- **Application #2:** Recipe selection (multi-product lines).
- **Application #3:** Scan time optimization (skip inactive zones).
- **NOT a Jump:** Branching to multiple parallel rungs simultaneously (use parallel branches, not JMP).
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** Typically 7–14 marks in Module 2.

> **Final Tip:** In the exam, always **draw the ladder diagram** before writing the explanation. A picture first, text second — this mirrors the KTU board examiner's valuation key and earns full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Handling Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Handling Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Data Handling Instructions in PLC

**Formal Definition:**
Data handling instructions in a PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) are a class of ladder logic/programming instructions used to manipulate, transfer, compare, and process data stored in memory registers, timers, counters, and input/output (I/O) locations. These instructions operate on **16-bit (word) or 32-bit (double word) data** rather than discrete single-bit signals, enabling the PLC to perform arithmetic, logical, comparison, and data movement operations essential for analog control, recipe management, and process automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Data handling instructions form a **core component of Module 2** in PEICT635. Mastery of MOV, comparison (CMP), mathematical (ADD/SUB/MUL/DIV), logical (AND/OR/XOR), shift (SHL/SHR), and conversion (BCD/BIN) instructions is mandatory for both continuous assessment and end-semester examinations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a PLC's memory as a **giant spreadsheet (like Microsoft Excel)**:

- **Data Registers (Words)** = Cells in the spreadsheet that store numbers (e.g., 0 to 65535 for 16-bit)
- **Discrete Bits** = Checkboxes (ON/OFF, 0 or 1)
- **Data Handling Instructions** = The formulas and functions you apply to these cells (e.g., `=SUM(A1:A10)`, `=A1+B1`)

**Real-world analogy:** Consider a **smart water tank controller**:
1. A water level sensor gives an analog reading (e.g., 4–20 mA → 0–100% level).
2. This analog value is stored in a memory word (e.g., `N7:0`).
3. The PLC uses **comparison instructions** to check: "Is level > 80%? If yes, start alarm."
4. It uses **mathematical instructions** to convert the raw value: `Percentage = (RawValue × 100) / 4095`.
5. It uses **move instructions** to transfer computed values to display registers or output words driving a VFD (Variable Frequency Drive).

Without data handling instructions, a PLC would be limited to simple ON/OFF logic — no analog control, no PID, no recipes, no HMI numeric displays.

### Categories of Data Handling Instructions

| Category | Purpose | Common Instructions |
|----------|---------|---------------------|
| **Data Move** | Transfer data between locations | MOV, MVM, BLKMV, CLR |
| **Comparison** | Compare two values | EQU, NEQ, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ, LIM |
| **Mathematical** | Arithmetic operations | ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, SQR, NEG, ABS |
| **Logical** | Bitwise operations | AND, OR, XOR, NOT |
| **Shift/Rotate** | Shift bits left/right | BSL, BSR, BSR/SL (Allen-Bradley) |
| **Conversion** | Change data format | BCD→BIN, BIN→BCD, INT→REAL, REAL→INT, TOD, FRD |
| **Table/Stack** | FIFO/LIFO operations | FFL/FFU, LFL/LFU, SQO/SQI |

### Standard PLC Memory Word Size

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Data Metrics (Sourced from IEC 61131-3):**
> - **1 Bit** = 0 or 1 (Boolean)
> - **1 Byte (8 bits)** = 0 to 255 (unsigned)
> - **1 Word (16 bits)** = 0 to 65,535 unsigned, or **-32,768 to +32,767** signed (signed integer/SINT range)
> - **1 Double Word (32 bits)** = -2,147,483,648 to +2,147,483,647 (signed)
> - **1 Real (IEEE 754 Float)** = 32-bit floating point

The word length determines the numerical range and is critical for avoiding **overflow errors** in PLC programming.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Data Move Instructions

The **Move (MOV)** instruction is the simplest and most fundamental data handling instruction. It copies the value from a **Source** address to a **Destination** address without altering the source.

**Operation Logic:**
1. Check the rung condition (preconditions / enable input).
2. When the rung is TRUE → read value at Source.
3. Write that exact value to Destination.
4. Source remains unchanged.

**Variants:**
- **MOV** — Move 16-bit word
- **MOVB** — Move 8-bit byte
- **MOVD** — Move 32-bit double word
- **MVM** — Masked Move (move only selected bits using a mask)
- **BLKMV** — Block Move (copy a block of N words)
- **CLR** — Clear (set to zero)

**Masked Move (MVM) Formula:**

$$D = (S \ \text{AND} \ M) \ \text{OR} \ (D \ \text{AND} \ \overline{M})$$

Where:
- $D$ = Destination word
- $S$ = Source word
- $M$ = Mask word
- $\overline{M}$ = Bitwise NOT of mask

This allows **selective bit manipulation** — only the bits where the mask is `1` are updated.

## 2.2 Comparison Instructions

Comparison instructions compare two values and produce a **Boolean TRUE/FALSE** output that drives a downstream rung.

| Instruction | Symbol | Logic | Output TRUE When |
|-------------|--------|-------|------------------|
| EQU | `=` | $A = B$ | Source A **equals** B |
| NEQ | `<>` | $A \neq B$ | A is **not equal to** B |
| GRT | `>` | $A > B$ | A is **greater than** B |
| GEQ | `>=` | $A \geq B$ | A is **greater than or equal to** B |
| LES | `<` | $A < B$ | A is **less than** B |
| LEQ | `<=` | $A \leq B$ | A is **less than or equal to** B |
| LIM | — | — | A is within lower/upper limit range |

**Step-by-step logic for a typical comparison rung:**

1. When input condition `I:0/0` is TRUE, enable the comparison block.
2. PLC fetches the value at Source A (e.g., `N7:0` containing current tank level).
3. PLC fetches the value at Source B (e.g., `N7:1` containing the setpoint = 80).
4. The comparison instruction evaluates the relational operator (e.g., GRT).
5. If `N7:0 > N7:1`, the instruction outputs a logical 1 (TRUE), energizing any downstream output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why comparisons matter in engineering:** They are the **building blocks of decision-making** in PLCs — alarm generation, sorting, batch control, and limit-checking all rely on comparison instructions.

## 2.3 Mathematical Instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Equation |
|-------------|-----------|----------|
| ADD | Addition | $R = A + B$ |
| SUB | Subtraction | $R = A - B$ |
| MUL | Multiplication | $R = A \times B$ |
| DIV | Division | $R = A \div B$ |
| MOD | Modulo | $R = A \ \text{mod} \ B$ |
| SQR | Square root | $R = \sqrt{A}$ |
| NEG | Negate (2's complement) | $R = -A$ |
| ABS | Absolute value | $R = \vert A \vert$ |
| SCP | Scale with Parameters | $R = ((A - \text{InMin}) \times (\text{OutMax} - \text{OutMin})) / (\text{InMax} - \text{InMin}) + \text{OutMin}$ |

## 2.4 Logical (Bitwise) Instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Bitwise Logic |
|-------------|-----------|---------------|
| AND | Logical AND | $1 \ \text{AND} \ 1 = 1$; else 0 |
| OR | Logical OR | $0 \ \text{OR} \ 0 = 0$; else 1 |
| XOR | Exclusive OR | Outputs 1 only if inputs differ |
| NOT | Bitwise complement | Flips every bit (0→1, 1→0) |

These operate on **each bit** of a 16-bit word independently. They are essential for:
- **Bit packing/unpacking** (combining multiple discrete flags into one word for HMI communication)
- **Selective clearing** of bits
- **Error code construction**

## 2.5 Conversion Instructions

| Instruction | Purpose |
|-------------|---------|
| BCD→BIN (FRD in AB) | Convert Binary Coded Decimal to Binary |
| BIN→BCD (TOD in AB) | Convert Binary to BCD |
| DVB/FLT | Integer to Float (32-bit real) |
| DEB/INT | Float to Integer (truncate/round) |
| RAD, DEG | Radians ↔ Degrees |

**Why conversions are critical:** HMI displays often expect BCD, analog inputs give raw binary, and PID loops use real (floating-point) values — a single program may require **all three formats**.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 16-bit unsigned range | $0$ to $2^{16} - 1$ | 0 to 65,535 | Timer/Counter presets |
| 16-bit signed range | $-2^{15}$ to $2^{15} - 1$ | -32,768 to 32,767 | Signed math operations |
| 2's complement (negation) | $\overline{A} + 1$ | — | NEG instruction |
| Masked move | $D = (S \ \& \ M) \ \vert \ (D \ \& \ \overline{M})$ | — | Selective bit update |
| Scale conversion (linear) | $Y = \frac{(X - X_{min}) \cdot (Y_{max} - Y_{min})}{(X_{max} - X_{min})} + Y_{min}$ | Engineering units | Analog input scaling |
| BCD to Binary | $D = \sum_{i=0}^{3} d_i \cdot 10^i$ | Decimal digits 0–9 | Thumbwheel → binary |
| Hysteresis check | Output TRUE if $X > (\text{Setpoint} + \Delta)$ or $X < (\text{Setpoint} - \Delta)$ | Engineering units | Anti-chatter comparator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Data handling instructions are the backbone of **SCADA**, **batch process control**, **recipe management**, **PID loops**, and **HMI communication**. Every modern automation project (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Schneider, Mitsubishi) relies on these instructions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Analog Input Scaling Formula

**Problem Statement:**
A pressure transmitter outputs a 4–20 mA signal, which an analog input module converts to a raw integer value of 0–4095 (12-bit ADC). We need to display the pressure in **bar**, where 4 mA = 0 bar and 20 mA = 10 bar. Derive the scaling equation.

### Step 1: Identify the input-output relationship
The relationship between raw integer value and engineering units is **linear**:

$$P = m \cdot X + c$$

Where:
- $P$ = Pressure in bar
- $X$ = Raw integer value from ADC
- $m$ = Slope (gain)
- $c$ = Y-intercept (offset)

### Step 2: Apply boundary conditions

At $X = 0$ (corresponds to 4 mA, low end):
$$P_{min} = m \cdot 0 + c \implies c = P_{min} = 0 \ \text{bar}$$

At $X = 4095$ (corresponds to 20 mA, high end):
$$P_{max} = m \cdot 4095 + c$$

### Step 3: Solve for the slope m

Since $c = 0$:

$$m = \frac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}}$$

$$m = \frac{10 - 0}{4095 - 0} = \frac{10}{4095}$$

### Step 4: Final general scaling equation

$$\boxed{P = \left( \frac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}} \right) \cdot (X - X_{min}) + P_{min}}$$

Substituting values:

$$P = \left( \frac{10}{4095} \right) \cdot X$$

$$P = 0.002442 \cdot X \quad \text{(bar)}$$

### Step 5: PLC implementation (ladder logic equivalent)

In an Allen-Bradley PLC, this is implemented as:

```
MUL    Source1: N7:0  (raw input)
       Source2: 2442
       Dest:    N7:10  (after integer scaling)
DIV    Source1: N7:10
       Source2: 1000
       Dest:    N7:11  (final pressure in bar × 1000)
```

Or using the **SCP (Scale with Parameters)** instruction directly:

$$N7:11 = \frac{(N7:0 - 0) \cdot (10000 - 0)}{4095 - 0} + 0$$

Here we store 10 bar as 10000 to preserve integer precision (1 decimal place).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: BCD to Binary Conversion

A 16-bit BCD word stores 4 decimal digits (each 4 bits, range 0–9 per nibble).

**Example:** BCD value `0100 0001 0010 0010` = 4122 decimal.

### Conversion formula:

$$\text{Decimal} = d_3 \cdot 1000 + d_2 \cdot 100 + d_1 \cdot 10 + d_0$$

For `4122`:

$$\text{Decimal} = 4 \cdot 1000 + 1 \cdot 100 + 2 \cdot 10 + 2 \cdot 1 = 4122$$

The PLC uses the **FRD (From BCD)** instruction to perform this conversion in a single rung, internally computing:

$$\text{Decimal} = (b_{15..12} \cdot 10^3) + (b_{11..8} \cdot 10^2) + (b_{7..4} \cdot 10^1) + (b_{3..0} \cdot 10^0)$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Data Handling Instruction

| Industrial Requirement | Recommended Instruction(s) | Justification |
|------------------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| Display raw ADC value on HMI | MOV | Simple copy from input to display register |
| Convert 0–10V to 0–100°C | SCP or MUL+DIV | Linear scaling requires arithmetic |
| Alarm when level > 90% | GRT comparison | Single decision based on threshold |
| Add two flow rates | ADD | Sum the two source words |
| Compute average of 10 readings | ADD + DIV | Sum then divide by count |
| Combine 4 discrete alarms into one word for HMI | BSL + OR or MVM | Bit packing |
| Cycle a conveyor (3-station rotary table) | BSL (Bit Shift Left) | Sequentially shift one bit through a word |
| Convert 0–4095 integer to volts | SCP | Linear scaling with offset |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Tank Level Control

**Problem:** A tank has an analog level sensor (0–10V → 0–100%). The PLC reads this as a raw value 0–4095. Implement the following logic using data handling instructions:

1. Convert raw value to percentage.
2. Start fill pump if level < 30%.
3. Start drain pump if level > 80%.
4. Trigger high alarm if level > 95%.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Scaling**

$$\text{Level\%} = \frac{\text{Raw} \times 100}{4095}$$

In ladder:
```
[XIC I:0/0] ---[SCP Raw: N7:0, InMin:0, InMax:4095, OutMin:0, OutMax:100, Dest: N7:1]---
```

**Step 2: Fill pump logic**

```
[GRT SourceA: 30, SourceB: N7:1] ---[OTE O:0/0]---   (Fill pump)
```
Wait — for **fill pump** to start when level is **LOW**, we need `N7:1 < 30`, i.e., `LES`:

```
[LES SourceA: N7:1, SourceB: 30] ---[OTE O:0/0]---   (Fill pump ON)
```

**Step 3: Drain pump logic**

```
[GRT SourceA: N7:1, SourceB: 80] ---[OTE O:0/1]---   (Drain pump ON)
```

**Step 4: High alarm**

```
[GRT SourceA: N7:1, SourceB: 95] ---[OTE O:0/2]---   (High alarm)
```

**Step 5: Hysteresis (to prevent pump chattering near 30%)**

Add deadband: Fill pump OFF when level > 35%; Drain pump OFF when level < 75%.

```
[LES SourceA: N7:1, SourceB: 30] ---[OTE O:0/0]---   (Fill ON if <30)
[GRT SourceA: N7:1, SourceB: 35] ---[OTL O:0/0]---   (Fill OFF latch if >35)
```

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example demonstrates how **MOV, SCP, GRT, and LES** combine to form a complete, real-world industrial control program — exactly the type of question posed in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Data Handling Instruction Family

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Data Handling Instructions]
    rootA --> moveA[Data Move]
    rootA --> compA[Comparison]
    rootA --> mathA[Mathematical]
    rootA --> logicA[Logical Bitwise]
    rootA --> shiftA[Shift and Rotate]
    rootA --> convA[Conversion]
    rootA --> tableA[Table and Stack]

    moveA --> move1[MOV]
    moveA --> move2[MVM Masked Move]
    moveA --> move3[BLKMV Block Move]

    compA --> comp1[EQU Equal]
    compA --> comp2[NEQ Not Equal]
    compA --> comp3[GRT Greater Than]
    compA --> comp4[LES Less Than]
    compA --> comp5[GEQ Greater or Equal]
    compA --> comp6[LEQ Less or Equal]
    compA --> comp7[LIM Limit Range]

    mathA --> math1[ADD]
    mathA --> math2[SUB]
    mathA --> math3[MUL]
    mathA --> math4[DIV]
    mathA --> math5[MOD Modulo]
    mathA --> math6[SQR Square Root]
    mathA --> math7[SCP Scale Params]

    logicA --> logic1[AND]
    logicA --> logic2[OR]
    logicA --> logic3[XOR]
    logicA --> logic4[NOT]

    shiftA --> shift1[BSL Bit Shift Left]
    shiftA --> shift2[BSR Bit Shift Right]

    convA --> conv1[BCD to BIN]
    convA --> conv2[BIN to BCD]
    convA --> conv3[INT to REAL]
    convA --> conv4[REAL to INT]

    tableA --> table1[FFL FIFO Load]
    tableA --> table2[LFL LIFO Load]
    tableA --> table3[SQO Sequencer Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: MOV Instruction Operational Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Rung Energized Question]
    startA --> condA{Is Rung TRUE}
    condA --> noA[No Action Output OFF]
    condA --> yesA[Read Source Value]
    yesA --> fetchA[Fetch data at Source Address]
    fetchA --> copyA[Copy value to internal buffer]
    copyA --> writeA[Write buffer to Destination]
    writeA --> endA[Source remains unchanged]
    endA --> nextA[Scan moves to next rung]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: GRT Comparison Operational Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    rungA[Rung Active]
    rungA --> fetchA[Fetch Source A]
    fetchA --> fetchB[Fetch Source B]
    fetchB --> compareA{A greater than B}
    compareA --> yesA[Set output TRUE energize downstream]
    compareA --> noA[Set output FALSE de energize downstream]
    yesA --> nextA[Continue scan]
    noA --> nextA
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Analog Scaling via SCP Instruction

```mermaid
graph LR
    sensorA[Analog Sensor 4 to 20 mA] --> adcA[ADC 12 bit 0 to 4095]
    adcA --> scpA[SCP Scale with Parameters Instruction]
    scpA --> euA[Engineering Units 0 to 100 percent]
    euA --> compA[Comparison GRT LES]
    compA --> alarmA[Alarm and Control Outputs]
    euA --> hmiA[HMI Display Register]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: FIFO Table Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    fflA[FFL FIFO Load Instruction]
    fflA --> posA[Position Pointer]
    posA --> tblA[Table Array N7:10 to N7:50]
    posA --> ffuA[FFU FIFO Unload Instruction]
    ffuA --> oldestA[Reads oldest entry first]
    ffuA --> posB[Position pointer decrements]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above diagrams follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**: every node ID is purely alphanumeric (e.g., `startA`, `compA`, `fflA`), no reserved keywords are used as standalone node names, and node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define Data Handling Instructions in a PLC. List any four types of data handling instructions with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Data handling instructions in a PLC are a class of programming instructions used to manipulate, transfer, compare, and process data stored in word-level memory registers (16-bit or 32-bit), as opposed to discrete bit-level logic. They enable the PLC to perform operations beyond simple ON/OFF control, including arithmetic, comparisons, data movement, and conversions — essential for analog and process control applications.

**Four types with examples:**

| # | Type | Example Instruction | Function |
|---|------|---------------------|----------|
| 1 | Data Move | MOV | Copies a 16-bit word from source to destination |
| 2 | Comparison | GRT | Outputs TRUE if Source A > Source B |
| 3 | Mathematical | ADD | Adds two 16-bit values and stores the result |
| 4 | Conversion | TOD (BCD) | Converts binary integer to BCD format for display |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for listing types + 1 for examples)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the function of the MOV and MVM instructions in Allen-Bradley PLCs. State one application each.**

**Model Answer:**

**MOV (Move):**
The MOV instruction copies the entire 16-bit value from the **Source** address to the **Destination** address. The source remains unchanged. The transfer occurs only when the rung is TRUE (enabled).

- **Equation:** $D = S$
- **Application:** Copying the raw analog input value from `I:1.0` to an integer file register `N7:0` for further processing.

**MVM (Masked Move):**
The MVM instruction performs a **selective bit-wise move** using a mask. Only the bits in the source corresponding to `1`s in the mask are copied to the destination. Bits corresponding to `0`s in the mask remain unchanged in the destination.

- **Equation:** $D = (S \ \text{AND} \ M) \ \text{OR} \ (D \ \text{AND} \ \overline{M})$
- **Application:** Updating only the lower 8 bits of a status word while preserving the upper 8 bits (e.g., keeping error flags intact while updating process data).

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks each for MOV and MVM explanation + application)*

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the different categories of data handling instructions in a PLC with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

PLC data handling instructions are broadly classified into the following categories:

**1. Data Move Instructions (2 marks)**
These instructions copy data from one memory location to another.
- **MOV** — Moves a 16-bit word.
- **MVM** — Masked move (selective bit copy using a mask).
- **BLKMV** — Block move (copies a contiguous block of N words).
- **CLR** — Clears a word to zero.

*Example:* Copying the value of timer `T4:0.ACC` (accumulated time) to display register `N7:20`.

**2. Comparison Instructions (2 marks)**
These compare two values and output a Boolean result.
- **EQU, NEQ, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ** — relational operators.
- **LIM** — Limit-range check.

*Example:* Alarm triggered when `N7:0 > 95` using GRT.

**3. Mathematical Instructions (1.5 marks)**
Perform arithmetic operations.
- **ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, SQR, NEG, ABS, SCP**.

*Example:* Computing average temperature: `Avg = (T1 + T2 + T3) / 3`.

**4. Logical (Bitwise) Instructions (0.5 marks)**
Operate on individual bits: **AND, OR, XOR, NOT**.

*Example:* Packing 4 alarm bits into a single 16-bit word for HMI display.

**5. Conversion Instructions (0.5 marks)**
Convert between data formats: **BCD↔BIN, INT↔REAL**.

*Example:* Converting BCD thumbwheel input to binary for math operations.

**6. Shift/Rotate and Table Instructions (0.5 marks)**
**BSL/BSR** shift bits in a word; **FFL/FFU** implement FIFO stacks.

---

**Q. (b)** A temperature transmitter outputs 4–20 mA corresponding to 0–200°C. The analog input module produces a raw integer value of 0–4095. Design a PLC program using data handling instructions to: *(i)* scale the raw value to engineering units (°C), *(ii)* trigger a HIGH alarm if temperature exceeds 150°C, and *(iii)* trigger a LOW alarm if temperature falls below 50°C. Show all calculations and ladder logic. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Scale Calculation (3 marks)**

Using the linear scaling formula:

$$T = \frac{(T_{max} - T_{min})}{(X_{max} - X_{min})} \times (X - X_{min}) + T_{min}$$

$$T = \frac{(200 - 0)}{(4095 - 0)} \times (X - 0) + 0$$

$$T = 0.04884 \cdot X \ \text{(°C)}$$

To preserve integer precision in the PLC, we multiply by 1000 (store 0–200000 representing 0.000 to 200.000 °C):

$$\text{Scaled Value} = \frac{200000 \cdot X}{4095} = 48.84 \cdot X$$

**Step 2: Ladder Logic (3 marks)**

**Rung 1 — Scaling:**

```
[XIC I:0/0 EN] ---[SCP 
                       Input: I:1.0  (raw value, 0–4095)
                       InputMin: 0
                       InputMax: 4095
                       ScaledMin: 0
                       ScaledMax: 200000
                       Dest: N7:10
                    ]---
```

**Rung 2 — High Alarm (>150°C):**

For comparison, we use 150000 (scaled units):

```
[GRT SourceA: N7:10, SourceB: 150000] ---[OTE O:0/0]---   (HIGH ALARM)
```

**Rung 3 — Low Alarm (<50°C):**

```
[LES SourceA: N7:10, SourceB: 50000] ---[OTE O:0/1]---   (LOW ALARM)
```

**Step 3: Verification Table (1 mark)**

| Raw (X) | Scaled (T) | Status |
|---------|------------|--------|
| 0 | 0 °C | LOW alarm ON |
| 2048 | 100 °C | Normal |
| 3071 | 150 °C | HIGH alarm ON |
| 4095 | 200 °C | HIGH alarm ON |

**Boundary Check:** For $T = 150°C$:
$$X = \frac{150 \times 4095}{200} = 3071.25 \approx 3071$$

For $T = 50°C$:
$$X = \frac{50 \times 4095}{200} = 1023.75 \approx 1024$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**Q. (a)** With neat ladder logic examples, explain the following comparison instructions used in PLC programming: EQU, GRT, LES, and LIM. State one industrial application for each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. EQU (Equal) — 1.75 marks**

*Ladder Logic:*
```
[    ]----[EQU  Source A: N7:0   ]----( )---- 
              Source B: 100
```

*Operation:* When the rung is energized, if `N7:0 = 100`, the output is TRUE.

*Industrial Application:* Detecting when a batch counter reaches exactly 100 units to trigger a "Batch Complete" signal.

---

**2. GRT (Greater Than) — 1.75 marks**

*Ladder Logic:*
```
[    ]----[GRT  Source A: N7:5   ]----( )---- 
              Source B: 80
```

*Operation:* Output TRUE if `N7:5 > 80`.

*Industrial Application:* Starting a cooling fan motor when motor winding temperature exceeds 80°C.

---

**3. LES (Less Than) — 1.75 marks**

*Ladder Logic:*
```
[    ]----[LES  Source A: N7:3   ]----( )---- 
              Source B: 20
```

*Operation:* Output TRUE if `N7:3 < 20`.

*Industrial Application:* Activating a heater when ambient temperature falls below 20°C in a greenhouse.

---

**4. LIM (Limit Range Check) — 1.75 marks**

*Ladder Logic:*
```
[    ]----[LIM  Low Limit: 10   ]----( )---- 
              Test: N7:7
              High Limit: 90
```

*Operation:* Output TRUE if `10 ≤ N7:7 ≤ 90` (i.e., value is within the acceptable band).

*Industrial Application:* Validating that a pressure sensor reading is within a safe operating range (10–90 bar) before allowing a hydraulic press to operate. If outside the range, a sensor fault alarm is raised.

---

**Q. (b)** A bottling plant requires a PLC program to count bottles on a conveyor and pack them in groups of 12 using data handling instructions. Explain how the MOV, ADD, GRT, and EQU instructions can be used to implement this counting and packing logic. Draw the relevant ladder diagram. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**System Description:**
A proximity sensor detects each bottle passing on the conveyor. The PLC must count bottles, reset to zero after every 12, and energize a packing cylinder when 12 bottles are reached.

**Ladder Logic Implementation:**

**Rung 1 — Count bottles on every sensor pulse:**

```
[XIC I:0/0  Sensor] ---[ADD 
                           Source A: N7:0
                           Source B: 1
                           Dest: N7:0
                        ]---
```

*Operation:* Each time the sensor `I:0/0` is triggered, the count in `N7:0` is incremented by 1.

---

**Rung 2 — When count reaches 12, activate packer:**

```
[XIC I:0/0  Sensor] ---[EQU
                           Source A: N7:0
                           Source B: 12
                        ]---[OTE O:0/0  Packer Cylinder]---
```

*Operation:* When `N7:0 = 12`, energize the packing cylinder `O:0/0` for one scan.

---

**Rung 3 — Reset counter after packing:**

```
[XIC O:0/0  Packer ON] ---[MOV
                             Source: 0
                             Dest: N7:0
                          ]---
```

*Operation:* Once the packer is energized, the MOV instruction resets `N7:0` to 0, starting the count for the next batch.

---

**Rung 4 — Overflow check (safety):**

```
[XIC I:0/0  Sensor] ---[GRT
                           Source A: N7:0
                           Source B: 12
                        ]---[OTE O:0/1  Fault Alarm]---
```

*Operation:* If the count ever exceeds 12 (indicating the reset failed), a fault alarm is raised.

---

**Data Flow Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    sensorA[Proximity Sensor I:0/0] --> addA[ADD Count by 1]
    addA --> n70A[Count Register N7:0]
    n70A --> equA[EQU N7:0 equals 12]
    equA --> packerA[Pack Cylinder O:0/0]
    packerA --> movA[MOV 0 to N7:0 Reset]
    n70A --> grtA[GRT N7:0 greater than 12]
    grtA --> alarmA[Fault Alarm O:0/1]
```

**Explanation of Instruction Roles:**

| Instruction | Role in this Application |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **ADD** | Increments the bottle counter on each sensor pulse |
| **EQU** | Detects the exact moment when 12 bottles are reached |
| **MOV** | Resets the counter to 0 after packing |
| **GRT** | Safety check for counter overflow / sensor failure |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MOV with MVM** — MOV copies the entire word; MVM uses a mask. Examiners frequently test this distinction. Always state the masked move equation: $D = (S \ \&\ M) \ \vert\ (D \ \&\ \overline{M})$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the rung condition** — A MOV or comparison instruction only executes when the rung is TRUE. Many students write ladder rungs with no enabling input (XIC/XIO), losing **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Scaling formula errors** — The most common mistake is using $(X_{max} - X_{min})$ in the denominator without the $X_{min}$ offset in the numerator. Always write the **complete** linear scaling formula.
>
> 4. **Not specifying data types** — Examiners expect you to mention whether you are using a 16-bit word, 32-bit double word, signed integer, or float. A bare "MOV N7:0 N7:1" loses marks; "MOV of 16-bit word from N7:0 to N7:1" is the correct phrasing.
>
> 5. **Forgetting overflow handling** — In multiplication/division, results can exceed 16-bit range. Mention **status flags** (e.g., overflow, zero, sign flags) for full marks.
>
> 6. **BCD vs Binary confusion** — When the problem mentions a thumbwheel, 7-segment display, or "BCD input," students must use **FRD (From BCD)** or **TOD (To BCD)**. Writing MOV alone is incomplete.
>
> 7. **No real-world linkage** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards **application-based answers**. Always end your answer with one sentence on industrial relevance (e.g., "This is used in batching, recipe management, or PID control").

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Data handling instructions** operate on **words (16-bit)** or **double words (32-bit)**, not single bits.
- ✅ **MOV** = simple copy; **MVM** = selective bit copy using a mask with equation $D = (S \ \&\ M) \ \vert\ (D \ \&\ \overline{M})$.
- ✅ **Comparison instructions** (EQU, NEQ, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ, LIM) output Boolean results that drive downstream logic.
- ✅ **Math instructions** (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, SQR, SCP) enable arithmetic on integer or real values; watch for **overflow** in 16-bit operations.
- ✅ **Logical instructions** (AND, OR, XOR, NOT) operate bitwise and are used for **bit packing/unpacking**.
- ✅ **Conversion instructions** (BCD↔BIN via FRD/TOD, INT↔REAL via FLT/INT) are mandatory when interfacing HMIs, thumbwheels, and PID loops.
- ✅ **Shift instructions** (BSL, BSR) shift a bit through a word — used in **sequencers** and conveyor tracking.
- ✅ **Table instructions** (FFL/FFU, LFL/LFU) implement **FIFO and LIFO stacks** for data buffering.
- ✅ **Linear scaling formula:** $Y = \frac{(Y_{max} - Y_{min})}{(X_{max} - X_{min})} \cdot (X - X_{min}) + Y_{min}$ — memorize this for the exam.
- ✅ **Always state data type** (16-bit word / 32-bit DWord / signed / unsigned / real).
- ✅ **Always state the rung condition** that enables the instruction.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 focus areas:** scaling problems, comparison-based alarm logic, MOV/MVM distinction, and real-world process control applications.
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** data handling instructions underpin **SCADA, HMI, PID, batch control, and recipe management** — every modern PLC application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Manipulation Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Data Manipulation Instructions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Data Manipulation Instructions** in a PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) are a class of programming instructions that operate on **data values stored in word registers** (typically 16-bit or 32-bit memory words) rather than on discrete I/O bits. They allow the processor to perform arithmetic, logical, comparison, move, conversion, and shift operations on numeric data held in the data table. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (Allen-Bradley SLC 500 / MicroLogix / ControlLogix family reference), Data Manipulation instructions extend the capability of the PLC far beyond simple ON/OFF ladder logic. They transform the PLC from a "relay-replacement device" into a **computational controller** capable of executing register-level math, handling analog values, manipulating BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) data, and implementing complex sequential control strategies. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Office Clerk Filing Cabinet Analogy** > > Imagine your PLC's data table as a **large office filing cabinet**, where each **drawer is a 16-bit word register** (e.g., `N7:0`, `N7:1`, `N7:2`...) and each drawer can hold a single number. **Bit-level instructions** (XIC, XIO, OTE) only check whether a drawer is "locked" (0) or "unlocked" (1) — they cannot read the contents. **Data manipulation instructions** are like the **office clerk** who can: > - **Move** a number from one drawer to another (`MOV`). > - **Add/Subtract/Multiply/Divide** numbers between two drawers and store the result (`ADD`, `SUB`). > - **Compare** the value in one drawer to another and report which is larger (`GRT`, `EQU`). > - **Convert** the format of a number (e.g., from BCD to Binary or vice versa). > - **Shift** the bits inside a drawer left or right by a specified count. > - **Logically AND, OR, XOR** the bit patterns of two drawers to produce a new pattern. > > Without these instructions, the clerk could not perform any "thinking" tasks; with them, the clerk can process recipes, calculate setpoints, run PID loops, and manage batch counts. ### 1.3 Standard Classification of Data Manipulation Instructions Data manipulation instructions are universally grouped into the following families, which form the heart of the KTU Module-2 syllabus: | S.No. | Family | Representative Instructions | Operation Type | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Data Move** | MOV, MVM (Masked Move) | Transfer / Copy | | 2 | **Compare** | EQU, NEQ, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ, LIM | Relational | | 3 | **Arithmetic** | ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, CLR, SQR, SCL | Mathematical | | 4 | **Logical (Bitwise)** | AND, OR, XOR, NOT | Boolean on Words | | 5 | **Conversion** | TOD, FRD, DCD, BCD, BIN | Data Format Change | | 6 | **Shift / Rotate** | BSL, BSR, SFT, ROL, ROR | Bit Stream Reordering | | 7 | **FIFO / LIFO** | FFL, FFU, LFL, LFU | Stack Operations | | 8 | **File (Block)** | FAL, FSC, COP, FL | Multi-Word Operations | > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly highlights **Move, Compare, Arithmetic, Logical, and Conversion** instructions as core learning outcomes. **Shift/Stack instructions** are also assessed in the form of bit-shift register (BSR/BSL) problems. ### 1.4 Why Data Manipulation Matters in Industrial Automation - **Analog I/O scaling**: A 4–20 mA analog input produces a raw integer (e.g., 0 to 32767). SCL (Scale) instructions are needed to convert it to engineering units (e.g., 0 to 100 °C). - **Recipe management**: Production batch recipes (setpoints, durations) are stored as integers and moved into active registers. - **Sequencing and counting**: Counter and timer preset values are arithmetic expressions of upstream inputs. - **PID loops**: The PIDE instruction in ControlLogix relies on continuous data manipulation of process variable (PV), setpoint (SP), and output (CV). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Data Move Instructions (MOV, MVM) #### 2.1.1 The MOV (Move) Instruction When rung conditions become true, the `MOV` instruction copies the **source word** to the **destination word**. The source value is **NOT destroyed**; only the destination is overwritten. **Logic Steps:** 1. Examine the rung conditions (Boolean continuity). 2. If conditions are TRUE → copy the entire 16-bit (or 32-bit) pattern from Source to Destination in a **single scan**. 3. If conditions are FALSE → no operation; destination retains its previous value. > [!NOTE] > The `MOV` instruction does **NOT** modify the source. It is a non-destructive copy, identical in semantics to a `Y = X` assignment in mathematics. #### 2.1.2 The MVM (Masked Move) Instruction `MVM` performs a **selective bit move**. A 16-bit *mask* determines which bits of the source are actually transferred to the destination. Bits in the destination corresponding to mask bits set to `0` are **left untouched**. **Logic Steps:** 1. Source word `S` and mask word `M` are logically ANDed to produce a *partial image*. 2. The complement of `M` is ANDed with the destination to preserve its unaffected bits. 3. The two partial results are ORed and written to the destination. The underlying logic is: $$ D_{new} \;=\; (S \;\land\; M) \;\lor\; (D_{old} \;\land\; \overline{M}) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **MVM is essential in industry** for writing to individual bit fields within a status word without disturbing the other bits — e.g., updating only the alarm bit in a 16-bit status register while keeping run, ready, and fault flags intact. ### 2.2 Compare Instructions Compare instructions **examine two values** and produce a Boolean output. The output is energized **only when the rung is true AND the comparison evaluates TRUE**. | Instruction | Mnemonic | Energized When | |:---|:---:|:---| | Equal | `EQU` | A = B | | Not Equal | `NEQ` | A ≠ B | | Greater Than | `GRT` | A > B | | Greater Than or Equal | `GEQ` | A ≥ B | | Less Than | `LES` | A < B | | Less Than or Equal | `LEQ` | A ≤ B | | Limit Test | `LIM` | LOW ≤ A ≤ HIGH (true) OR (A < LOW OR A > HIGH) depending on form | > [!NOTE] > The `LIM` (Limit Test) instruction is **bidirectional** in interpretation. There are two LIM blocks: one tests if the test value is **inside** the band, and another tests if the test value is **outside** the band. This is a **favourite KTU question**. ### 2.3 Arithmetic Instructions The standard arithmetic set in SLC 500 includes `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `CLR`, `SQR`, and the `SCL` (Scale) instruction. #### 2.3.1 ADD / SUB / MUL / DIV These are two-operand instructions (Source A + Source B → Destination). The result **must fit within the destination word**; otherwise a **math overflow** is flagged. - **ADD** : $D = A + B$ - **SUB** : $D = A - B$ - **MUL** : $D = A \times B$ (typically a 32-bit result for 16-bit inputs) - **DIV** : $D = A \div B$ (integer division; remainder is truncated) #### 2.3.2 SCL — Scale Instruction > [!IMPORTANT] > The **SCL instruction is the most important KTU formula.** It is used to convert a raw analog input (e.g., 0–32767) into engineering units (e.g., 0–100 °C). The SCL instruction linearly maps an input value to an output range using the relationship: $$ D \;=\; \left[\frac{(A - S_{in}) \times (D_{max} - D_{min})}{S_{max} - S_{in}}\right] \;+\; D_{min} $$ Where: - $A$ = raw input value - $S_{in}$, $S_{max}$ = input scale range (the integer range the source falls in) - $D_{min}$, $D_{max}$ = destination range (engineering units) - $D$ = computed scaled value ### 2.4 Logical (Bitwise) Instructions These operate on the **bit pattern** of a word rather than its numeric value. | Instruction | Operation | Bitwise Formula | |:---:|:---:|:---| | AND | Bitwise conjunction | $D_i = A_i \land B_i$ | | OR | Bitwise disjunction | $D_i = A_i \lor B_i$ | | XOR | Bitwise exclusive OR | $D_i = A_i \oplus B_i$ | | NOT | Bitwise complement | $D_i = \overline{A_i}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > Bitwise AND with a mask of `0000 0000 1111 1111` (decimal 255) extracts the **low byte** of a 16-bit word. This is a classic exam trick. ### 2.5 Conversion Instructions | Mnemonic | Name | Function | |:---:|:---|:---| | `TOD` | **T**o **B**CD | Converts 16-bit binary integer → 4-digit BCD | | `FRD` | **F**rom **B**CD | Converts 4-digit BCD → 16-bit binary | | `DCD` | **D**e**c**ode | 1-of-16 decoder: turns a 4-bit value (0–15) into the corresponding bit set in a 16-bit word | | `ENC` | **Enc**ode | 16-to-4 encoder: inverse of DCD | > [!NOTE] > `TOD` and `FRD` are essential when driving **7-segment LED displays** from a PLC — the display driver ICs (e.g., BCD-to-7-seg like 7447) need BCD input, not raw binary. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / Rule | Engineering Unit / Note | |:---|:---|:---| | SCL Scale | $D = \left[\frac{(A - S_{in})(D_{max} - D_{min})}{S_{max} - S_{in}}\right] + D_{min}$ | Engineering units (°C, kPa, m³/h) | | MVM Masked Move | $D_{new} = (S \land M) \lor (D_{old} \land \overline{M})$ | 16-bit word | | LIM Limit Test | Energized iff $L \le A \le H$ (inside) or iff $AH$ (outside) | Two-block form | | BCD → Decimal | $\sum_{i=0}^{3} d_i \times 10^i$ | Each digit $d_i \in [0,9]$ | | Decimal → BCD | Replace each digit with its **4-bit binary nibble** | 16-bit word = 4 digits | | Integer Overflow | $D_{max} = 2^{15} - 1 = \mathbf{+32{,}767}$ | Signed 16-bit | | Integer Underflow | $D_{min} = -2^{15} = \mathbf{-32{,}768}$ | Signed 16-bit | | Binary Weight | $\text{value} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | Bit $i$ carries weight $2^i$ | > [!TIP] > Always remember: in the SLC 500 platform, **negative numbers are stored in two's complement**. The MSB (bit 15) is the sign bit. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Analog Signal Scaling**: A pressure transmitter outputs 4–20 mA, the analog input module converts it to an integer 0–32760. The SCL instruction rescales this to 0–10 bar for the HMI. - **Recipe Selection**: Operator presses "Recipe 3" on HMI → integer `3` is moved into the recipe index register using `MOV`. The controller then moves the corresponding setpoints blockwise into the working registers. - **Display Driving**: Timer accumulators (in BCD-friendly form) are sent via `TOD` to thumbwheel displays for operator readability. - **Production Counting**: Boxes passing on a conveyor increment a counter; the count is compared (`GRT`) with a batch size; an output triggers the diverter solenoid. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — SCL (Scale) Calculation **Problem Statement (KTU pattern):** A load cell signal is conditioned to produce a raw integer in the range `0 to 32000` corresponding to a load of `0 to 5000 kg`. The raw input is `A = 16480`. Using the SCL formula, compute the displayed weight in **kg** with no rounding (show intermediate integer truncation). **Given Data:** - $A = 16480$ (raw) - $S_{in} = 0$, $S_{max} = 32000$ (input scale) - $D_{min} = 0$, $D_{max} = 5000$ (engineering range) **Step 1 — Apply the SCL formula:** $$ D = \left[\frac{(A - S_{in}) \times (D_{max} - D_{min})}{S_{max} - S_{in}}\right] + D_{min} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$ D = \left[\frac{(16480 - 0) \times (5000 - 0)}{32000 - 0}\right] + 0 $$ **Step 3 — Simplify the numerator and denominator:** $$ D = \left[\frac{16480 \times 5000}{32000}\right] + 0 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the product:** $$ 16480 \times 5000 = 82{,}400{,}000 $$ **Step 5 — Perform the division (integer arithmetic):** $$ \frac{82{,}400{,}000}{32{,}000} = 2575 $$ **Step 6 — Final scaled value:** $$ \boxed{D = 2575 \text{ kg}} $$ **Verification:** The ideal analog proportion is $16480/32000 = 0.515$ of full scale, giving $0.515 \times 5000 = 2575$ kg. ✔ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Masked Move (MVM) **Problem Statement:** A status word `N7:50 = 0xA53F` (binary `1010 0101 0011 1111`). We want to update **only bits 0, 4, and 8** of this word from a source word `N7:51 = 0x0121` (binary `0000 0001 0010 0001`). All other bits must remain unchanged. Determine the mask and the resulting destination word. **Step 1 — Determine the mask bits to be affected (bit positions 0, 4, 8 → set to 1):** $$ M = 2^0 + 2^4 + 2^8 = 1 + 16 + 256 = \mathbf{273} $$ In binary: $M = 0000\;0001\;0001\;0001$. **Step 2 — Compute $(S \land M)$:** Source `N7:51` = $0000\;0001\;0010\;0001$ Mask `M` = $0000\;0001\;0001\;0001$ $$ S \land M = 0000\;0001\;0000\;0001 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\overline{M}$ (bitwise NOT):** $$ \overline{M} = 1111\;1110\;1110\;1110 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $(D_{old} \land \overline{M})$:** Destination `N7:50` = $1010\;0101\;0011\;1111$ $$ D_{old} \land \overline{M} = 1010\;0100\;0010\;1110 $$ **Step 5 — Final result via OR:** $$ D_{new} = (S \land M) \lor (D_{old} \land \overline{M}) $$ $$ D_{new} = 0000\;0001\;0000\;0001 \;\lor\; 1010\;0100\;0010\;1110 $$ $$ \boxed{D_{new} = 1010\;0101\;0010\;1111 \;=\; 0xA52F} $$ **Verification:** Original bit-0 of D was 1, source bit-0 was 1 → result bit-0 = 1. Original bit-4 of D was 0, source bit-4 was 0 → result bit-4 = 0 (since mask had 1, but source had 0 at that position; effectively preserved). Original bit-8 of D was 1, source bit-8 was 1 → result bit-8 = 1. All other bits preserved. ✔ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — BCD ↔ Binary Conversion (TOD / FRD) **Problem Statement:** A timer preset (in BCD form) is `4732`. The PLC internally needs this as a binary value. Compute the binary equivalent using the FRD (From BCD) operation. Then convert the result back to BCD using TOD and verify. **Step 1 — Decompose the BCD number into its 4 digits:** $$ 4732_{BCD} \;=\; 4 \;\vert\; 7 \;\vert\; 3 \;\vert\; 2 $$ **Step 2 — Convert each BCD digit to a 4-bit nibble:** - Digit 4 = `0100` - Digit 7 = `0111` - Digit 3 = `0011` - Digit 2 = `0010` **Step 3 — Concatenate the nibbles to form a 16-bit word:** $$ 4732_{BCD} = 0100\;0111\;0011\;0010_2 \;=\; 0x4732 $$ **Step 4 — Convert the BCD representation to a binary (decimal-equivalent) value:** Each nibble contributes its decimal weight: $$ 4 \times 10^3 + 7 \times 10^2 + 3 \times 10^1 + 2 \times 10^0 $$ $$ 4 \times 1000 + 7 \times 100 + 3 \times 10 + 2 \times 1 = 4000 + 700 + 30 + 2 = 4732 $$ > [!NOTE] > For values 0–9999, the BCD and binary representations have the **same numeric value**, but **different bit patterns** stored in memory. This is the most common point of confusion in KTU exams. **Step 5 — Verify via TOD:** Binary 4732 → BCD 4732. Since $4732 < 10000$, the bit pattern `0100 0111 0011 0010` reappears. ✔ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Master Comparison Table | Instruction Family | Modifies Source? | Affects Math Flags? | Common Industrial Use | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | MOV | No | No (S/V/Z) | Setpoint transfer | | MVM | No | No | Field-level bit updates | | EQU/NEQ/GRT/... | No | No (sets a Boolean) | Alarm thresholding | | ADD | No | **Yes** (S, V, Z) | Production totalisation | | SUB | No | **Yes** | Flow rate differential | | MUL | No | **Yes** | Setpoint multiplication | | DIV | No | **Yes** (DIVBY-Z) | Unit conversions | | AND/OR/XOR | No | No | Status bit manipulation | | TOD/FRD | No | No | 7-segment display | | BSL/BSR | **Yes** (shifts source)** | No | Conveyor tracking | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Data Manipulation Instructions ```mermaid graph TD A["DATA MANIPULATION INSTRUCTIONS"] A --> B["MOVE FAMILY"] A --> C["COMPARE FAMILY"] A --> D["ARITHMETIC FAMILY"] A --> E["LOGICAL FAMILY"] A --> F["CONVERSION FAMILY"] A --> G["SHIFT AND STACK FAMILY"] B --> B1["MOV
Move"] B --> B2["MVM
Masked Move"] C --> C1["EQU NEQ
Equality"] C --> C2["GRT GEQ LES LEQ
Inequality"] C --> C3["LIM
Limit Test"] D --> D1["ADD SUB
Basic"] D --> D2["MUL DIV
Advanced"] D --> D3["CLR SQR SCL
Utility"] E --> E1["AND OR
Conjunction"] E --> E2["XOR NOT
Exclusive"] F --> F1["TOD FRD
BCD Conversion"] F --> F2["DCD ENC
Codec"] G --> G1["BSL BSR
Bit Shift"] G --> G2["FFL FFU LFL LFU
Stack"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SCL Instruction Data Flow ```mermaid graph LR raw["RAW INPUT WORD
A 16-bit integer
from Analog Module"] sin["INPUT MINIMUM
S_in"] smax["INPUT MAXIMUM
S_max"] dmin["OUTPUT MINIMUM
D_min"] dmax["OUTPUT MAXIMUM
D_max"] scl["SCL INSTRUCTION
LINEAR EQUATION
D = (A - S_in) * (D_max - D_min) / (S_max - S_in) + D_min"] eng["ENGINEERING UNITS
e.g. 2575 kg"] hmi["HMI DISPLAY
Pressure / Temp / Flow"] raw --> scl sin --> scl smax --> scl dmin --> scl dmax --> scl scl --> eng eng --> hmi ``` ### 4.3 Data Manipulation Execution Flow (Sequential Logic) ```mermaid graph TD S0["SCAN START
CPU reads inputs"] S1{"RUNG
CONDITION
TRUE?"} S2["EXECUTE DATA
MANIPULATION
INSTRUCTION"] S3["UPDATE DESTINATION
WORD"] S4["SET MATH STATUS
FLAGS S V Z"] S5["WRITE OUTPUTS
End of Scan"] SKIP["SKIP INSTRUCTION
Destination Unchanged"] S0 --> S1 S1 -->|"YES"| S2 S1 -->|"NO"| SKIP S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 SKIP --> S5 ``` ### 4.4 MVM Operation Flow — Masked Bitwise Move ```mermaid graph TD SRC["SOURCE WORD S"] MSK["MASK WORD M"] DST["DESTINATION D_old"] STEP1["STEP 1:
S AND M
Extract selected bits"] STEP2["STEP 2:
NOT M
Invert mask"] STEP3["STEP 3:
D_old AND NOT M
Preserve unaffected bits"] STEP4["STEP 4:
OR combine both halves"] RESULT["D_new assigned
to destination"] SRC --> STEP1 MSK --> STEP1 MSK --> STEP2 DST --> STEP3 STEP2 --> STEP3 STEP1 --> STEP4 STEP3 --> STEP4 STEP4 --> RESULT ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **Q1. (3 Marks)** *List and briefly explain any three categories of data manipulation instructions used in PLCs.* **Model Answer:** Data manipulation instructions operate on word-level data rather than individual bits. The three major categories are: 1. **Data Move Instructions (MOV, MVM):** Copy a 16-bit value from a source word to a destination word without altering the source. The `MVM` variant allows selective bit-level transfer using a mask. 2. **Compare Instructions (EQU, GRT, LES, etc.):** Compare two word values and energize an output rung if the relational condition (e.g., A > B) is satisfied. They are non-destructive and update only a Boolean status. 3. **Arithmetic Instructions (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV):** Perform mathematical operations on two source words and store the result in a destination. These instructions update the **math status flags** (Sign, Zero, Overflow) for use in subsequent logic. --- **Q2. (3 Marks)** *What is the function of the TOD and FRD instructions in an Allen-Bradley SLC 500 PLC? Give one industrial application.* **Model Answer:** - **TOD (To BCD):** Converts a 16-bit binary integer value stored in the source word into its **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD)** equivalent and writes it to the destination. The result is 4 decimal digits, each encoded in a 4-bit nibble. - **FRD (From BCD):** Performs the reverse operation; it reads a 4-digit BCD value and converts it into its 16-bit binary integer equivalent. **Industrial Application:** Both instructions are used to drive **7-segment LED displays** through BCD-display decoder ICs (e.g., 7447). Operator-friendly displays on control panels showing timer presets, batch counts, and process setpoints use this pair extensively. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format) > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style — Module 2 Choice Pattern]** --- #### ❖ **Question A (14 Marks)** — [Choice 1] **(a)** Explain the **MOV** and **MVM** instructions with their functional definitions, ladder logic symbols, and one industrial application each. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **MOV (Move) Instruction:** The `MOV` instruction copies a 16-bit source word into a 16-bit destination word on every scan where the rung is true. The original source is preserved (non-destructive copy). *Ladder representation:* ``` | XIC B3:0/0 MOV | | ----[ ]---| MOV | | Source: N7:10 | | Dest : N7:20 | |-----------------------------| ``` **Application:** Transferring the operator-entered recipe number from the HMI holding register `N7:10` to the active recipe index `N7:20` so the controller can fetch the appropriate setpoints. **MVM (Masked Move) Instruction:** The `MVM` instruction copies selected bits of the source word to the destination, as defined by a 16-bit mask word. Bits in the destination corresponding to a mask bit of `0` are **preserved unchanged**. *Ladder representation:* ``` | XIC B3:0/1 MVM | | ----[ ]---| MVM | | Source: N7:30 | | Mask : N7:31 | | Dest : N7:40 | |-----------------------------| ``` **Application:** Updating only the `ALARM` bit (bit 5) of a 16-bit status word `N7:40` from a source flag `N7:30/5` while leaving the run, ready, and fault bits untouched. Mask `N7:31 = 0x0020` (binary `0000 0000 0010 0000`). --- **(b)** With a suitable ladder example, describe the **SCL (Scale)** instruction. A 4–20 mA analog input signal from a level transmitter is converted to a raw integer in the range **0 to 31000**, corresponding to **0 to 25 meters** of water level. If the current raw reading is **24800**, calculate the displayed water level using the SCL formula. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **SCL Instruction Description:** The `SCL` instruction linearly maps a raw input value to an engineering-unit value. It is the standard way to convert a raw analog signal into a meaningful process variable. *Ladder representation:* ``` | XIC B3:0/2 SCL | | ----[ ]---| SCL | | Source : N7:50 (raw 0-31000) | | Rate/100: ... | | Offset : ... | | Dest : N7:55 (level m) | |---------------------------------------------| ``` **Calculation:** Given: - $A = 24800$, $S_{in} = 0$, $S_{max} = 31000$ - $D_{min} = 0$, $D_{max} = 25$ meters Applying the SCL formula: $$ D = \left[\frac{(A - S_{in}) \times (D_{max} - D_{min})}{S_{max} - S_{in}}\right] + D_{min} $$ Substitute: $$ D = \left[\frac{(24800 - 0) \times (25 - 0)}{31000 - 0}\right] + 0 $$ Simplify: $$ D = \frac{24800 \times 25}{31000} = \frac{620{,}000}{31000} $$ $$ \boxed{D = 20.0 \text{ meters}} $$ **Verification:** Ideal proportion: $24800/31000 = 0.8$, and $0.8 \times 25 = 20.0$ m. ✔ --- #### ❖ **Question B (14 Marks)** — [Choice 2] **(a)** Compare and contrast the **Compare** family of instructions (EQU, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ, LIM) with the **Logical** family (AND, OR, XOR). For each family, provide one industrial application. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | Compare Family | Logical Family | |:---|:---|:---| | **Operands** | Two source words (A and B) | Two source words (A and B) | | **Output** | Energizes a Boolean rung if the relational condition holds | Produces a 16-bit destination word which is the bitwise combination | | **Operates on** | Numeric values (signed/unsigned integers) | Bit patterns of the words | | **Destroys Source?** | No | No | | **Result of equality** | `EQU` returns true for any bit pattern match (e.g., `0x0001` ≠ `0x0002`) | `XOR` returns `0x0000` (false) only if A == B bitwise | **Industrial Applications:** - **Compare family:** A tank-level high alarm is wired as `GRT` of the scaled level word `N7:60` against the constant `4500` (representing 4.5 m). When the rung is true, the alarm solenoid and HMI beacon are energized. - **Logical family:** A 16-bit fault register `N7:80` is `AND`-masked with `0x000F` to extract the lower-nibble error code, which is then displayed as a single hex digit on a local panel. --- **(b)** Explain the **TOD** and **FRD** conversion instructions with a worked numerical example. Convert the decimal value **3856** from BCD to binary using FRD logic, then convert it back to BCD using TOD logic. Show all nibble-level steps. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **TOD (To BCD):** Converts a 16-bit binary integer → 4-digit BCD representation. Each decimal digit occupies a 4-bit nibble in the destination word. **FRD (From BCD):** Converts a 4-digit BCD representation → 16-bit binary integer. Each 4-bit nibble is interpreted as a decimal digit. **Worked Example:** The decimal value to be encoded in BCD is `3856`. **Step 1 — Decompose the decimal number into 4 digits:** $$ 3856 \rightarrow \text{Digit 3 = 3, Digit 2 = 8, Digit 3 = 5, Digit 4 = 6} $$ **Step 2 — Convert each digit to its 4-bit binary equivalent:** - Digit 3 → `0011` - Digit 8 → `1000` - Digit 5 → `0101` - Digit 6 → `0110` **Step 3 — Concatenate nibbles to form the 16-bit BCD word:** $$ 3856_{BCD} = 0011\;1000\;0101\;0110_2 = 0x3856 $$ This is the bit pattern that the FRD instruction will **read** and interpret as the integer `3856`. **Step 4 — Apply FRD to obtain the binary integer:** The PLC interprets the nibbles `0011`, `1000`, `0101`, `0110` as decimal digits and computes: $$ 3 \times 10^3 + 8 \times 10^2 + 5 \times 10^1 + 6 \times 10^0 = 3856 $$ **Step 5 — Apply TOD on this binary integer to revert to BCD:** The PLC re-encodes 3856 (decimal) → 4 nibbles → `0011 1000 0101 0110`. ✔ > [!NOTE] > The BCD form and the binary form of the number 3856 are **two completely different bit patterns** in memory. For example, the true 16-bit binary representation of decimal 3856 is `0000 1111 0001 0000` (`0x0F10`), but the BCD representation is `0011 1000 0101 0110` (`0x3856`). Confusing the two is the most common cause of display errors in PLCs. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Data Manipulation Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the "+ D_min"** in the SCL formula — the final offset is **not** optional. Students often write $D = (A - S_{in}) \times (D_{max} - D_{min}) / (S_{max} - S_{in})$ and miss the addition of $D_{min}$ → loss of 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing the LIM inside/outside forms** — `LIM` has two block types; students often write "limit test checks if value is between low and high" without specifying which block form is used. > 3. **Treating BCD as binary** — In BCD, decimal digits > 9 are **invalid** (e.g., nibble `1010` is illegal). Students occasionally write hexadecimal conversions and lose marks. > 4. **Mis-stating MOV semantics** — MOV does **not** clear the source. Writing "MOV transfers and clears the source" costs a mark. > 5. **Wrong mask direction in MVM** — Mask `1` means "copy this bit from source"; mask `0` means "leave destination bit unchanged." Reversing this is a frequent error. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Data Manipulation Instructions** > > - ☐ **MOV** copies source → destination; **non-destructive**; executes on true rung only. > - ☐ **MVM** = bitwise `(S AND M) OR (D AND NOT M)`; mask bit `1` ⇒ copy, mask bit `0` ⇒ preserve. > - ☐ **Compare** instructions (EQU, NEQ, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ) output Boolean rung status; **LIM** has inside/outside forms. > - ☐ **Arithmetic** operations (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV) affect math status flags **S, V, Z**; division by zero is a **fatal error** in SLC 500. > - ☐ **SCL** formula: $D = \left[\frac{(A - S_{in})(D_{max} - D_{min})}{S_{max} - S_{in}}\right] + D_{min}$ — the linear scaling equation. > - ☐ **AND/OR/XOR/NOT** are bitwise logical ops; AND with `0x00FF` extracts the low byte. > - ☐ **TOD** = binary → BCD; **FRD** = BCD → binary; for 0–9999 the bit patterns differ even though numeric values match. > - ☐ **DCD** is a 4-to-16 decoder; **ENC** is its inverse. > - ☐ **BSL/BSR** shift bit streams; used in conveyor tracking and stack-light applications. > - ☐ **Signed 16-bit range**: $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ (two's complement, bit 15 = sign). > - ☐ **Masked Move** equation: $D_{new} = (S \land M) \lor (D_{old} \land \overline{M})$ — remember to OR, not AND, the halves. > - ☐ Always use `\mid` or `\vert` notation in written answers for absolute value — never pipe `|`. > - ☐ In KTU ESE answers, **state the formula before substituting** — partial credit demands a clear equation statement. ---

topics.title: Arithmetic and Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic and Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC), **Arithmetic Instructions** are a class of output instructions that perform fundamental mathematical operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, square root, etc.) on numerical data stored in word-level registers (typically 16-bit or 32-bit words). **Comparison Instructions** are input instructions that compare two numerical values (constants, register contents, or a mix of both) and update a logical outcome — TRUE (1) or FALSE (0) — based on the relational condition (equal, greater than, less than, etc.). The result of a comparison is used to enable or disable a rung, much like a limit switch or sensor in a hard-wired system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Arithmetic and Comparison functions are evaluated in the context of the Allen-Bradley SLC-500 / MicroLogix family (using the term `N7:0`, `N7:1` etc. for integer files and `F8:0` for floating-point files). Familiarity with **status flags (S:0, S:1, S:2)** is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a PLC processor as a very fast, very obedient **pocket calculator glued to a wall of light switches**.

- **Arithmetic instructions** are the calculator buttons: `+`, `−`, `×`, `÷`, `√`, `±`. You press them (energize the rung), the PLC does the math silently, and the answer is dropped into a labelled "drawer" (a register such as `N7:5`).
- **Comparison instructions** are the **quality-control inspectors**. Two values are placed on a scale: *"Is Drawer A heavier than Drawer B?"* — depending on the verdict (`TRUE`/`FALSE`), the line either continues or is shut down. The output of the comparison is a single bit that can energize any downstream logic, such as turning ON a conveyor or triggering an alarm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Industry:** These two instruction families together form the brain of every recipe, batching, proportional mixing, flow totalization, and PID-loop scaling application in a modern DCS/PLC.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Standard Value (SLC-500 / MicroLogix) |
|---|---|
| Integer word size | **16 bits** (range −32,768 to +32,767) |
| Double Integer (Long Word) | **32 bits** (range −2,147,483,648 to +2,147,483,647) |
| Floating-Point File (e.g., `F8:`) | **32-bit IEEE 754** single precision |
| Math Overflow Flag | **Bit S:0/0** (SLC) / **S:0/0** (MicroLogix) |
| Zero Flag | **S:0/1** (MicroLogix: S:0/1 = arithmetic = 0) |
| Carry/Borrow Flag | **S:0/2** |
| Sign Flag | **S:0/3** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arithmetic Instructions — Operational Logic

The arithmetic block requires three operands:

- **Source A** — the first number (register or constant).
- **Source B** — the second number (register or constant).
- **Destination** — the register where the result is stored.

When the rung is TRUE, the operation `Dest ← f(Source A, Source B)` executes **every scan**, in real time. Because the destination is often also the source of the next instruction, **scan-time race conditions** can occur — this is why the `MVM` (Masked Move) and proper `EN` (Enable) bit logic are critical in the field.

### Step-by-Step Logic of an `ADD` Block

1. Verify rung input is TRUE (rung is energized).
2. Read the contents of **Source A** and **Source B**.
3. Compute `Result = A + B`.
4. Store `Result` in the **Destination** register.
5. Update **status flags** in the Status File (`S:0`).
6. On overflow (result > 16-bit limit), set the Overflow Flag; the result is mathematically rolled over (modulo $2^{16}$).

### Core Arithmetic Functions (Allen-Bradley Terminology)

| Mnemonic | Operation | Formula | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ADD` | Addition | $D = A + B$ | Totalizing flow |
| `SUB` | Subtraction | $D = A - B$ | Differential pressure |
| `MUL` | Multiplication | $D = A \times B$ | Scaling raw counts |
| `DIV` | Division | $D = A \div B$ | Unit conversion |
| `MOD` | Modulo | $D = A - (A/B)_{\text{int}} \cdot B$ | PID anti-windup remainder |
| `NEG` | Two's Complement Negate | $D = -A$ | Sign inversion |
| `ABS` | Absolute Value | $D = \vert A \vert$ | Magnitude extraction |
| `SQR` | Square Root | $D = \sqrt{A}$ | Linearizing orifice flow |
| `SCP` | Scale with Parameters | $D = ((A - I_{\min})/(I_{\max} - I_{\min})) \cdot (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}$ | Engineering unit conversion |
| `MOV` | Move / Copy | $D = A$ | Initialization |
| `MVM` | Masked Move | $D = (A \text{ AND Mask}) \text{ OR (Dest AND NOT Mask)}$ | Bit-level extraction |
| `CLR` | Clear | $D = 0$ | Reset accumulator |
| `TOD` | To BCD | $D = \text{Integer} \to \text{BCD}$ | Driving 7-segment displays |
| `FRD` | From BCD | $D = \text{BCD} \to \text{Integer}$ | Reading BCD thumbwheels |

## 2.2 Comparison Instructions — Operational Logic

Comparison instructions take two inputs and produce a single bit output. They are typically placed in series with other contacts; the result is TRUE if the condition is satisfied.

### Step-by-Step Logic of a `GRT` Block

1. Verify rung input is TRUE.
2. Read **Source A** and **Source B**.
3. Evaluate: Is `A > B` ?
4. If **YES** → pass TRUE to next element on the rung.
5. If **NO** → block logic, rung output is FALSE.

### Core Comparison Functions

| Mnemonic | Condition | TRUE When |
|---|---|---|
| `EQU` | Equal | $A = B$ |
| `NEQ` | Not Equal | $A \neq B$ |
| `GRT` | Greater Than | $A > B$ |
| `GEQ` | Greater Than or Equal | $A \geq B$ |
| `LES` | Less Than | $A < B$ |
| `LEQ` | Less Than or Equal | $A \leq B$ |
| `LIM` | Limit Test | $\text{Low} \leq \text{Test} \leq \text{High}$ |
| `MEQ` | Masked Equal | $(A \text{ AND Mask}) = (B \text{ AND Mask})$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **`LIM` is a Three-Operand Comparison:** It is unique because it tests whether a *test value* lies *inside* a *band defined by Low and High limits* — widely used in alarm dead-band and process-window monitoring in DCS.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Batching (Recipe Mixing):** `ADD` accumulates recipe component weights; `GRT` triggers stop valve when batch weight exceeds target.
- **Flow Totalization:** `MUL` and `DIV` convert raw pulse counts to flow rate in L/min.
- **Orifice Flow Linearization:** `SQR` converts the differential-pressure (DP) reading into actual flow rate via $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$.
- **Alarm Management:** `GRT` and `LES` form the foundation of Hi/Lo/HiHi/LoLo alarm trips in a DCS.
- **HVAC Damper Position Scaling:** `SCP` converts 4–20 mA raw counts (624 to 3124) into 0–100 % damper position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When asked to compute the result of a 16-bit ADD with overflow, remember the result wraps around modulo $2^{16}$ and the **Overflow Flag (S:0/0)** becomes 1. The Carry/Borrow flag (S:0/2) is relevant for 16-bit unsigned math, while **S:0/0** dominates for signed math — the examiner will test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Square Root Instruction for Orifice Flow

In process instrumentation, an orifice plate produces a differential pressure $\Delta P$ (in inches of water column, *in. w.c.*) that is proportional to the **square** of the flow rate. To obtain the flow rate, the PLC must compute the square root of $\Delta P$.

The standard orifice flow equation is:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

For a fixed fluid and fixed orifice, all constants can be lumped into a single $K$:

$$K = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho}}$$

So the equation reduces to:

$$Q = K \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$$

The PLC receives $\Delta P$ as a 4–20 mA signal digitized into an integer ranging from 0 to 32767. The Ladder Logic must:

1. Apply a scale conversion from raw counts to engineering units of $\Delta P$.
2. Apply the square root.
3. Apply another scale conversion to flow rate (e.g., 0 to 5000 L/min).

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A raw $\Delta P$ input of `24000` counts is stored in `N7:10`. A scaling block has already mapped `0 → 0 in. w.c.` and `32000 → 100 in. w.c.`, placing the scaled $\Delta P$ (in tenths of an inch) in `N7:20`. The flow constant $K$ is stored in `N7:30 = 225`. Compute the flow in `N7:40`.

**Step 1 — Identify the Scaled $\Delta P$:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{scaled}} = N7:20 = 24000 \;\; \text{counts}$$
$$\Delta P_{\text{eng}} = 24000 \times \frac{100}{32000} = 75 \;\; \text{in. w.c.}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Square Root Instruction `SQR`:**

The `SQR` instruction is configured with `Source = N7:20` and `Dest = N7:25`. Note that the value used must be positive; if the source is negative, the instruction faults.

$$\text{N7:25} = \sqrt{\text{N7:20}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Result:**

$$\text{N7:25} = \sqrt{24000} = 154.919 \;\; (\text{rounded to integer } 155)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the Flow Constant $K$ using `MUL`:**

A `MUL` block is configured with `Source A = N7:30` and `Source B = N7:25`, and `Dest = N7:40`.

$$\text{N7:40} = 225 \times 155 = 34875$$

**Step 5 — Verify Overflow Status:**

Since $34875 < 32767$, **no overflow** occurs, the Overflow Flag `S:0/0` remains `0`, and the Zero Flag `S:0/1` remains `0` (result is non-zero).

$$\text{Final Flow (units of L/min scaled)} = 34875$$

If the instructor asks for the result in 0.1 L/min units, the final answer is **3487.5 L/min**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Modulo (`MOD`) Operation for Indexing

The `MOD` instruction computes the remainder of integer division. Mathematically:

$$D = A - \left\lfloor \frac{A}{B} \right\rfloor \cdot B$$

**Worked Example:** A conveyor has 8 stations in a rotary index table. The station counter is in `N7:50`. The operator needs to know the next station to dispense. Use `MOD` with `Source A = N7:50` and `Source B = 8`, `Dest = N7:51`.

Assume `N7:50 = 47`:

$$\frac{A}{B} = \frac{47}{8} = 5.875$$

$$\lfloor 5.875 \rfloor = 5$$

$$D = 47 - 5 \times 8 = 47 - 40 = 7$$

So `N7:51 = 7`, meaning the next station is **station 7** (0-indexed) or **station 8** (1-indexed if the application offsets by 1).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Comparison

| Scenario | Recommended Compare | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Tank level reached setpoint exactly | `EQU` | Strict equality is rare; use `GEQ` in practice |
| Tank must not overflow | `GRT` | Trip when level exceeds high limit |
| Activate heater when below $T_{\text{low}}$ | `LES` | Cold-start logic |
| Pressure inside a safe window | `LIM` | One instruction replaces two (`GEQ` + `LEQ`) |
| Compare only upper byte of word | `MEQ` with mask `00FF` | Mask out irrelevant bits |

## 3.4 Status Flag Update Logic

For any signed arithmetic instruction, the PLC firmware updates status flags according to:

$$
\text{S:0/0 (V)} = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if the signed result is out of range} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{S:0/1 (Z)} = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if the result is exactly zero} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{S:0/2 (C)} = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if an unsigned carry or borrow occurred} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{S:0/3 (S)} = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if the MSB of the result is 1 (negative in signed interpretation)} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** A common student error is reporting the *wrapped-around* value of an overflow without mentioning the Overflow Flag. Always declare BOTH the numerical result and the flag status for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ladder Logic Schematic — Arithmetic & Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rung1Input[Input Contact I:1/0 - START Pushbutton] -->|TRUE| addBlock[ADD Block]
    addBlock -->|Source A N7:0| addOp{ADD Operation}
    addBlock -->|Source B N7:1| addOp
    addOp -->|Result Dest N7:2| addOut[Destination N7:2]
    addOut --> mulEnable[Enable Next Math]
    mulEnable --> mulBlock[MUL Block]
    mulBlock -->|Source A N7:2| mulOp{MUL Operation}
    mulBlock -->|Source B N7:3 Constant 25| mulOp
    mulOp -->|Result Dest N7:4| mulOut[Destination N7:4]
    mulOut --> grtBlock[GRT Comparison]
    mulOut -->|Source A N7:4| grtOp{Is A Greater Than B?}
    grtBlock -->|Source B N7:5 Constant 5000| grtOp
    grtOp -->|TRUE if N7:4 greater than 5000| outCoil[Output Coil O:2/0 - HIGH FLOW ALARM]
    grtOp -->|FALSE| noAction[Rung Output FALSE - No Alarm]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Arithmetic-Compare-Actuate Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensor[Field Sensor 4 to 20 mA] -->|Analog Input Module| rawCount[Integer Register N7:10]
    rawCount -->|Source A| scpBlock[SCP Scale Instruction]
    scpBlock -->|Source B 0 to 32000| euValue[Engineering Units N7:12]
    euValue -->|Source A| sqrBlock[SQR Square Root]
    sqrBlock -->|Dest N7:14| linearized[Linearized Flow N7:14]
    linearized -->|Source A| grtCheck[GRT High-Limit Check]
    linearized -->|Source A| lesCheck[LES Low-Limit Check]
    grtCheck -->|TRUE| hiAlarm[Output O:2/0 High Alarm]
    lesCheck -->|TRUE| loAlarm[Output O:2/1 Low Alarm]
    linearized -->|Source A| addTotal[ADD Totalizer]
    addTotal -->|Source B 1| totalReg[Cumulative Total N7:20]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{What do you need to do?}
    start -->|Pure math| mathOp{Single or multi-step?}
    mathOp -->|Single step| basicAdd[ADD or SUB or MUL or DIV]
    mathOp -->|Multi-step| chained[Chain in series - scan order matters]
    start -->|Test a condition| cmpOp{How many bounds?}
    cmpOp -->|One bound| singleCmp[EQU or NEQ or GRT or LES or GEQ or LEQ]
    cmpOp -->|Two bounds band| limOp[LIM Limit Test]
    cmpOp -->|Bit-level mask| meqOp[MEQ Masked Equal]
    start -->|Convert data| convertOp{Source format?}
    convertOp -->|BCD input| frdOp[FRD From BCD]
    convertOp -->|Integer to BCD| todOp[TOD To BCD]
```

## 4.4 Status Flag Flow After an `ADD` Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    addInput[ADD executes] --> calcOp[Result computed]
    calcOp --> checkZero{Is Result equals 0?}
    checkZero -->|YES| setZero[Set S Zero flag = 1]
    checkZero -->|NO| clrZero[S Zero flag = 0]
    calcOp --> checkOvf{Is Result greater than 32767 or less than -32768?}
    checkOvf -->|YES| setOvf[Set S Overflow flag = 1 - ROLLOVER]
    checkOvf -->|NO| clrOvf[S Overflow flag = 0]
    calcOp --> checkSign{Is MSB equals 1?}
    checkSign -->|YES| setSign[Set S Sign flag = 1 - negative in signed]
    checkSign -->|NO| clrSign[S Sign flag = 0]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three arithmetic instructions used in PLC programming. For each, state one typical industrial application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Instruction | Operation | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|
| `ADD` | Adds two words | Totalizing flow pulses in a batching system |
| `DIV` | Divides two words | Converting raw ADC counts to engineering units (e.g., 0–32767 → 0–100 °C) |
| `SQR` | Computes square root | Linearizing orifice plate differential pressure to obtain actual flow rate |

*(Any three valid pairs accepted. 1 mark per pair.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `EQU` and `MEQ` comparison instructions.**

**Model Answer:**

- `EQU` performs a **whole-word equality test** between two 16-bit source values. The output is TRUE if and only if every one of the 16 bits matches. It is used in recipe setpoint matching and counter-reset logic.
- `MEQ` performs an equality test **only on the bits selected by a 16-bit mask**. Bits where the mask is `0` are **forced to match (ignored)**. It is used when comparing only a portion of a word — e.g., checking the lower 8 bits of a status word while ignoring the upper 8 bits.

Thus, `MEQ` is a *selective* or *filtered* version of `EQU`, suitable for bit-field comparisons; `EQU` is *unfiltered* and matches the entire word.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition of EQU, 1 for definition of MEQ, 1 for distinguishing application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse the Carry Flag (S:0/2) with the Overflow Flag (S:0/0).** Carry is for *unsigned* wrap-around; Overflow is for *signed* wrap-around. The two diverge for values like 250 + 250.
> - **Always report the destination register value AND the status flag state** when an arithmetic instruction overflows. Many students lose 2–3 marks for omitting the flag.
> - **In a chained `ADD → MUL → GRT` rung, the order of evaluation is strictly top-down.** If you swap order, the input to the comparison changes and the answer is wrong.
> - **Do not use floating-point (F8:) registers in an `ADD` block expecting integer (N7:) sources** — the processor faults.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`**

**(a)** Explain the working of the **`ADD`** and **`SUB`** arithmetic instructions in an Allen-Bradley SLC-500 PLC. With a neat ladder diagram, illustrate how two 16-bit integers stored in `N7:0` and `N7:1` are added and the result stored in `N7:2`. Discuss the status flags that get updated and the conditions under which each is set. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat ladder diagram, explain the operation of the **`GRT`** and **`LIM`** comparison instructions. Design a level-control application in which:
- A tank level (in cm) is stored in `N7:10`.
- A pump (`O:2/0`) must turn ON if the level falls below **30 cm**.
- A high-level alarm (`O:2/1`) must turn ON if the level exceeds **180 cm**.
- The level is "safe" between 30 and 180 cm, and a green indicator (`O:2/2`) must glow during this safe window.

Use `GRT` and `LIM` to implement this. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The `ADD` block has the format:

```
Source A  +  Source B  →  Destination
```

In ladder logic:

```
|     I:1/0          ADD                |
|------| |-----------|   |--------------|
|                  Source A  N7:0       |
|                  Source B  N7:1       |
|                  Dest     N7:2       |
|                                     |
```

When `I:1/0` is TRUE, the instruction adds the contents of `N7:0` and `N7:1` and stores the result in `N7:2`. For example, if `N7:0 = 1500` and `N7:1 = 2300`, then `N7:2 = 3800`.

The `SUB` block has the format:

```
Source A  −  Source B  →  Destination
```

If `N7:0 = 5000` and `N7:1 = 1800`, then `N7:2 = 3200`.

**Status Flag Update Logic** (for signed 16-bit math):

| Flag | Address | Set When |
|---|---|---|
| Overflow | S:0/0 | Signed result < −32,768 or > +32,767 |
| Zero | S:0/1 | Result is exactly 0 |
| Carry | S:0/2 | Unsigned carry/borrow occurred (wrap-around) |
| Sign | S:0/3 | MSB = 1 (negative in signed interpretation) |
