**Example with overflow:** If `N7:0 = 30000` and `N7:1 = 5000`, then mathematically `Result = 35000`, but the 16-bit signed limit is 32767. The instruction stores `N7:2 = 35000 - 65536 = -20536` (wrap-around) **and** sets `S:0/0 = 1`.

*(7 marks — 2 for ladder, 2 for ADD/SUB examples, 2 for status flag table, 1 for overflow example.)*

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

The `GRT` (Greater Than) block returns TRUE when `Source A > Source B`. The `LIM` (Limit Test) block returns TRUE when `Low ≤ Test ≤ High`.

**Ladder Logic Implementation:**

```
Rung 1 — Low-Level Pump Control (using LES):
|     I:1/0              LES              |
|------| |----------------|   |------------|
|                    Source A  N7:10      |
|                    Source B  30         |
|                                    |
|                                    O   |
|                                    |   |
|                                    O:2/0  (Pump)


Rung 2 — High-Level Alarm (using GRT):
|     I:1/0              GRT              |
|------| |----------------|   |------------|
|                    Source A  N7:10      |
|                    Source B  180        |
|                                    |
|                                    O   |
|                                    |   |
|                                    O:2/1  (High Alarm)


Rung 3 — Safe Window Indicator (using LIM):
|     I:1/0              LIM              |
|------| |----------------|   |------------|
|                       Low  30          |
|                     Test  N7:10        |
|                     High  180         |
|                                    |
|                                    O   |
|                                    |   |
|                                    O:2/2  (Green Indicator)
```

**Operating Truth Table:**

| `N7:10` (Level) | Pump `O:2/0` | High Alarm `O:2/1` | Green `O:2/2` |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 30 cm | ON | OFF | OFF |
| 30 ≤ level ≤ 180 | OFF | OFF | ON |
| > 180 cm | OFF | ON | OFF |

*(7 marks — 2 for GRT/LIM explanation, 3 for ladder logic, 2 for truth table.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 3]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Square Root (`SQR`)** and **Scale with Parameters (`SCP`)** instructions. A 4–20 mA differential pressure transmitter is connected to an orifice plate. The raw count for 4 mA is 624, for 20 mA is 3124, and the corresponding DP range is 0 to 100 inches water column. The flow equation is $Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P}$, with $K = 80$. Develop the complete ladder program to compute flow in `N7:40` given the raw count in `N7:10`. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **`MEQ` (Masked Equal)** comparison instruction with a suitable ladder diagram example. How is it different from `EQU`? State one industrial scenario where `MEQ` is preferred. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The `SCP` instruction scales a raw input value using the linear formula:

$$D = \left( \frac{A - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}} \right) \cdot (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}$$

The `SQR` instruction computes the integer square root of a non-negative source.

**Step 1 — `SCP` Block to Convert Raw Count to DP (in tenths of inch w.c.):**

| Parameter | Value | Address |
|---|---|---|
| Input (Raw) | `N7:10` | — |
| Input Min | 624 | — |
| Input Max | 3124 | — |
| Scaled Min | 0 | — |
| Scaled Max | 1000 | (100 in w.c. × 10 for resolution) |
| Output | `N7:20` | DP × 10 |

**Step 2 — `SQR` Block:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Source | `N7:20` |
| Destination | `N7:30` |

**Step 3 — `MUL` Block (Q = K × √DP):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Source A | `N7:30` |
| Source B | 80 (constant for K) |
| Destination | `N7:40` |

**Ladder Program:**

```
Rung 1 — Scaling:
|    I:1/0/EN             SCP                |
|------| |-----------------|   |--------------|
|                   Input        N7:10       |
|                   Input Min    624         |
|                   Input Max    3124        |
|                   Scaled Min   0           |
|                   Scaled Max   1000        |
|                   Output       N7:20       |

Rung 2 — Square Root:
|                    SQR                    |
|-------------------------------------------|
|                   Source      N7:20       |
|                   Dest        N7:30       |

Rung 3 — Multiply by K:
|                    MUL                    |
|-------------------------------------------|
|                   Source A   N7:30        |
|                   Source B   80           |
|                   Dest       N7:40        |
```

**Numerical Verification:** If `N7:10 = 1874` (i.e., 12 mA → mid-range):

$$DP_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{(1874 - 624)}{(3124 - 624)} \times 1000 = \frac{1250}{2500} \times 1000 = 500$$

$$N7:30 = \sqrt{500} = 22$$

$$N7:40 = 22 \times 80 = 1760$$

*(7 marks — 2 for SQR/SCP theory, 3 for ladder, 2 for numerical verification.)*

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

`MEQ` compares two source values **bit-by-bit** but only on the bit positions where the **mask is 1**. Bits where the mask is 0 are excluded from the comparison.

**Ladder Diagram Example:**

```
|     I:1/0              MEQ                |
|------| |----------------|   |------------|
|                    Source A  N7:5         |
|                    Source B  N7:6         |
|                    Mask     0F00h         |
|                                    |
|                                    O   |
|                                    |   |
|                                    O:2/3  (Upper Nibble Match Indicator)
```

**Difference from `EQU`:**

| Aspect | `EQU` | `MEQ` |
|---|---|---|
| Bits compared | All 16 bits | Only bits where mask = 1 |
| Use case | Whole-word matching | Selective bit-field matching |
| Example | Recipe index = 25 (full) | Compare only the upper byte of a 16-bit status word |

**Industrial Scenario:** A PLC is reading a 16-bit **fault word** from a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD). Only the upper 8 bits indicate drive faults; the lower 8 bits indicate warnings. To trip the process on a *fault* but ignore *warnings*, an `MEQ` instruction is used with mask `FF00h`, comparing against a fault code. The warnings in the lower byte do not affect the comparison outcome.

*(7 marks — 2 for MEQ definition, 2 for ladder, 1 for EQU vs MEQ table, 2 for VFD scenario.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Arithmetic** = Math operations on word-level data (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, NEG, ABS, SQR, SCP, MOV, MVM, CLR, TOD, FRD).
- **Comparison** = Logical tests returning TRUE/FALSE (EQU, NEQ, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ, LIM, MEQ).
- **Three operands** for math: `Source A`, `Source B`, `Destination`. **Two sources** for compare: `Source A`, `Source B`.
- **Status Flags (SLC-500):** `S:0/0` Overflow (signed), `S:0/1` Zero, `S:0/2` Carry (unsigned), `S:0/3` Sign.
- **16-bit signed integer range** is **−32,768 to +32,767**; overflow causes *modulo* wrap-around and sets `S:0/0`.
- **`SQR` only accepts non-negative sources**; negative input → processor fault.
- **`SCP` is a 4-step linear interpolation** of the form $D = \frac{(A - I_{\min})(E_{\max} - E_{\min})}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}} + E_{\min}$.
- **`LIM` replaces two comparisons** (GEQ + LEQ) in one rung for window/boundary checks.
- **`MEQ` requires a third operand** — the **mask** — that selects which bits participate in the equality test.
- **Always evaluate status flags after every arithmetic instruction** in exam problems.
- **PLC scan is deterministic and top-down**; the order of rungs in a ladder program matters.
- **Master the orifice flow linearization** $Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P}$ — it is a **favourite KTU question** linking instrumentation, scaling, and SQR.
- **Mask = 0** ⇒ *ignore that bit*; **Mask = 1** ⇒ *include that bit* — never the reverse.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PID and Other Important Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PID and Other Important Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 PID Instruction - Formal Definition

The **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) instruction** in a PLC/DCS is a closed-loop control algorithm that calculates an error value continuously as the difference between a desired **Setpoint (SP)** and a measured **Process Variable (PV)**, and applies a corrective output based on three mathematical terms — **Proportional**, **Integral**, and **Derivative** — to drive the process to the setpoint with minimum error, overshoot, and oscillation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The PID block is treated as a *high-level instruction* (not built from primitive ladder logic). The exam focuses on the PID equation form, tuning parameters, control block structure, auto/manual modes, and gain/reset/rate scheduling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Cruise Control" Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain exactly **80 km/h**:

- **P (Proportional) — The Gas Pedal Pressure:** The harder you press the gas pedal, the more throttle you give. The more the speed deviates from 80, the more you press. This is *proportional* to the error.
- **I (Integral) — The "Long Drive" Memory:** If the wind keeps slowing you down by 2 km/h, a proportional-only controller will let you settle at 78 km/h (a small constant error called *offset*). The integral term "remembers" this lingering error over time and adds extra throttle to push you back to 80.
- **D (Derivative) — The Anticipator:** If you suddenly see a downhill slope, the derivative term notices the *rate of change of error* and preemptively reduces the throttle to prevent overshooting 80.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all three?** No single term is perfect. P alone leaves offset. I alone is sluggish and oscillates. D alone cannot reach the setpoint. **P + I + D** gives fast, accurate, and stable control.

## 1.3 Other Important Instructions — Overview

Apart from PID, Module 2 typically covers instructions that handle **data manipulation, scaling, sequencing, and program flow control**:

| Instruction Family | Purpose | KTU-Relevant Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Math Instructions** | Arithmetic on data | ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, SQR, NEG |
| **Move/Logical** | Data transfer and bit ops | MOV, MVM, AND, OR, XOR, NOT |
| **Comparison** | Decision making | EQU, NEQ, GRT, GEQ, LES, LEQ, LIM, MEQ |
| **Scaling** | Convert raw ADC to engineering units | **SCL (Scale)** and **SCP (Scale with Parameters)** |
| **Jump/Subroutine** | Program flow control | JMP, LBL, JSR, SBR, RET |
| **Sequencer** | Step-based batch control | SQC, SQO, SQL |
| **Shift Register** | Tracking product flow | BSL, BSR |
| **File/Array** | Bulk data ops | FAL, FSC, COP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling (SCL/SCP)** is the most frequently asked instruction alongside PID. It bridges raw 16-bit analog input (e.g., 0–32767) to real engineering units (e.g., 0–200 °C).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PID Equation — Standard Form

The PLC implements a **position-form** PID equation (also called the *ISA* or *non-interacting* form):

$$MV(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

Where:
- $MV(t)$ = Manipulated Variable (Controller Output)
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ = Error signal
- $K_c$ = Controller Gain (dimensionless)
- $T_i$ = Integral Time (Reset Time), in **seconds** or **minutes**
- $T_d$ = Derivative Time (Rate Time), in **seconds** or **minutes**

### 2.2 The Three Terms Decoded

#### (a) Proportional Term — P
$$MV_P = K_c \cdot e(t)$$
- **Why:** Immediate response to current error.
- **Engineering Utility:** Fast reaction, but leaves a steady-state *offset* if used alone.

#### (b) Integral Term — I
$$MV_I = \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$$
- **Why:** Eliminates offset by accumulating past errors.
- **Engineering Utility:** Drives the long-term average error to zero. Causes *oscillation* if $T_i$ is too small.

#### (c) Derivative Term — D
$$MV_D = K_c \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
- **Why:** Anticipates future error by sensing rate of change.
- **Engineering Utility:** Adds **damping**, reducing overshoot. Cannot correct a constant error on its own.

## 2.2 PID Control Block — Typical PLC Structure

A PID block (e.g., Allen-Bradley SLC 500, Siemens FB41) requires:

| Parameter | Description | Typical Data Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Block** | A reserved file (e.g., `PD10`) storing internal states | 23-word structure |
| **Process Variable (PV)** | Real-time measurement from analog input | INT or REAL |
| **Setpoint (SP)** | Desired target value | INT or REAL |
| **Output (CV / MV)** | Control signal sent to actuator | INT or REAL |
| **Gain $K_c$** | Proportional gain | REAL |
| **Reset $T_i$** | Integral time (0 = no integral action) | REAL |
| **Rate $T_d$** | Derivative time (0 = no derivative action) | REAL |
| **Mode** | Auto / Manual / Cascade | Word (bit-mapped) |

## 2.3 PID Operating Modes

- **Manual Mode:** Operator sets the output (CV) directly. PID calculation is bypassed. Used during startup, tuning, or emergency.
- **Auto Mode:** Controller computes CV from SP, PV, and tuning constants.
- **Cascade Mode:** Output of one PID becomes the Setpoint of a second (slave) PID. Used when two loops interact (e.g., temperature + flow).

## 2.4 Scaling Instructions — SCL and SCP

### (a) SCL — Scale
Converts a raw value $S$ from one range to another using a **linear equation**:

$$y = (S - S_{min}) \cdot \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}} + R_{min}$$

### (b) SCP — Scale with Parameters
Same as SCL but uses floating-point math and has **independent min/max** for input and output, more accurate for ADC conversions.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Error signal | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Engineering units | Input to PID |
| Proportional output | $MV_P = K_c \cdot e(t)$ | % of scale | Immediate response |
| Integral output | $MV_I = \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int e\, dt$ | % of scale | Removes offset |
| Derivative output | $MV_D = K_c \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{de}{dt}$ | % of scale | Adds damping |
| Discrete PID (position form) | $MV_n = K_c\left[ e_n + \frac{T_s}{T_i}\sum e_k + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - e_{n-1}) \right]$ | — | PLC implementation |
| Discrete PID (velocity form) | $\Delta MV_n = MV_n - MV_{n-1}$ | — | Bumpless transfer |
| Scaling | $y = R_{min} + (S - I_{min}) \cdot \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}}$ | Engineering units | ADC → Process |
| Loop update time | $T_s$ | seconds | Sample period |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** The **velocity form** is preferred in PLCs because it provides **bumpless transfer** between Auto and Manual modes. Position form requires integrator anti-windup protection.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Temperature control** in a chemical reactor (PLC PID)
- **Level control** in a tank using cascade (level master + flow slave)
- **Pressure control** in a steam boiler (DCS with feedforward)
- **Motor speed control** using VFD with PID feedback
- **HVAC systems** in smart buildings
- **Batch processes** in pharmaceutical industries (PID + Sequencer)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous PID to Discrete (PLC) PID

A PLC is a **discrete** system — it samples the process variable at a fixed interval $T_s$. We must convert the continuous PID equation into a difference equation.

### Step 1 — Discretize the Integral Term

Use the **backward rectangular (Euler)** approximation:

$$\int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k$$

This is the sum of all past errors multiplied by the sample time.

### Step 2 — Discretize the Derivative Term

Use the **first backward difference**:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into Position-Form PID

$$MV_n = K_c\left[ e_n + \frac{1}{T_i} T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + T_d \cdot \frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s} \right]$$

$$\boxed{MV_n = K_c \cdot e_n + \frac{K_c \cdot T_s}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{K_c \cdot T_d}{T_s} (e_n - e_{n-1})}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify Constants

Define discrete constants:
- $K_p = K_c$ (proportional gain)
- $K_i = \frac{K_c \cdot T_s}{T_i}$ (integral coefficient)
- $K_d = \frac{K_c \cdot T_d}{T_s}$ (derivative coefficient)

Final discrete form:
$$MV_n = K_p \cdot e_n + K_i \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + K_d \cdot (e_n - e_{n-1})$$

### Step 5 — Derive the Velocity Form (Bumpless Transfer)

Take $\Delta MV = MV_n - MV_{n-1}$:

$$\Delta MV_n = K_p (e_n - e_{n-1}) + K_i \cdot e_n + K_d (e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})$$

> **Why this matters:** The velocity form only requires the last **3 error samples** to compute the new output. Switching from Manual to Auto does not cause a "bump" because output is computed incrementally.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Scaling a Thermocouple Input

A thermocouple outputs **4 mA to 20 mA**. The PLC's analog input card reads **0 to 32767** counts. The temperature range is **0 °C to 500 °C**. The raw count is **16384**. Find the temperature.

**Given:**
- $I_{min} = 0$, $I_{max} = 32767$
- $R_{min} = 0$, $R_{max} = 500$
- $S = 16384$

**Apply the SCP formula:**

$$T = R_{min} + (S - I_{min}) \cdot \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}}$$

$$T = 0 + (16384 - 0) \cdot \frac{500 - 0}{32767 - 0}$$

$$T = 16384 \cdot \frac{500}{32767}$$

$$T = 16384 \cdot 0.015259$$

$$\boxed{T = 250.0\, ^\circ\text{C}}$$

**Verification:** The raw count 16384 is exactly half of 32767, so temperature should be exactly half of 500 → **250 °C** ✓

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — PID Output Calculation

**Given:**
- Setpoint $SP = 200\, ^\circ\text{C}$
- Process Variable $PV = 180\, ^\circ\text{C}$
- Previous $PV = 175\, ^\circ\text{C}$
- $K_c = 2$, $T_i = 10$ s, $T_d = 2$ s
- Sample time $T_s = 1$ s
- Sum of past errors $\sum e_k = 50$

**Step 1 — Compute current error:**

$$e_n = SP - PV = 200 - 180 = 20\, ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute previous error:**

$$e_{n-1} = 200 - 175 = 25\, ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Compute proportional term:**

$$MV_P = K_c \cdot e_n = 2 \times 20 = 40$$

**Step 4 — Compute integral term:**

$$MV_I = \frac{K_c \cdot T_s}{T_i} \cdot \sum e_k = \frac{2 \times 1}{10} \times 50 = 10$$

**Step 5 — Compute derivative term:**

$$MV_D = \frac{K_c \cdot T_d}{T_s} \cdot (e_n - e_{n-1}) = \frac{2 \times 2}{1} \times (20 - 25) = -20$$

**Step 6 — Total output:**

$$MV_n = MV_P + MV_I + MV_D = 40 + 10 + (-20) = 30$$

$$\boxed{MV_n = 30\% \, \text{(of output scale)}}$$

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Instruction Comparison

| Instruction | Function | Operands | Use Case | Memory Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PID** | Closed-loop control | Control block, PV, SP, CV | Process control | High (23 words) |
| **SCL** | Linear scaling | Source, Input min/max, Output min/max, Dest | Convert ADC to engineering units | Low (4 words) |
| **SCP** | Scaled with parameters | Source, Input min/max, Scaled min/max, Dest | High-precision scaling | Low (4 words) |
| **MOV** | Move word | Source, Dest | Data transfer | 0 |
| **MVM** | Masked move | Source, Mask, Dest | Selective bit transfer | 0 |
| **ADD/SUB** | Arithmetic | Source A, Source B, Dest | Sum/Difference | 0 |
| **EQU/GRT/LES** | Comparison | Source A, Source B | Conditional logic | 0 |
| **LIM** | Limit test | Low limit, Source, High limit | Range checking | 0 |
| **JMP/LBL** | Jump | Label number | Skip logic | 0 |
| **JSR/SBR/RET** | Subroutine | Routine number, parameters | Modular code | Variable |
| **SQC** | Sequencer compare | File, Mask, Source, Control | Step detection | 17 words |
| **SQO** | Sequencer output | File, Mask, Source, Control, Dest | Step output | 16 words |
| **BSL/BSR** | Bit shift left/right | File, Control, Bit address | Conveyor tracking | 1 word |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PID Closed-Loop Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Sum Block e = SP minus PV]
    SUM -->|Error e| PID[PID Controller Kc, Ti, Td]
    PID -->|Output MV| ACT[Actuator Valve Motor Heater]
    ACT --> PROC[Process Reactor Tank]
    PROC -->|Physical Response| SENSOR[Sensor Transmitter]
    SENSOR -->|Analog 4-20mA| ADC[ADC Module]
    ADC -->|Digital Counts| SCL[SCP SCL Scaling Block]
    SCL -->|Engineering Units| PV[Process Variable PV]
    PV --> SUM
    PID -.->|Tune| TUNE[Engineering Operator]
    ACT -.->|Feedback Position| FB[Position Feedback]
    FB -.-> ACT
    TUNE -.->|Auto or Manual| MODE[Mode Selector]
    MODE -.-> PID
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the loop clockwise. The **dashed lines** represent configuration/feedback signals (not part of the forward control path).

## 4.2 PID Algorithm Internal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start of Scan] --> READ[Read PV from Analog Input]
    READ --> CALC_E[Compute e = SP minus PV]
    CALC_E --> P_TERM[Proportional Term Kc times e]
    CALC_E --> I_TERM[Integral Term Kc over Ti times sum of e]
    CALC_E --> D_TERM[Derivative Term Kc times Td times de by dt]
    P_TERM --> SUM2[Sum P plus I plus D]
    I_TERM --> SUM2
    D_TERM --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> LIMIT{Clamp Output to 0 to 100 percent}
    LIMIT -->|Within Range| WRITE[Write CV to Analog Output]
    LIMIT -->|Exceeded| CLAMP[Clamp to Limit and Set Anti Windup Flag]
    CLAMP --> WRITE
    WRITE --> DELAY[Wait for Sample Time Ts]
    DELAY --> START
```

## 4.3 Scaling Block Functional Flow (SCP Instruction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw ADC Input 0 to 32767] --> B[Input Min Imin]
    A --> C[Input Max Imax]
    B --> D{Linear Interpolation}
    C --> D
    D --> E[Output Min Rmin]
    D --> F[Output Max Rmax]
    E --> G[Scaled Engineering Units]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Destination Word]
```

## 4.4 Cascaded PID — Master/Slave Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP_OUTER[Outer Setpoint Temperature] --> PID1[Master PID Temperature Loop]
    PID1 --> SP_INNER[Inner Setpoint Flow]
    SP_INNER --> PID2[Slave PID Flow Loop]
    PID2 --> MV[Manipulated Variable Valve]
    MV --> PROC[Process]
    PROC --> FT[Flow Transmitter]
    FT --> PV2[PV Inner Flow]
    PV2 --> PID2
    PROC --> TT[Temperature Transmitter]
    TT --> PV1[PV Outer Temperature]
    PV1 --> PID1
```

## 4.5 Sequencer (SQO) Step-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Step1
    Step1 --> Step2: Condition Met
    Step2 --> Step3: Condition Met
    Step3 --> Step4: Condition Met
    Step4 --> Step5: Condition Met
    Step5 --> [*]: End of Batch
    note right of Step2: SQO outputs preloaded bit patterns
    note left of Step3: Position pointer increments each scan
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the PID instruction in a PLC. List its three tuning parameters and state the effect of each on the process response.**

**Model Answer:**

The **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) instruction** is a closed-loop control algorithm implemented in the PLC that continuously computes the error between the Setpoint (SP) and Process Variable (PV) and generates a Manipulated Variable (MV) to drive the process to the desired value.

The three tuning parameters are:
1. **Controller Gain ($K_c$):** Determines the proportional response. Higher $K_c$ → faster response, but increases overshoot and oscillation. Lower $K_c$ → slower, more stable response.
2. **Integral Time / Reset ($T_i$):** Determines how fast the integral term eliminates steady-state error. Smaller $T_i$ → faster offset removal but more oscillation. Larger $T_i$ → slower but more stable.
3. **Derivative Time / Rate ($T_d$):** Adds damping based on the rate of change of error. Larger $T_d$ → more damping, less overshoot, but sensitive to noise.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the function of the SCL (Scale) instruction. How is it different from the SCP instruction?**

**Model Answer:**

The **SCL instruction** performs linear scaling of an input value from one numerical range to another. It uses the equation:
$$y = (S - I_{min}) \cdot \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}} + R_{min}$$
This is used to convert raw analog input counts (e.g., 0–32767) to engineering units (e.g., 0–200 °C).

**Difference between SCL and SCP:**

| Feature | SCL | SCP |
|---|---|---|
| Math type | Integer | Floating-point |
| Accuracy | Lower (rounded) | Higher |
| Input/Output ranges | Must overlap | Independent min/max |
| Use | Simple applications | High-precision applications |

SCP uses independent input and output min/max parameters, allowing non-overlapping ranges and higher precision.

---

## Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop PID control system implemented using a PLC. Identify the role of the Setpoint, Process Variable, Control Block, and Manipulated Variable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature control loop has the following parameters at sample $n$:
- $SP = 250\, ^\circ\text{C}$, $PV_n = 235\, ^\circ\text{C}$, $PV_{n-1} = 225\, ^\circ\text{C}$
- $K_c = 1.5$, $T_i = 8$ s, $T_d = 1.5$ s, $T_s = 1$ s
- Sum of past errors = 60

Calculate the discrete PID output (position form) and identify which term dominates. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram:**

Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes. **(2 Marks for diagram)**

**Roles:** **(5 Marks for explanation)**

1. **Setpoint (SP):** The desired value of the process variable (e.g., 250 °C). It is entered by the operator via HMI or preset in the program.
2. **Process Variable (PV):** The actual measured value fed back from the sensor (thermocouple → transmitter → ADC → scaled value).
3. **Error Signal:** $e = SP - PV$ computed in the PID instruction.
4. **Control Block:** A reserved memory file (e.g., `PD10`) that stores internal states, tuning constants, integral sum, and previous error. The PLC updates it every scan/cycle.
5. **PID Calculation:** The PLC computes $MV = K_c e + \frac{K_c}{T_i} \int e\, dt + K_c T_d \frac{de}{dt}$ using stored parameters.
6. **Manipulated Variable (MV) / Control Variable (CV):** The output (0–100% or 4–20 mA) sent to the actuator (valve, heater, VFD) to correct the process.
7. **Actuator + Process:** The physical system being controlled.
8. **Sensor + Transmitter:** Provides the feedback signal completing the loop.

The block diagram must show **SP → Comparator → PID → Actuator → Process → Sensor → Comparator** in a closed loop.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute current and previous errors:** **(1 Mark)**

$$e_n = 250 - 235 = 15\, ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$e_{n-1} = 250 - 225 = 25\, ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute discrete constants:** **(1 Mark)**

$$K_p = K_c = 1.5$$

$$K_i = \frac{K_c \cdot T_s}{T_i} = \frac{1.5 \times 1}{8} = 0.1875$$

$$K_d = \frac{K_c \cdot T_d}{T_s} = \frac{1.5 \times 1.5}{1} = 2.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:** **(3 Marks)**

$$MV_P = K_p \cdot e_n = 1.5 \times 15 = 22.5$$

$$MV_I = K_i \cdot \sum e_k = 0.1875 \times 60 = 11.25$$

$$MV_D = K_d \cdot (e_n - e_{n-1}) = 2.25 \times (15 - 25) = 2.25 \times (-10) = -22.5$$

**Step 4 — Total output:** **(1 Mark)**

$$MV_n = 22.5 + 11.25 + (-22.5) = 11.25$$

$$\boxed{MV_n = 11.25\% \, \text{(of output scale)}}$$

**Step 5 — Dominant term analysis:** **(1 Mark)**

The **Proportional term (22.5)** is the largest in magnitude. The Integral term (11.25) is moderate and is steadily removing offset. The Derivative term (-22.5) is large in magnitude but *negative* — it actively damps the response since the process is changing rapidly toward the setpoint. The **Proportional + Derivative** combination dominates the dynamic response.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the SCL and SCP instructions with their operational differences. A pressure transmitter outputs 4–20 mA for 0–10 bar. The PLC ADC gives 0–32767 counts. Determine the pressure when the ADC reading is 24576. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **three** operating modes of a PID block. How does the **velocity form** of PID provide bumpless transfer between Auto and Manual? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**SCL vs SCP Explanation:** **(3 Marks)**

(Write the table from Question 2 of Part A and explain both instructions with formulas.)

**Numerical Problem:** **(4 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Input range: $I_{min} = 0$, $I_{max} = 32767$ (raw counts)
- Output range: $R_{min} = 0$ bar, $R_{max} = 10$ bar
- Raw count: $S = 24576$

**Apply SCP formula:**

$$P = R_{min} + (S - I_{min}) \cdot \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}}$$

$$P = 0 + (24576 - 0) \cdot \frac{10 - 0}{32767 - 0}$$

$$P = 24576 \cdot \frac{10}{32767}$$

$$P = 24576 \times 0.0003052$$

$$\boxed{P = 7.5\, \text{bar}}$$

**Verification:** $24576 / 32767 = 0.75$ → $0.75 \times 10 = 7.5$ bar ✓

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Three Operating Modes:** **(4 Marks)**

1. **Manual Mode:** The operator directly sets the output (CV/MV) from the HMI. The PID calculation is disabled. The loop is *open* in terms of automatic correction. Used during startup, tuning, sensor failure, or maintenance.
2. **Auto Mode:** The PID instruction is active. It reads PV, computes error, applies the PID equation, and writes the output to CV automatically. The operator can only change SP and tuning parameters.
3. **Cascade Mode:** The output of a *primary* (master) PID becomes the Setpoint of a *secondary* (slave) PID. Used when a slow outer variable (e.g., temperature) controls a fast inner variable (e.g., flow) to improve dynamic response and reject disturbances faster.

**Bumpless Transfer using Velocity Form:** **(3 Marks)**

In the **position form** of PID, switching from Manual to Auto causes a sudden "bump" in the output because the integral term holds the old manual value, and the new PID calculation jumps to a different value.

In the **velocity form**:
$$\Delta MV_n = K_p (e_n - e_{n-1}) + K_i \cdot e_n + K_d (e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})$$

The new output is computed as an **increment** added to the previous output:
$$MV_n = MV_{n-1} + \Delta MV_n$$

Since the loop is always running (even in Manual, $MV_{n-1}$ is being updated), switching to Auto starts from the actual current output, producing a **smooth, bump-free transition**. This is critical in process industries where sudden output changes can damage equipment or cause safety issues.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing $T_i$ and $T_d$ units:** $T_i$ is in the *denominator* of the integral term, not the numerator. Larger $T_i$ = *slower* integral action. Same convention for $T_d$.
> 2. **Forgetting Anti-Windup:** If the actuator saturates (e.g., valve fully open) but error persists, the integral term keeps growing. On desaturation, this causes a huge overshoot. Always clamp the integral sum.
> 3. **Sign of the Error:** Some processes (e.g., cooling) require *reverse acting* control — error sign may be $PV - SP$ instead of $SP - PV$. Always check the process.
> 4. **Sampling Time $T_s$ Mistake:** Students often forget to multiply by $T_s$ in the integral or divide by $T_s$ in the derivative. This is the most common mark-loss area.
> 5. **Scaling Direction:** For SCP, ensure $(R_{max} - R_{min}) / (I_{max} - I_{min})$ is positive for direct-acting sensors; negative for reverse-acting (e.g., differential pressure).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **PID equation (position form):** $MV = K_c\left[ e + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e\,dt + T_d \frac{de}{dt} \right]$
- ✅ **Discrete PID coefficients:** $K_p = K_c$, $K_i = \frac{K_c T_s}{T_i}$, $K_d = \frac{K_c T_d}{T_s}$
- ✅ **Velocity form** is preferred for **bumpless transfer** between Auto and Manual.
- ✅ **Tuning parameters:** $K_c$ (gain), $T_i$ (reset/integral), $T_d$ (rate/derivative).
- ✅ **Three modes of PID:** Manual, Auto, Cascade.
- ✅ **SCL** = integer scaling; **SCP** = floating-point scaling with independent ranges.
- ✅ **Scaling formula:** $y = R_{min} + (S - I_{min}) \cdot \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}}$
- ✅ **Error sign convention:** Most loops use $e = SP - PV$ (direct acting); reverse for cooling.
- ✅ **Anti-windup** is mandatory for integrating processes with saturation.
- ✅ **Sample time $T_s$** must be chosen carefully — too fast adds noise, too slow loses dynamics. Typical: $T_s = T_i / 10$ to $T_i / 5$.
- ✅ **PID block in PLC** uses a reserved control block (e.g., 23-word file in AB SLC 500).
- ✅ **Derivative on PV** (not error) is preferred to avoid "derivative kick" on setpoint changes.
- ✅ **Cascade control** uses two PIDs: master sets SP of slave.
- ✅ **Sequencer (SQO/SQC/SQL)** is used for batch and step-based processes.
- ✅ **Comparison instructions (EQU, GRT, LES, LIM)** are used in PID alarm and limit logic.
- ✅ **JSR/SBR/RET** allow modular code; reduce scan time for large programs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timers and Counters in PLC, Problems, Design, Development and Simulation of PLC Programs on Temperature Control and Valve Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Timers and Counters in PLC, Problems, Design, Development and Simulation of PLC Programs on Temperature Control and Valve Sequencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are PLC Timers and Counters?

A **PLC Timer** is a soft-implemented instruction (a function block) inside the CPU's scan cycle that emulates the behaviour of an electromechanical timing relay. It produces a time-delayed change in its output bit, expressed as a function of an **Enable Input**, a **Preset Value (PV)**, and a **Time Base (TB)**.

A **PLC Counter** is a soft-implemented instruction that counts the number of transitions (rising edges) on its input signal and compares the accumulated count against a **Preset Value (PV)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (IEC 61131-3):** A timer is a function block whose elapsed-time output (`ET`) increases monotonically while the input `IN` is TRUE, and whose boolean output `Q` becomes TRUE when `ET ≥ PT` (for an `TON` block). A counter is a function block whose output `Q` becomes TRUE when the accumulated count `CV` reaches the preset `PV`.

**Mathematical statement for an On-Delay Timer (TON):**

$$
ET(t) = \int_{t_0}^{t} \big[ IN(\tau) \cdot TB \big] \, d\tau, \quad \text{reset to } 0 \text{ when } IN = \text{FALSE}
$$

$$
Q(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } ET \geq PV \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a PLC Timer like a Tea-Steeping Egg (Kitchen Analogy):**
> Imagine you drop an egg into boiling water and set a **3-minute kitchen timer**. The moment the timer is *enabled* (you press start), time starts accumulating — but the bell does **NOT** ring immediately. The bell rings **only after the preset (3 min) has elapsed**. If you lift the egg early (disable the input), the timer *resets to zero*. This is exactly how an **On-Delay Timer (TON)** works: output turns ON **after** the delay period.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a PLC Counter like a Turnstile at a Football Stadium:**
> Each fan passing through increments a counter. After the 50,000th fan (preset), the gate closes (output ON). Even if more fans are still passing (count keeps increasing), the gate stays closed — the bit has already latched. A **Down Counter (CTD)** works in reverse: you start with 50,000 tokens and each fan subtracts one until zero is reached.

### 1.3 Classification of Timers

| Timer Type | Full Name | Output Behaviour |
|------------|-----------|------------------|
| **TON** | On-Delay Timer | Output turns ON **after** delay elapses |
| **TOF** | Off-Delay Timer | Output stays ON **for** delay after input goes OFF |
| **TP** | Pulse Timer | Output is a fixed-width pulse on rising edge |
| **RT** | Retentive Timer | Holds accumulated time across power loss |

### 1.4 Classification of Counters

| Counter Type | Full Name | Direction |
|--------------|-----------|-----------|
| **CTU** | Count-Up Counter | Increments on rising edge |
| **CTD** | Count-Down Counter | Decrements on rising edge |
| **CTUD** | Count-Up/Down Counter | Bidirectional with separate U/D inputs |

### 1.5 Standard Time Bases Available in Commercial PLCs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Time Base resolutions:** **1 ms**, **10 ms**, **100 ms**, **1 s**, **10 s**
>
> Example: Allen-Bradley SLC-500 offers 0.01 s and 1.0 s bases. Siemens S7-1200 offers 1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms resolutions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an On-Delay Timer (TON)

A TON block has **three inputs** and **two outputs**:

- **Inputs:** `IN` (enable), `Preset Value (PV)`, `Time Base (TB)`
- **Outputs:** `Q` (boolean done-bit), `ET` (Elapsed Time — analogue word)
- **Internal:** `ACC` (Accumulator register, 16-bit or 32-bit word)

**Operating logic — step by step:**

1. When `IN = FALSE` (off): accumulator resets to **0**; `Q = 0` immediately.
2. When `IN = TRUE` (on): accumulator starts incrementing in steps of `TB`.
3. Each scan cycle: `ACC ← ACC + TB`.
4. When `ACC ≥ PV`: output `Q` turns ON (TRUE) and latches.
5. `Q` stays ON as long as `IN` remains TRUE.
6. The instant `IN` goes FALSE: `ACC ← 0` and `Q ← 0`.

### 2.2 Anatomy of an Off-Delay Timer (TOF)

1. When `IN` transitions FALSE → TRUE: output `Q` turns ON **immediately**, `ACC` resets to 0.
2. When `IN` transitions TRUE → FALSE: `ACC` begins to count from 0 upward.
3. When `ACC ≥ PV`: output `Q` finally turns OFF.
4. If `IN` goes TRUE again before delay completes: `ACC` resets and `Q` re-energises immediately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common pitfall:** Students confuse TON and TOF. Remember — *delay is applied to the **transition** of the output, not the input.*

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Retentive Timer (RT)

Unlike TON, the Retentive Timer **does NOT reset** its accumulator when `IN` goes FALSE. It only resets when an explicit **Reset (R)** input is energised. Used for totalising run-hours of a motor, regardless of interruptions.

### 2.4 Anatomy of Counters

**CTU (Count-Up Counter):**
- Inputs: `CU` (count pulse), `R` (reset), `PV` (preset).
- On each rising edge of `CU`: `CV ← CV + 1`.
- When `CV ≥ PV`: `Q = 1` (latched).
- `R = 1` forces `CV ← 0` and `Q ← 0`.

**CTD (Count-Down Counter):**
- Inputs: `CD` (count pulse), `LD` (load preset), `PV`.
- On rising edge of `CD`: `CV ← CV − 1`.
- When `CV ≤ 0`: `Q = 1`.
- `LD = 1` forces `CV ← PV`.

**CTUD (Up/Down Counter):**
- Has both `CU` and `CD` pulse inputs.
- Increments on `CU` rising edge; decrements on `CD` rising edge.
- Provides both **QU** (done-up) and **QD** (done-down) outputs.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Variable Description | Units |
|---|----------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{d} = TB \times PV$ | Total delay = Time Base × Preset Value | seconds |
| 2 | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot T_{scan}}$ | Maximum countable pulse frequency (Nyquist for digital IO) | Hz |
| 3 | $CV_{\max} = 2^{n} - 1$ | Maximum count for an n-bit counter | counts |
| 4 | $N_{overflow} = PV - CV_{\max}$ | Overflow beyond preset (wrap-around) | counts |
| 5 | $T_{acc} = \dfrac{PV}{f_{pulse}}$ | Time for CTU to reach preset at pulse frequency $f_{pulse}$ | seconds |
| 6 | $V_{set} = \Delta V \cdot ADC_{res}$ | Setpoint voltage from ADC count | volts |
| 7 | $P_{out} = \begin{cases} 100\%, & T_{meas} < T_{set} - H \\ 0\%, & T_{meas} > T_{set} + H \end{cases}$ | Two-position (ON/OFF) controller output with hysteresis H | % |
| 8 | $u(t) = K_{p} e(t) + K_{i} \int e(t)\, dt + K_{d} \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Standard PID control law | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** Timers and counters form the **backbone of discrete-event sequence control** in industry — bottling plants, traffic lights, batch mixing, conveyor diverters, and even missile launch sequencers rely on these blocks. Temperature control with PLC combines an analogue input (thermocouple via RTD module) with a discrete output via PID or two-position control.

### 2.6 Ladder Logic Representation (Conceptual)

For a typical TON block in ladder logic:

```
       ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
   ───┤  Input   ├──┤  TON         ├──
   IN  │  (I:0/0) │  │  T4:0        │
       └──────────┘  │  PV = 50     ├──( Q )──
                     │  TB = 100ms  │
                     └──────────────┘
```

**Meaning:** When input I:0/0 turns ON, timer T4:0 begins. After 50 × 100 ms = 5000 ms = **5 seconds**, output coil Q energises.

### 2.7 Why Timers and Counters are Critical for Temperature Control

Temperature processes are **slow** (thermal lag in the order of seconds to minutes). A PLC cannot close the loop purely in software-PID at every scan without considering:

- **Sensor warm-up time** (delay before valid readings)
- **Cool-down delay** (heater must OFF, then wait before re-measurement to avoid overshoot)
- **Duty-cycle time-proportioning** (e.g., heater ON for 4 s out of every 10 s)

All of these are implemented using **timers**. The actual switching decision is based on **counter** logic when ON-time pulses are accumulated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Timer Delay Calculation

**Problem:** A TON block is programmed with `PV = 250` and `TB = 100 ms`. Calculate the total delay before the output energises.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$
T_{d} = TB \times PV
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
T_{d} = 100 \text{ ms} \times 250
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
T_{d} = 25000 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to seconds:**

$$
T_{d} = \dfrac{25000}{1000} = 25 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** A 100 ms base × 250 counts = 25 000 ms. Since 1 second = 1000 ms, the result is 25 s. Dimensionally consistent: $[ms] \times [\text{dimensionless count}] = [ms]$ → converted to $[s]$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Preset Value Calculation

**Problem:** A process requires an exact delay of **18 minutes and 30 seconds**. The PLC supports a time base of **0.01 s**. Determine the required `PV`.

**Step 1 — Convert the delay to seconds:**

$$
T_{d} = 18 \times 60 + 30 = 1110 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to milliseconds:**

$$
T_{d} = 1110 \times 1000 = 1{,}110{,}000 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PV:**

$$
PV = \dfrac{T_{d}}{TB} = \dfrac{1{,}110{,}000 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ ms}} = 111{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check:** $111{,}000 \times 10$ ms = $1{,}110{,}000$ ms = $1110$ s ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always quote the **final PV** and the **chosen TB** together. Marks are lost when students give only one of the two.

---

### 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Counter Maximum Count for n-bit Register

**Problem:** A CTU counter is implemented using a **16-bit accumulator word**. Compute the maximum count before overflow.

**Step 1 — Apply the bit-width formula:**

$$
CV_{\max} = 2^{n} - 1
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute n = 16:**

$$
CV_{\max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65536 - 1 = 65535
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** After 65 535 counts, the counter wraps to 0 (or trips an overflow flag depending on PLC model).

---

### 3.4 Worked Problem 4 — Maximum Countable Pulse Frequency

**Problem:** A PLC has a scan time of **15 ms**. A CTU is being used to count pulses from a flow-meter. Calculate the **maximum pulse frequency** that the counter can reliably capture.

**Step 1 — Sampling criterion (Nyquist for digital input):** the pulse must be present for at least **two consecutive scans** to be detected reliably.

**Step 2 — Apply formula:**

$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot T_{scan}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 15 \text{ ms}} = \dfrac{1}{0.030 \text{ s}} \approx 33.33 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering comment:** If pulses exceed 33 Hz, the PLC **will miss counts**. Solutions: use a high-speed counter (HSC) module, or reduce scan time.

---

### 3.5 Worked Problem 5 — Time for CTU to Reach Preset

**Problem:** A conveyor belt generates **2 pulses per second**. The counter is to trigger an alarm after **150 bottles** have passed. Calculate the time taken.

**Step 1 — Apply formula:**

$$
T_{acc} = \dfrac{PV}{f_{pulse}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
T_{acc} = \dfrac{150}{2 \text{ Hz}} = 75 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert:**

$$
T_{acc} = 1 \text{ minute } 15 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Problem 6 — Two-Position Temperature Controller

**Problem:** A furnace must maintain temperature at **$T_{set} = 200°\text{C}$** with hysteresis $H = 5°\text{C}$. Heater turns ON when temperature falls below $195°\text{C}$ and OFF when it rises above $205°\text{C}$. Draw the controller logic and compute the duty cycle.

**Step 1 — Define the ON/OFF bands:**

$$
T_{low} = T_{set} - H = 200 - 5 = 195°\text{C}
$$

$$
T_{high} = T_{set} + H = 200 + 5 = 205°\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the two-position rule:**

$$
P_{out} = \begin{cases} 100\% & T_{meas} < 195°\text{C} \\ 0\% & T_{meas} > 205°\text{C} \\ \text{no change} & 195 \leq T_{meas} \leq 205 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Ladder logic representation (textual):**

```
   T_meas < 195 ─┐
                  ├──( SET Heater_ON )
   Heater_OFF ───┘

   T_meas > 205 ────( RST Heater_ON )
```

**Step 4 — Duty cycle estimation:** If heat-up takes 60 s and cool-down takes 90 s:

$$
D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \dfrac{60}{60 + 90} = 0.40 = 40\%
$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** Hysteresis prevents the relay from **chattering** at the setpoint. This is a classic KTU-style sub-question.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Timer Types

| Feature | TON | TOF | TP | RT |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Output turns ON at | $t = PV$ | $t = 0$ (instant) | $t = 0$ (instant) | $t = PV$ |
| Output turns OFF at | $t = 0$ if IN=FALSE | $t = PV$ | $t = PV$ (fixed pulse) | only via R |
| Resets on IN=FALSE | Yes | No (keeps counting after IN=FALSE) | Yes | No |
| Retentive across power cycle | No | No | No | Yes |
| Typical use | Conveyor start delay | Fan run-down delay | Lamp flasher | Total run-time |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TON Timer — Timing Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IN Input Signal] --> B[TON Function Block]
    B --> C[Accumulator ACC]
    B --> D[Output Coil Q]
    B --> E[Elapsed Time ET]
    F[Preset PV] --> B
    G[Time Base TB] --> B
    C --> H{ACC reaches PV}
    H -- Yes --> I[Q energises TRUE]
    H -- No --> J[Q remains FALSE]
```

### 4.2 CTU Counter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CU Pulse Input] --> B[CTU Counter Block]
    B --> C[Current Value CV]
    B --> D[Done Output Q]
    E[Reset R] --> B
    F[Preset PV] --> B
    C --> G{CV reaches PV}
    G -- Yes --> H[Q energises TRUE latched]
    G -- No --> I[Q remains FALSE]
    E --> J[Force CV to zero]
```

### 4.3 Temperature Control Loop — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RTD or Thermocouple Sensor] --> B[Analogue Input Module]
    B --> C[ADC Conversion to Engineering Units]
    C --> D[PLC Scan Cycle CPU]
    D --> E{Two Position or PID Compare}
    E -- Below Setpoint --> F[Heater Energised ON]
    E -- Above Setpoint --> G[Heater De energised OFF]
    F --> H[Process Furnace]
    G --> H
    H --> A
    D --> I[HMI Display of T measured and T setpoint]
```

### 4.4 Valve Sequencing Logic — State Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[State 0 Idle] --> S1[State 1 Open Valve A]
    S1 -- Timer T1 done --> S2[State 2 Open Valve B]
    S2 -- Timer T2 done --> S3[State 3 Open Valve C]
    S3 -- Timer T3 done --> S4[State 4 Hold for Process]
    S4 -- Process OK signal --> S5[State 5 Close all Valves in Reverse]
    S5 --> S0
```

### 4.5 Timer-Counter Combination — Traffic Light Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Pushbutton PB] --> B[TON Timer Red 30s]
    B --> C[TON Timer Green 25s]
    C --> D[TON Timer Yellow 5s]
    D --> E[CTU Counter cycle count]
    E --> F{Count equals 10}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[TON Timer Night Blink Mode]
    G --> H[Reset and Return to A]
```

### 4.6 PLC Temperature Control — Signal Flow with PID

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Variable T measured] --> B[Error e equals T set minus T measured]
    B --> C[PID Function Block]
    C --> D[Controller Output u 0 to 100 percent]
    D --> E[PWM Generation via TON TON block]
    E --> F[Digital Output to Solid State Relay]
    F --> G[Heater Element]
    G --> H[Process Furnace]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note:** The PWM-style duty-cycle generation in sub-block E uses **two TON timers** and a flip-flop to convert the analogue PID output into a time-proportioned discrete signal. This is the standard industrial technique when only digital outputs are available for heating elements.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEICT635]**
*With the help of a neat timing diagram, explain the operation of an On-Delay Timer (TON). Mention its typical industrial applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A TON timer output `Q` energises **only after** the input `IN` has been continuously TRUE for a duration equal to the preset time `PV`. The accumulator `ACC` starts from zero when `IN = TRUE` and increments in steps of the time base `TB`. When `ACC ≥ PV`, output `Q = TRUE`. The instant `IN` becomes FALSE, the accumulator resets and `Q = FALSE`.

**Timing diagram (textual):**

| Time (s) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 | 14 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|----|----|----|
| IN | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| ACC | 0 | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 0 |
| Q | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

(Assuming PV = 8 s, TB = 1 s)

**Applications:** *(1 mark)* conveyor start delay, motor star-delta switching, sequential batching, traffic lights, machine warm-up sequencing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEICT635]**
*Differentiate between a Count-Up (CTU) and Count-Down (CTD) counter. Give one industrial example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CTU | CTD |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Direction | Increments on each pulse | Decrements on each pulse |
| Initial value | 0 (or LD-loaded) | PV (loaded via LD) |
| Done-bit | `Q = 1` when `CV ≥ PV` | `Q = 1` when `CV ≤ 0` |
| Reset input | `R = 1` resets to 0 | `LD = 1` reloads PV |

**Examples:** *(1 mark)*
- **CTU** — counting bottles on a filling line to count finished goods.
- **CTD** — counting remaining parts in a magazine to signal refill-needed.

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEICT635 — Modified]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain with a neat diagram the internal architecture and operating principle of a **Retentive On-Delay Timer (RTO)**. Compare it with a non-retentive TON in terms of reset behaviour.

**(b) (7 marks)** A bottling plant uses a TON timer with `TB = 100 ms` and `PV = 150` to delay the start of the filling nozzle after a bottle is detected. Calculate the delay time. If the requirement is changed to **22 seconds**, determine the new preset value. Also state one engineering measure if pulses from a flow meter exceed the maximum countable frequency.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The Retentive Timer (RT/RTO) operates exactly like a TON, **except** that the accumulator is **not reset** when `IN` becomes FALSE. Instead, it continues to retain the accumulated value. The accumulator is cleared **only** when an explicit `Reset (R)` input is energised.

**Internal architecture (textual):**

```
       ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
   IN ─┤                                  │
       │   ACC ← ACC + TB  (each scan)    ├── ACC (held in non-volatile RAM)
   R ──┤   IF R=1  THEN ACC ← 0           │
       │                                  │
       └──────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
                  ACC ≥ PV ? ──Yes──► Q = 1
                       │
                      No
                       │
                       ▼
                    Q = 0
```

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Property | TON | RTO |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Reset on IN = FALSE | Yes | No |
| Requires explicit R | No | Yes |
| Used for | One-shot delays | Totalised run-time, total hours |
| Power-loss retentive | No | Yes (in modern PLCs) |

**Key application:** Measuring total running hours of a motor across start-stop cycles.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Part (i) — Delay calculation:**

Given: `TB = 100 ms`, `PV = 150`.

$$
T_{d} = TB \times PV = 100 \text{ ms} \times 150 = 15000 \text{ ms} = 15 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Part (ii) — New preset for 22 seconds:**

$$
PV_{new} = \dfrac{T_{d}}{TB} = \dfrac{22000 \text{ ms}}{100 \text{ ms}} = 220
$$

**Part (iii) — Engineering measure for over-speed pulses:**

Apply the Nyquist criterion:

$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot T_{scan}}
$$

If incoming pulses exceed this, **install a High-Speed Counter (HSC) module** that counts independently of the CPU scan, OR use a frequency divider (divide-by-N counter) before the PLC input.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEICT635 — Modified]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Design a **PLC ladder program** (textual representation acceptable) for a **valve sequencing application** where three solenoid valves (V1, V2, V3) must open in sequence with a **5-second delay** between each, hold for **10 seconds** after V3 opens, and then close in **reverse sequence with 3-second intervals**. Include emergency stop logic.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the design of a **PLC-based two-position temperature controller** for a furnace set to maintain **$T_{set} = 350°\text{C}$ ± 10°C**. Show the comparator logic and the role of hysteresis.

---

**Model Solution (a) — Valve Sequencing:**

**Step 1 — Define timer presets:**

| Timer | Purpose | Preset (s) |
|-------|---------|-----------|
| T1 | Delay before V1 opens | 0 (start) |
| T2 | Delay between V1 and V2 | 5 |
| T3 | Delay between V2 and V3 | 5 |
| T4 | Hold time after V3 opens | 10 |
| T5 | Delay between V3 close and V2 close | 3 |
| T6 | Delay between V2 close and V1 close | 3 |

**Step 2 — Sequence flags (internal bits):**

- `S1`, `S2`, `S3` for sequence stages.
- `S_HOLD` for hold phase.
- `S_RESET` for closing phase.

**Step 3 — Ladder logic (textual representation):**

```
   Start_PB ──┬── TON T2 PV=5s ── (Flag S1)
              │
   S1 ─────────┤── TON T3 PV=5s ── (Flag S2)
              │
   S2 ─────────┤── TON T4 PV=10s ─(Flag S_HOLD)
              │
   S_HOLD ────┤── Open V1, Open V2, Open V3
              │
   T4 done ───┤── Close V3
              │
   Close V3 done ── TON T5 PV=3s ── Close V2
              │
   Close V2 done ── TON T6 PV=3s ── Close V1
              │
   Close V1 done ── Reset all flags → Idle

   E_STOP ─────┴── (RST V1, RST V2, RST V3, RST all flags)
```

**Step 4 — Sequencing verification (state diagram):**

```
IDLE ──Start──► S1 ──5s──► S2 ──5s──► HOLD (10s) ──► Close V3 ──3s──► Close V2 ──3s──► Close V1 ──► IDLE
```

**Step 5 — Emergency stop:** any actuation of `E_STOP` resets all valve coils and all sequence flags in a single rung using `(RST)` instructions.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Two-Position Temperature Controller:**

**Step 1 — Identify the bands:**

$$
T_{low} = T_{set} - H = 350 - 10 = 340°\text{C}
$$

$$
T_{high} = T_{set} + H = 350 + 10 = 360°\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Comparator logic:**

```
   T_meas < 340 ──┬── (SET Heater_ON)
                  │
   Heater_ON ─────┘

   T_meas > 360 ────── (RST Heater_ON)
```

**Step 3 — Role of hysteresis:**

Without hysteresis, the relay would oscillate (chatter) at the exact setpoint due to sensor noise — causing wear. With **H = ±10°C**, the controller has a **dead-band of 20°C** within which no action is taken. This:

- Prevents relay wear.
- Accommodates sensor noise of ±1 to 2°C.
- Allows the heater time to physically respond.

**Step 4 — Engineering extension:** For tighter control (±1°C), replace two-position control with **PID** implemented in a dedicated PID function block (FB 41 in Siemens, PIDE in Allen-Bradley).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TON and TOF** — remember the delay applies to the **transition** of `Q`, not `IN`.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units** — always write `PV = 220` along with `TB = 100 ms` together.
> 3. **Counter overflow not addressed** — for a 16-bit CTU, max count is 65 535; beyond that, behaviour is platform-specific.
> 4. **No mention of scan-time impact** — for high-frequency counting, scan time limits the pulse detection. Always quote the Nyquist formula.
> 5. **Ladder diagrams drawn but not labelled** — every rung must have a rung number and brief comment.
> 6. **PID without anti-windup** — integrator term saturates if not clamped, causing huge overshoot.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Timers & Counters + Temperature/Valve Programs:**

- ✅ Timer types: **TON, TOF, TP, RT** — know the operating truth table for each.
- ✅ Counter types: **CTU, CTD, CTUD** — know the meaning of done-bits `Q`, `QU`, `QD`.
- ✅ Core formula: $T_{d} = TB \times PV$. Always state TB and PV together.
- ✅ Maximum count for n-bit counter: $2^{n} - 1$.
- ✅ Maximum countable frequency: $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot T_{scan}}$.
- ✅ Retentive timers survive power-down; non-retentive do not.
- ✅ Two-position (ON/OFF) temperature control requires **hysteresis** to prevent relay chattering.
- ✅ Hysteresis bands: $T_{set} \pm H$.
- ✅ Valve sequencing uses **multiple TON timers** chained with **internal flags** for state tracking.
- ✅ Emergency stop always uses **direct-reset (RST)** instructions overriding all outputs.
- ✅ PID controller law: $u(t) = K_{p} e(t) + K_{i} \int e(t)\, dt + K_{d} \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$.
- ✅ High-Speed Counters (HSC) are needed when pulse frequency exceeds PLC scan-limited $f_{\max}$.
- ✅ Always include **anti-windup** in any practical PID implementation.
- ✅ Ladder logic uses **rungs** with **inputs on left**, **outputs on right** — power flow is left to right.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conveyor Belt Control and Control of a Process, PLC Installation
Trouble Shooting and Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT635 - PLC AND DCS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Trouble Shooting and Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Troubleshooting** in the context of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC) and Distributed Control Systems (DCS) is defined as the systematic, logical, and methodical process of identifying, isolating, diagnosing, and rectifying faults or abnormal conditions within the automation hardware, firmware, software, and communication networks to restore the system to its specified operational state.

**Maintenance** refers to the combined set of technical, administrative, and managerial actions undertaken to retain a PLC/DCS system in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its intended functions. Maintenance is broadly classified into:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Classification of Maintenance:**
> 1. **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** – Scheduled, routine, planned interventions performed to prevent failure.
> 2. **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** – Reactive repairs carried out after a fault has occurred.
> 3. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** – Condition-monitoring driven interventions using diagnostics.
> 4. **Breakdown Maintenance (BDM)** – Emergency restoration after catastrophic failure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital ICU" Intuition

Imagine a PLC/DCS as the **central nervous system of an industrial plant**, much like the human body managed inside a hospital ICU:

- The **CPU / Controller** = the **Brain** (decision-making center).
- The **I/O Modules** = the **Senses and Limbs** (sensors gather data, actuators execute commands).
- The **Communication Network (Fieldbus/Ethernet)** = the **Nervous System** (carries signals).
- The **HMI / Operator Station** = the **Eyes and Ears of the Doctor** (visualization).
- The **Power Supply** = the **Heart** (supplies vital energy).

**Troubleshooting** is exactly like a doctor diagnosing a patient: first observe symptoms (alarms, LED codes), isolate the affected organ (CPU vs. I/O vs. Network), run tests (multimeter, software diagnostics), and then prescribe treatment (replace, repair, reprogram). **Maintenance**, on the other hand, is the regular health check-up — annual blood tests (calibration), routine exercise (firmware updates), and lifestyle changes (cleaning, tightening) — to ensure the patient (plant) never falls critically ill.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

In KTU and industry practice, the following standardized metrics quantify the effectiveness of troubleshooting and maintenance:

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reliability Metrics (bolded for emphasis):**
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** = Total operational uptime / Number of failures. Industry benchmark for PLCs ≈ **100,000 to 500,000 hours**.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** = Total downtime / Number of repairs. KTU target value for industrial PLCs ≤ **30 minutes** for I/O faults, ≤ **2 hours** for CPU faults.
> - **Availability (A)** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR). Typical target for DCS systems = **99.95%** (the "Four Nines" standard).

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

From a Board Examiner's perspective, this module directly tests the **Course Outcomes CO3 and CO5** of PEICT635:

- **CO3** (Apply): Students must apply troubleshooting flowcharts to given fault scenarios.
- **CO5** (Maintain): Students must design maintenance schedules and interpret diagnostic LEDs.

Questions frequently appear in the form of: *"Given a specific fault symptom (e.g., 'RUN LED OFF, all outputs de-energized'), identify the fault and state the corrective action."* — These require both **Recall (Bloom Level 1)** and **Application (Bloom Level 3)** cognitive skills.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Systematic Troubleshooting Methodology

Effective troubleshooting of PLC/DCS systems follows a rigorous, hierarchical procedure. The KTU Board expects students to recall and apply this sequence:

### Step 1: Symptom Recognition
- Observe **alarm messages** on HMI/SCADA screens.
- Read **LED indicators** on PLC racks and DCS cards.
- Listen for abnormal sounds (buzzing relays, fan noise).
- Note any **operator complaints** or production losses.

### Step 2: Information Gathering
- Check the **system logs** and event historians in DCS.
- Review the **last modification** made to the program or configuration.
- Verify **environmental conditions** (temperature, humidity, dust).
- Inspect **recent maintenance records**.

### Step 3: Hypothesis Formation
- Formulate possible causes ranked by **probability × criticality**.
- Apply the **"80/20 Pareto Rule"**: 80% of faults typically arise from 20% of recurring causes (loose wiring, blown fuses, EMC noise).

### Step 4: Fault Isolation
- Use the **"Half-Split Method"**: Divide the system into halves; test each half; proceed to the quarter containing the fault, and so on.
- Use **"Substitution Method"**: Replace suspect modules with known-good spares.

### Step 5: Root Cause Correction
- Replace faulty component, repair wiring, reprogram logic.
- Update documentation after repair.

### Step 6: Verification & Restoration
- Test in **simulation mode** first, then **dry run**, then **full production**.

## 2.2 PLC Troubleshooting — Hardware Level

| Subsystem | Symptom | Likely Fault | Diagnostic Action |
|-----------|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Power Supply (PSU)** | PWR LED OFF, all outputs OFF | Mains failure, blown fuse, PSU failure | Check input voltage with multimeter; measure **24 V DC** across PSU terminals |
| **CPU Module** | RUN LED OFF, ERR LED ON | Watchdog timeout, memory corruption | Cycle power; reload program; check battery |
| **Input Module** | Input LED OFF despite field signal | Blown input fuse, opto-coupler failure, wiring break | Measure voltage at terminal; check sensor supply |
| **Output Module** | Output LED ON but load not operating | Blown output transistor/relay, open coil | Measure load voltage; check contactor coil resistance |
| **Communication Module** | COM LED blinking red, timeout errors | Cable break, wrong baud rate, EMI, terminator missing | Check cable continuity; verify protocol configuration |

## 2.3 PLC Troubleshooting — Software Level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Software Faults (favourite KTU question area):**
> 1. **Scan Time Overrun** — Program too long or infinite loop. Fix: use cyclic interrupts, avoid tight FOR-NEXT loops.
> 2. **Addressing Errors** — Overlap of bit/word memory (e.g., MW10 and MW12 conflict). Fix: use memory partitioning discipline.
> 3. **Watchdog Timer Expiry** — Hardware watchdog trips if scan time exceeds limit (typical **500 ms** to **2 s**).
> 4. **Retentive Memory Loss** — Battery dead. KTU fact: lithium battery life ≈ **3–5 years**.
> 5. **Program Inconsistency** — Offline/Online mismatch after hot-edit.

## 2.4 DCS Troubleshooting — Architecture Level

A DCS is hierarchically structured as:

1. **Level 0 — Field Instruments** (sensors, valves, transmitters).
2. **Level 1 — Direct Digital Control (DDC) Controllers** (e.g., ABB AC 800M, Honeywell C300).
3. **Level 2 — Supervisory Stations** (Engineering Station, Operator Station, Historian).
4. **Level 3 — Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES)**.

Faults at each level have distinct signatures. A **"Yellow-banding"** on a controller faceplate indicates a **"BUMPLESS TRANSFER"** failure — the backup controller could not take over seamlessly from the primary.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Reliability Mathematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all standard formulas a KTU student must memorize for ESE questions on this module.

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Standard Value / Unit |
|-------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Expressed as **% (0 to 1)** |
| Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Decimal fraction |
| Failure Rate | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | **Failures per hour** |
| Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{repair,i}}{n}$ | Hours |
| System MTBF (Series) | $MTBF_{sys} = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \right]^{-1}$ | Hours |
| System MTBF (Parallel) | $\lambda_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Failures/hour |
| Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{comm}$ | **Milliseconds** |
| Watchdog Limit | $T_{WD} \geq 1.5 \times T_{scan(max)}$ | Seconds |

> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE:** All `|x|` notations in formulas above are written as `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

The reliability formulas above are the **backbone of Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** design as per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**. Plant safety auditors compute **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** using MTBF and MTTR figures. Thus, mastering these formulas is not merely academic — they directly govern whether a plant is legally permitted to operate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Series-System MTBF Formula

The MTBF of a **series reliability block diagram** is fundamental to understanding why adding more components to a control system reduces overall reliability.

### Step 1: Define Series Reliability

In a series system, **all components must function** for the system to function. The overall system reliability $R_s(t)$ is the product of individual reliabilities.

$$
R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Exponential Reliability Model

For electronic components (PLCs, DCS cards), the reliability follows the exponential distribution, where $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$. Substituting:

$$
R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \right) t}
$$

### Step 3: Equate to a Single Exponential

The combined system therefore behaves as a single exponential with effective failure rate:

$$
\lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i
$$

### Step 4: Convert to MTBF

Since $MTBF = 1/\lambda$, we obtain the working formula:

$$
MTBF_{sys} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{sys}} = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \right]^{-1}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Illustration

Consider a PLC rack with the following failure rates:
- Power Supply: $\lambda_1 = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- CPU: $\lambda_2 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- 8 Input Cards (each): $\lambda_3 = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- 4 Output Cards (each): $\lambda_4 = 0.8 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour

Total failure rate:

$$
\lambda_{sys} = 5 + 2 + 8(1) + 4(0.8) = 18.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

System MTBF:

$$
MTBF_{sys} = \frac{1}{18.2 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 54{,}945 \text{ hours}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
MTBF_{sys} = \frac{54{,}945}{24 \times 365} \approx 6.27 \text{ years}
$$

This explains why **redundant, parallel architectures** are used for critical DCS controllers — the failure rate of two components in parallel is the product, not the sum.

## 3.2 Derivation: Parallel-System Failure Rate

For two identical controllers operating in parallel (1-out-of-2 redundancy), both must fail for the system to fail.

$$
R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{2}\left(1 - R_i(t)\right) = 1 - \left(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right)^2
$$

For small $\lambda t$:

$$
R_{parallel}(t) \approx 1 - (\lambda t)^2 = e^{-(\lambda t)^2}
$$

The effective parallel failure rate therefore scales as $\lambda^2$, dramatically reducing system-level failure probability.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Availability Calculation

**Problem:** A DCS controller has $MTBF = 80{,}000$ hours and $MTTR = 4$ hours. Compute the **availability**.

**Solution:**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{80{,}000}{80{,}000 + 4} = \frac{80{,}000}{80{,}004}
$$

$$
A \approx 0.99995 = 99.995\%
$$

This satisfies the **"Four Nines"** availability target required for continuous-process plants (refineries, power stations).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Fault-to-Action Mapping (Examiner's Favourite)

| Rank | Symptom | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|------|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | All outputs de-energized, PWR LED OFF | Mains supply failure, tripped MCB | Reset MCB; verify mains; inspect PSU fuse |
| 2 | Single input not registering | Broken sensor cable, sensor failure | Test sensor with multimeter; replace cable |
| 3 | ERR LED steady, RUN LED OFF | CPU in STOP, programming error | Connect programmer, read fault code, correct logic |
| 4 | HMI shows "Communication Timeout" | Loose Ethernet cable, IP conflict | Reseat connectors; verify IP subnet mask |
| 5 | Outputs chattering (rapid ON/OFF) | Electrical noise, missing surge suppressor | Install RC snubbers, check grounding |
| 6 | Battery Low alarm on HMI | Lithium battery aged | Replace battery within scheduled window |
| 7 | Controller in "Yellow band" (DCS) | Backup failed to synchronize | Manual force switchover; check redundancy cable |
| 8 | Historian stops recording | Disk full, service crashed | Archive old data, restart historian service |

## 3.5 Maintenance Interval Derivation

For a critical component with $MTBF = 100{,}000$ hours, the **maximum safe PM interval** $T_{PM}$ is typically set using the **"Half-Life Rule"**:

$$
T_{PM} \leq \frac{MTBF}{2} = 50{,}000 \text{ hours} \approx 5.7 \text{ years}
$$

In practice, KTU-aligned industries adopt shorter intervals (6 to 12 months) accounting for environmental derating factors (heat, dust, vibration) that effectively reduce MTBF.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Systematic PLC Troubleshooting Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Fault Reported] --> B{Symptom Identified?}
    B -- No --> C[Read HMI Alarms and LEDs]
    C --> B
    B -- Yes --> D{Power Indicators ON?}
    D -- No --> E[Check Mains, MCB, PSU Fuse]
    E --> F{PSU Output 24V DC?}
    F -- No --> G[Replace PSU Module]
    F -- Yes --> H[Trace Wiring for Shorts]
    D -- Yes --> I{CPU RUN LED ON?}
    I -- No --> J[Check CPU Diagnostics via Programmer]
    J --> K{Programming Error?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Correct Logic and Reload]
    K -- No --> M[Replace CPU Module]
    I -- Yes --> N{Inputs Functioning?}
    N -- No --> O[Check Field Sensors and Wiring]
    O --> P{Input Module LED ON?}
    P -- No --> Q[Replace Input Module]
    P -- Yes --> R[Sensor/Field Fault]
    N -- Yes --> S{Outputs Functioning?}
    S -- No --> T[Check Output LED Status]
    T -- U{Output LED ON?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Load or Wiring Fault]
    U -- No --> W[Replace Output Module]
    S -- Yes --> X{Communication Healthy?}
    X -- No --> Y[Check Cables, IP, Protocol]
    Y --> Z[Replace Comm Module if Needed]
    X -- Yes --> AA[System Restored - Document Fault]
    AA --> AB[END]
    G --> AB
    H --> AB
    L --> AB
    M --> AB
    Q --> AB
    R --> AB
    V --> AB
    W --> AB
    Z --> AB
```

## 4.2 DCS Functional Architecture with Maintenance Touchpoints

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L0["LEVEL 0: FIELD LAYER"]
        F1[Pressure Transmitter]
        F2[Temperature Sensor]
        F3[Control Valve]
        F4[Motor Starter]
    end
    subgraph L1["LEVEL 1: CONTROL LAYER"]
        C1[Controller Primary]
        C2[Controller Backup]
        I1[I/O Cards AI AO DI DO]
        N1[Fieldbus Network Profibus Foundation]
    end
    subgraph L2["LEVEL 2: SUPERVISORY LAYER"]
        O1[Operator Station HMI]
        E1[Engineering Station]
        H1[Historian Server]
        A1[Alarm Management Server]
    end
    subgraph L3["LEVEL 3: MANAGEMENT LAYER"]
        M1[MES Production Tracking]
        M2[ERP Integration]
    end
    F1 --> N1
    F2 --> N1
    F3 --> N1
    F4 --> N1
    N1 --> I1
    I1 --> C1
    I1 --> C2
    C1 <--> C2
    C1 --> N1
    C2 --> N1
    N1 --> O1
    N1 --> E1
    N1 --> H1
    N1 --> A1
    O1 --> M1
    E1 --> M1
    H1 --> M2
    A1 --> M1
```

## 4.3 Maintenance Strategy Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Asset Criticality] --> B{Category?}
    B -- High --> C[Predictive Maintenance]
    B -- Medium --> D[Preventive Maintenance]
    B -- Low --> E[Run to Breakdown]
    C --> F[Vibration Analysis Thermography Oil Analysis]
    D --> G[Scheduled Inspections Lubrication Calibration]
    E --> H[Corrective Maintenance on Failure]
    F --> I[Reduced Downtime]
    G --> J[Planned Downtime]
    H --> K[Unplanned Downtime]
    I --> L[OPTIMAL]
    J --> M[ACCEPTABLE]
    K --> N[COSTLY]
```

## 4.4 Diagnostic LED Interpretation Reference (Generic PLC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[Power LED] --> P1{Green Steady?}
    P1 -- Yes --> POK[Healthy Power]
    P1 -- No --> PFAIL[PSU Fault: Replace PSU]
    RUN[Run LED] --> R1{Green Steady?}
    R1 -- Yes --> ROK[CPU in RUN Mode]
    R1 -- No --> RFAIL[CPU in STOP or Error]
    RFAIL --> R2{ERR LED Red?}
    R2 -- Yes --> R3[Read Fault Code via Programmer]
    R2 -- No --> R4[CPU in Program Mode]
    COM[Communication LED] --> C1{Green Flashing?}
    C1 -- Yes --> COK[Data Exchange OK]
    C1 -- No --> C2{Red Steady?}
    C2 -- Yes --> C3[Link Down: Check Cable]
    C2 -- No --> C4[Check Baud Rate and IP]
    IOL[Input Output LEDs] --> IO1{Per-Channel LED}
    IO1 --> IO2[ON means Field Signal Present]
    IO1 --> IO3[OFF means No Field Signal]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Mermaid Compilation:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `RFAIL`, `C3`) and all node labels contain only raw uppercase, lowercase, and numeric characters — no markdown formatting tags inside the labels — to ensure the diagrams compile cleanly in the KTU e-learning portal.
